P169342.project_abstract: The objective of the Rural Water, Sanitation, and Hygiene for Human Capital Development Project is to: (a) improve access to ‘safely-managed’ water supply and sanitation in selected areas of rural Bangladesh; and (b) strengthen sector institutional capacity for water and sanitation. There are six components to the project, the first component being Investments in water supply. The second component is the Investments in sanitation and hygiene. The third component is the institutional strengthening. The fourth component is the project implementation and management. This component includes two subcomponents: subcomponent 4.1 will support the DPHE’s capacity for project implementation and monitoring through, among others, procurement, fiduciary, and environmental and social (E&S) safeguards management; development and implementation of the stakeholder engagement plan. Subcomponent 4.2 will support the PKSF’s capacity for the project implementation and monitoring through, among others, procurement, fiduciary, and E&S safeguards management; development and implementation of the stakeholder engagement plan. The fifth component is the COVID-19 emergency response. This component will provide quick, just-in-time WASH services where needed in the COVID-19 pandemic hotspots, to cope with the fast-changing COVID-19 situation, among others, through (i) setting up handwashing stations/devices (fixed and portable) with running water, proper drainage, and sanitation facilities; (ii) providing quality water access or delivery points, compact water treatment plants, and water kiosks; and (iii) providing emergency operational supplies, such as for personal protective equipment, and for water supply and sanitation service providers to ensure disaster preparedness and continuity of service delivery. Finally, the sixth component is the Contingent emergency response component (CERC). This component will provide immediate response to an eligible crisis or emergency as needed.

P171422.project_abstract: The proposed Second Karachi Water and Sewerage Services Improvement Project (KWSSIP 2) will invest US$600 million of which 40 percent to be financed by IBRD. These investments will scale-up infrastructure rehabilitation and expansion, complemented by capacity building to increase operational and financial performance and improvements to the enabling environment. The project builds on the achievements of the ongoing Bank-funded Karachi Water and Sewerage Services Improvement Project (KWSSIP) that was approved in June 2019. The Project will benefit around 16 million people in Karachi.The project activities of KWSSIP 2 will be grouped into four components.  Component 1: Capacity Building and Reform of KWSC, focuses on transforming KWSC into an efficient utility through technical assistance, HR reforms, NRW reduction, water safety plans, and establishment of a water quality testing tab, strengthen KWSC’s corporate governance, and increase its operational and financial performance other initiatives. These activities will contribute to strengthen KWSC’s corporate governance and increase its improving KWSC's operational and financial performance. Component 2: Safely Managed Water and Sanitation Services, focuses on providing safely managed water and sanitation services. This component will finance activities that will directly address Karachi's water supply and sanitation challenges by investing in infrastructures, including addressing water shortages, reduce network leaks, improve service quality, including in low-income areas. Component 3: Project Management and Studies, will finance the project's management costs, including coordination, supervision, risk management, and preparation for future water and wastewater infrastructure projects. Component 4: Contingent Emergency Response Component (CERC), has no funds allocated but it will allow us to reallocate uncommitted loan resources to support emergency response and reconstruction if needed.
P171422.regionname: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan, and Pakistan

P176429.project_abstract: The project (Phase I of the Bangladesh Water Security and WSS MPA) will support the Government of Bangladesh in achieving the SDG Goal 6 and advance the utility reform agenda ensuring long-term sustainability of Water Supply and Sanitation (WSS) service provision in Chattogram. The MPA will comprise four phases spanning over nine years (2024-2033) to support 15 cities with an estimated 20 million beneficiaries. The program framework includes three mutually reinforcing categories of interventions: (i) strengthen institutions and policies for planning, service delivery and regulations, (ii) improve infrastructure and service delivery systems, and (iii) enhance financial sustainability and access to finance for capital and operational expenditures. Phase 1 of the MPA will lay the foundation of implementing this framework by implementing important activities across all three categories in Chattogram and introducing the performance orientation in Chattogram Water Supply and Sewerage Authority's (CWASA) operations through Performance Based Conditions (PBCs) for accessing IDA resources. The project builds on the achievements of the previous Bank-funded Chittagong Water Supply Improvement and Sanitation Project (CWSISP) that closed on December 31, 2020 by financing investment in infrastructure and by supporting institutional strengthening. The project will include: (a) expansion of the water distribution system, following the District Metered Area (DMA) concept to enable efficient, reliable and equitable water supply to all customers within the utility’s service area; (b) expansion of water supply and non-network sanitation services in Low-income Communities (LIC); and (c) support to strengthen the Chattogram Water Supply and Sewerage Authority's (CWASA) utility systems, financial performance and creditworthiness including tariff roadmap, and building CWASA’s capacity for planning, delivering, and managing WSS services. The proposed project provides emphasis on improving service delivery and institutional sustainability while scaling up access.

P176619.project_abstract: The development objective of the Water Sector Efficiency Project for Jordan is to improve the efficiency of water services in Jordan. The project comprises of five components. The first component, sustainable non-revenue water reduction will focus on improving the efficiency of water supply services, through non-revenue water (NRW) reduction (commercial and technical) to improve water availability and revenues within the project areas. It consists of following sub-components: (i) improved service delivery and NRW reduction; (ii) modernized systems for sustaining NRW reduction; and (iii) community engagement to improve financial sustainability, collections efficiency, and demand control. The second component, increased energy efficiency (EE) and reduced energy supply costs will improve efficiency of the water sector by reducing energy used, costs and greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions by improving EE of existing water systems through pump and water equipment rehabilitation and replacement and installation of renewable energy (RE) systems. It consists of following sub-component: (i) improving energy efficiency in the existing water systems. The third component, water security measures to underpin efficiency improvements will support mechanisms to enable efficient and fair management of water resources, providing tools to adapt to the impacts of climate change. It consists of following sub-components: (i) strengthening drought management and water allocation systems; and (ii) safeguarding existing water storage. The fourth component, project management and implementation support will focus on project management required to implement this project and to strengthen systems for the planned series of projects (SOP). It consists of following sub-components: (i) project management and implementation support; and (ii) performance-based conditions (PBC) verification and audit support. The fifth component, contingency emergency response (CERC) with zero allocation will be created and made implementation-ready to allow the Government of Jordan (GOJ) to respond quickly in case of an eligible emergency.
P176619.regionname: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan, and Pakistan

P177146.project_abstract: The development objective of Urban Resilience Emergency Project for Burundi is to respond to the flood emergency, increase flood resilience, and improve climate-resilient urban management in Bujumbura. This project has four components. 1) The first component, Emergency Flood Response and Climate-Resilient Infrastructure, has the following sub-components: (i) Emergency Response Interventions; (ii) Climate-Resilient Infrastructure; and (iii) Strategic studies for urban and flood risk management investments and resilient urban services. 2) The second component, Capacity Building and Institutional Strengthening for Urban Resilience, has the following sub-components: (i) Strengthening capacity for improved information, flood early warning services and emergency preparedness; (ii) Urban Management Capacity Building; and (iii) Strengthening the Resilience and Performance of Urban Water Services. 3) The third component, Project management, will finance incremental project management costs for the Project Implementation Unit (PIU). 4) The fourth component, Contingency Emergency Response, will provide an immediate response to an eligible crisis or emergency, as needed, by financing the implementation of emergency infrastructure rehabilitation and reconstruction.

P178389.project_abstract: The Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), the largest country in Sub-Saharan Africa and its third most populous, is rich in natural resources but remains one of the world’s poorest countries. With an estimated population of 110 million - over half living in rural areas - and a low population density, poverty is widespread, with 73 percent of households living on less than US$ 1.90 per day. Access to water supply and sanitation (WSS) services is extremely limited: 53 percent of the population lacks basic water access and 84 percent lacks basic sanitation access. Disparities are stark, particularly between urban and rural areas. Despite abundant water resources, the sector is constrained by weak infrastructure, fragmented governance, limited institutional capacity, and insufficient investment, especially in rural and peri-urban areas. The institutional framework remains complex, with overlapping mandates across multiple ministries and agencies, and key sector reforms introduced by the 2015 Water Law - such as the regulatory authority (ARSPE) and the national water resources agency (OCE) are not yet fully operational. Sanitation remains particularly fragmented, with an incomplete legislative framework and unclear responsibilities across the service chain. To address these challenges, the Government of DRC (GoDRC) has developed a national WASH program and a WASH Compact with ambitious targets and financing needs of US$ 20 billion. In support of this agenda, the Water Supply and Sanitation Access Program (PASEA) adopts a long-term Multi-Phase Approach (MPA) over 11 years, with a projected IDA financing envelope of US$1.2 billion. The program aims to expand access to sustainable WSS services while strengthening institutional capacity and enabling private sector participation. Phase 1 (US$ 350 million) focuses on the provinces of Kasai, Kasai Central, Kasai Oriental, and Kwilu, targeting the most underserved areas based on access gaps, poverty, and development priorities. It aims to provide basic water services to 2 million people and sanitation services to 1.2 million people, using an area-based approach at decentralized territorial levels. Subsequent phases will progressively scale up geographically, with increasing provincial responsibility and potential co-financing. PASEA is aligned with the World Bank’s Country Partnership Framework (2022–2026), contributing to improved access to infrastructure, stronger governance, and private sector development. By prioritizing fragile and conflict-affected regions, the program also seeks to strengthen the social contract and support inclusive economic development. Overall, the project combines infrastructure investments with institutional strengthening and private sector development to sustainably expand WASH services and prepare for scale-up in subsequent phases.Component 1: Increasing access to and capacities for drinking water supply service delivery (IDA: US$ 255.8 million). This component supports sector reforms, strengthens governance and institutional capacity at national and provincial levels, and expands water supply infrastructure in rural and peri-urban areas. It also promotes private and not-for-profit operator development and builds a skilled workforce for solar-powered systems. It includes the rehabilitation of the Kananga water treatment plant, transferred from the Access, Governance and Reform for the Electricity and Water Sectors Project (AGREE Project). Subcomponent 1.1: Expanding access to water supply (IDA: US$ 232.4 million) finances the construction, rehabilitation, and expansion of water systems (boreholes, networks, storage, tap stands, connections, metering), including solar-powered systems with backup. It also covers studies, supervision, safeguards, and support to ONHR and not-for-profit operators (ASUREPs). Subcomponent 1.2: Strengthening private and not-for-profit operators (IDA: US$ 6.6 million) supports PPP structuring, service delegation contracts, tariff analysis, operator capacity building, and result-based financing to expand services. Subcomponent 1.3: Strengthening public sector capacity (IDA: US$ 16.8 million) supports policy development, institutional strengthening, and operationalization of provincial utilities. It includes technical assistance, equipment, performance systems, communication campaigns, and training programs, particularly for solar water systems.Component 2: Increasing access to and capacities for sanitation service delivery (IDA: US$ 52.2 million). This component strengthens the sanitation policy and institutional framework while scaling up access to basic sanitation and improving WASH in schools and health centers. It also promotes behavior change, private sector development, and workforce skills. Subcomponent 2.1: Expanding sanitation and hygiene access (IDA: US$ 25.7 million) supports implementation of the open defecation eradication roadmap using Community-Led Total Sanitation (CLTS) approaches, combined with material support in rural areas and sanitation marketing and e-vouchers in peri-urban areas. It also finances fecal sludge treatment infrastructure and related studies. Subcomponent 2.2: Improving WASH in institutions (IDA: US$ 18.5 million) finances construction and rehabilitation of WASH facilities in schools (195) and health centers (105), strengthens O&M systems, and supports hygiene promotion, including menstrual hygiene management. Subcomponent 2.3: Strengthening the private sector for sanitation (IDA: US$ 3.1 million) supports sanitation enterprises through capacity building, market development, and catalytic grants for products and services. Subcomponent 2.4: Strengthening public sector capacity (IDA: US$ 4.9 million) supports development of the legal and regulatory framework, urban sanitation planning, institutional strengthening, Management Information System development, and training programs, with a focus on scaling up future investments.Component 3: Program Management, Learning and Scale-up (IDA: US$ 42 million). Sub-component 3.1: Project management and learning (IDA: US$ 36.0 million) finances overall implementation, coordination, and monitoring of MPA-Phase 1. It supports the operation of the Project Coordination Unit (PCU), establishment and functioning of Provincial Project Implementation Units (PPIU) in Kasai, Kasai Central, Kasai Oriental, and Kwilu, and capacity building of national and provincial entities. It also covers M&E systems (including GIS and remote monitoring), grievance redress and GBV-related measures, learning and knowledge exchanges, and a Young Professionals program to strengthen fiduciary, technical, environmental, and social capacities. In addition, it finances independent verification (IVA), audits, and land acquisition and resettlement costs. Sub-component 3.2: Preparing scale-up for MPA-Phase 2 (IDA: US$ 6.0 million) supports preparation of the next phase in additional provinces (including Lomami and Kongo Central, with potential expansion). It finances preparatory studies (baseline, feasibility, technical, environmental, and social), project design, and initial operational costs for future PPIUs, including staffing and equipment.Component 4: Contingency Emergency Response Component (US$ 0 allocated). A Contingent Emergency Response Component (CERC) with zero allocation may be used to contribute to an emergency response through the timely implementation of activities in response to an eligible national emergency. A CERC is included in the project in accordance with Investment Project Financing (IPF) Policy, paragraphs 12 and 13, for Situations of Urgent Need of Assistance and Capacity Constraints.

P178687.project_abstract: The Green, Resilient, and Inclusive Development (GRID) programmatic Development Policy Credit (DPC) series is a catalyst for Nepal’s ongoing transition to a GRID path. The series of three operations is a key instrument for Nepal to operationalize its homegrown GRID Platform across the economy. This ambition is articulated in the 2021 Kathmandu Declaration on GRID, 2023 Joint Communiqué on GRID, Nepal’s 16th Five-Year Development Plan, and Nepal’s GRID Strategic Action Plan 2024-2034 (SAP) which is now being finalized after a series of provincial consultations. The GRID SAP includes over US10 billion dollars of ongoing and pipeline investments for ten key transitions. The World Bank and 15 other Development Partners (DPs) back this effort, including the International Monetary Fund (IMF), International Finance Corporation (IFC), Asian Development Bank (ADB), the United Nations, and other multilateral, bilateral, and nongovernmental organizations that coordinate on policy, knowledge, and investment via Nepal’s GRID Platform. Nepal’s political situation remains fluid, yet commitment to GRID and this DPC series has been maintained by four successive coalition governments that have taken office since the November 2022 federal and provincial elections. The current coalition took over on July 14, 2024, led by the two largest parties in Parliament. This change has impacted the provincial government set up. At the sub-national level, funds, functions, and staff are managed by the seven provinces and 753 local governments for which legislation, institutions, and administrative procedures have been formalized as constitutionally prescribed. The federalization process is supported by this DPC series.

P178954.project_abstract: The development objective of the First Water and Sanitation Project for Malawi is to increase access to improved water supply and sanitation services in Blantyre Metropolitan Area, and to enhance the operational and financial efficiency of the Blantyre Water Board (BWB). The project comprises of five components. The first component, water supply improvements will be implemented by the BWB and will finance investments in water production (motivated by the increase in flood frequency and breakdowns at the existing water treatment plant), network stabilization, leak reduction, renewable energy sources, and water network expansion to unserved areas, thereby lowering greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and boosting water access. The second component, priority sanitation investments will finance interventions to increase access to improved sanitation and reduce environmental pollution that impacts public health, thus contributing to the global crisis response framework (GCRF) pillar 3 on climate resilience. The third component, institutional capacity strengthening will finance a set of institutional development activities. The fourth component, (TA) technical assistance and project management support will finance TA to support the project implementation unit (PIU) and the incremental operating costs for project management, including environment and social risks, communications, and project monitoring and evaluation. The fifth component, contingency emergency response (CERC) will provide preparedness and rapid response measures to address disaster, emergency and or catastrophic events in accordance with the applicable CERC manual.

P179017.project_abstract: Guinea is a water rich country, endowed with 1,165 rivers spread over 23 watersheds, 14 of which are shared with its neighbouring countries, including the Niger, Senegal and Gambia rivers (respectively the first, second and fourth largest rivers in West Africa). Guinea is a member of the associated regional river basin authorities, including NBA (9 countries), OMVS (4 countries) and OMVG (3 countries).  These activities are still at a nascent stage and need substantial support to scale them up. There is also a need to improve Guinea’s participation in transboundary platforms, to enable the country to benefit fully from its position as an upstream riparian and mobilize its surface water resources for multiple uses. There are also significant groundwater resources but they have not been well managed, with some gaps in knowledge about the state of the resource, and weak institutional capacity to monitor and exploit groundwater in a sustainable manner. While some groundwater modeling studies have been undertaken, there is little understanding of the proliferation of boreholes in major urban areas and the potential impact on water resources.Guinea has a population of 13.1 million and is experiencing a strong demographic growth of more than 2.84%.  Although two thirds of the population of Guinea is still rural , the country is undergoing a strong urbanisation process. Only 64% of the national population has access to improved water supply and 30% to safely managed sanitation. Water services are provided by the national water utility, SEG, but it has been undermined by weak financial and operational capacity for several years. In the face of rapid population growth (3.5% a year in Greater Conakry), there is currently a water production deficit of 180,000 m3/day, equivalent to 120% of current production. While there is existing water production infrastructure such as dams, reservoirs and boreholes, these assets suffer from poor operational functionality and are in need of repair. The distribution network is also dilapidated with an estimated 60% of non—revenue water (physical and commercial losses) in SEG’s network. A Water Masterplan for Greater Conakry was developed in 2019 and a similar study is currently underway for secondary towns and cities. With an estimated 1.345 billion € needed for water infrastructure until 2040 for Greater Conakry alone, the investment needs are significant and mobilizing funding and financing is an urgent task.Sanitation has been a long-neglected part of the country’s overall development. Sanitation, covering sewerage, onsite, rainwater evacuation and solid waste, is managed under the Ministry of Housing, Urbanism and Territorial Development (MINHUT) with local communes responsible for services, but formal network services are available to only a small part of the capital city, with the rest of the country making do with rudimentary sanitation facilities and informal services such as for pit emptying. Several development partners  are already starting to invest in wastewater infrastructure to start addressing the gap in services but more needs to be done to improve sanitation service delivery. As the Guinea national sanitation strategy (2022) noted, the institutional arrangements are inadequate given the growing population, significant backlog in investments and the poor quality of services – with knock-on impacts on human health and the environment. A Master Plan has been developed and several recommendations submitted to the government for addressing this serious crisis. To address the most pressing water and sanitation challenges, the Guinea Water and Sanitation Project (PEAG) will focus on investments to increase water production, water supply and water services to boost access to water for unconnected people in Greater Conakry. At the same time, these investments will enhance the reliability and quality of water services for people who are already connected to SEG's water services but only receive water intermittentl. A major non-revenue water reduction component will address leakages and dysfunction in the current distribution network, which will also improve SEG's operational efficiency and enhance services for current and future customers. To ensure the sustainability of investments, the project will support institutional reforms, particularly as regards the urban water utility, SEG, and the sanitation sector whose institutional and regulatory arrangements require massive reinforcement. In parallel, an the Guinea Fiscal Sustainability and Efficiency Reforms Development Policy Financing (P501272) is currently being prepared and includes prior actions related to the revision of the water policy based on tariff methodology that aims at operational cost recovery for SEG over time. to the Government of Guinea will inIn addition, the project will support the GoG to implement tariff reforms, updates to sector regulations and policies, and other measures needed to strengthen the capacity of sector institutions to achieve universal access to water and sanitation services. The project will be implemented using the IPF with PBC instrument to ensure adequate incentives for achieving investments and reforms.

P179643.project_abstract: The Project aims to increase access to water supply, sanitation, and electricity services and improve performance in the water supply and electricity sectors in Gabon. The Project finances: (i) the construction and rehabilitation of water supply and electricity infrastructure in urban and rural areas; (ii) technical assistance to sector institutions to improve their capacities to plan, develop, manage, monitor and regulate water, sanitation, and electricity services; (iii) the procurement and installation of meters, regularization of connections, extension of access to both services, and improved management systems to reduce losses and improve cost-recovery and service continuity, particularly in Greater Libreville where 36 percent of the Gabonese live; (iv) the construction of a fecal sludge treatment plant in Greater Libreville; and (v) studies for critical short- and medium-term investments to improve electricity service delivery in Gabon.

P179684.project_abstract: The proposed Sustainable Power and Irrigation in Nigeria (SPIN) project will address nexus of water-food-energy challenges in Nigeria through investments in irrigation, dam safety, and hydropower planning. By mobilizing water for productive purposes, optimizing the use of existing storage facilities, and enhancing hydropower planning capacity, the project will make integrated management of water, energy and food more effective and resilient in Nigeria.  It will do this by investing in the management and resilience of multipurpose water infrastructure as well as strengthening the policies, institutions, capacities, and inter-governmental cooperation required. The proposed project includes four main components:1) Institutional strengthening and capacity building for water resources management; 2) Irrigation Modernization; 3) Improvements in Dam Operations and enhancing dam safety; and 4) Project Management.

P179770.project_abstract: The Proposed Operation will provide financing to rehabilitate the Maradi-Zinder section along the National Road 1 (RN1) in Niger, as well as associated secondary and rural roads, periodic maintenance of the RN1, and enable transport and logistics services. These interventions are part of a comprehensive national program to improve connectivity and to unlock the development potential of areas around southern Niger's RN1 basin while improving climate resilience and maximizing benefits for local and rural communities, especially  youth, women, refugees and host communities.The project intends to support the stabilization of the region by fostering economic opportunities through support to value chains with growth potential and sub-regional trade. As such, the project will support the design of a value chains support program through rural logistics while promoting multimodality and regional trade facilitation through the development of technical studies and environmental and social instruments. The project is expected to improve food security, climate resilience, and access to basic services around the RN1 basin, thereby enhancing living conditions for host and refugee communities in the Maradi-Zinder region and building in flexibility to finance urgent and critical needs as they arise during implementation. The project will be structured around three components: (i) Component 1: Climate-resilient improvement of Niger’s transport backbone; (ii) Component 2: Enabling environment for southern Niger basin logistics; and (iii) Component 3: institutional strengthening and project management. The Proposed Operation will also include a Contingent emergency response component (CERC).

P179781.project_abstract: The AF is SDC-RETF Grant of US$5.33 million, to be added to the Parent Project which has US$27.40 million IDA Credit approved on June 9, 2020, with the closing date on November 30, 2025.  The AF will enable bridging the financing gap under Components 1 and 2 to cover the envisaged soft-type activities, as well as to incorporate  additional activities under Subcomponent 1.3, in response to client’s demand, and establish a more sound foundation for future investments, including FLOWS2.  The AF will finance all formerly-approved soft/technical assistance (TA)-type activities and few goods/equipment contracts under Component 1, Foundational measures for water security.   In particular, the AF funds  will be used to finance investments in water supply infrastructure and services, including investments in rehabilitation and upgrade of critical water supply system (Subcomponent 2.2) in addition to improving the operation and safety of its existing large‐scale water infrastructure assets, through dam safety programs and small rehabilitation works (Subcomponent 1.3).   The PDO, the results framework and Project closing date will remain unchanged. Safeguards management remains as the Parent Project.  The Project has prepared Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) instruments which govern the aforementioned scale-up activities worth US$3.5 million, including the floating PV demonstrations. For these activities, after the PMT and design consultants develop the site-specific designs/Bills of Quantities (BoQs), the PMT will prepare Site-specific ESIA/ESMP, or possibly a Checklist-type ESMP in case of small works that do not have an E&S footprint.

P180017.project_abstract: The development of objective of the Second Fiscal Sustainability, Inclusive, and Sustainable Growth Development Policy Financing Program for Cameroon is to: (i) improve fiscal sustainability, governance, and public sector management; (ii) improve inclusion by ensuring equitable access to services and empowering women and girls; and (iii) lay foundations for sustainable infrastructure development. Cameroon’s Vision 2035 aims to achieve upper-middle-income status by 2035, focusing on poverty reduction, industrialization, and consolidating democracy and national unity. The National Development Strategy 2020-2030 (SND30) adopted in November 2020 supports this vision by promoting economic growth, improving social services, strengthening climate resilience, and enhancing governance. SND30’s four pillars: (1) structural economic transformation; (2) human capital development; (3) job creation and economic inclusion; and (4) governance, decentralization, and strategic state management. Pillars 2 and 3 emphasize social inclusion, while Pillar 4 seeks to address various drivers of conflict through: (i) advancing decentralization and local development; (ii) strengthening rule of law and the security of people and property reduction; (iii) enhancing public services; (iv) strengthening economic and financial governance by rigorously fighting corruption, embezzlement, and conflicts of interest; and (v) improving regional planning.

P180321.project_abstract: Cameroon has, in general, abundant water resources but they are geographically unevenly distributed and the poor implementation of IWRM is hampering their sustainability. In addition, the delivery of water and sanitation services is not satisfactory both in terms of delivery rate and quality of service, in urban areas and even more in rural areas. The three Northern regions selected for the project (Far North, North and Adamawa) represent 29% of national population  and the majority of the poorest (56%).  They are also most affected by climate change and are the most exposed to Fragility, Conflict and Violence (FCV). Water scarcity, even during the rainy season, and pollution are negatively affecting these regions socio economic development. Climate change brings droughts and floods which exacerbate the situation, adding conflicts and displacements. Despite recent institutional progress in the latest years (2019 decentralization law, 2022 NWP, ongoing update of the 1998 water law), there are still shortcomings to build a sustainable/resilient water sector. This is why the Government requested financial support from the World Bank to support ongoing institutional reforms; strengthen capacity of MINEE, CAMWATER, and local institutions; help operationalize IWRM in selected areas; and provide improved access to WSS services in peri-urban and rural areas. The Government and the Bank agreed to focus the Project on the Far North, North and Adamawa regions. These regions cover the portion of the Benue River basin located in Cameroon, the rural and peri-urban areas where access to water and sanitation is most critical and areas where internal and cross-borders migration and FCV situations take place. Focusing the proposed Project activities on both IWRM and WSS on these three regions. In addition the project will deliver 20 000 new drinking water connection in Yaounde and Douala to provide drinking water to 225 000 new beneficiaries.  The project also aims at achieving greater and noticeable impacts, which could then be replicated and /or scaled up in other areas through future supports. Considering the foregoing, Project activities will capitalize on and promote synergy with existing WB-funded VIVA Benue and VIVA Logone projects.

P180429.project_abstract: The proposed project aims to improve access to markets, climate change resilience of family farmers, and access to safe water in selected rural areas. The project will benefit mostly small-scale family farmers, indigenous people, and traditional communities.

P180533.project_abstract: The proposed Mexico Emission Reductions Project (Mexico’s ERP) is led by the Government of Mexico (GoM) with the goal of generating verified ISFL emission reductions and distribute emission reductions payments in accordance with an agreed Benefit Sharing Plan. Mexico’s ERP will be implemented across four northern states Chihuahua, Coahuila, Durango, and Nuevo Leon. The selection of these states is based on their mitigation potential in the forestry and agricultural sectors, as well as their political commitment to integrated landscape management. For the GoM, the project represents an opportunity to scale up various interventions at the forest-landscape level to better address the main drivers of deforestation and degradation. The Project recognizes that deforestation and forest degradation are complex and multifactorial phenomena, especially considering that in Mexico forests are found in multiple land use landscapes and deforestation is not only determined by direct productive activities but also by underlying causes involving multiple stakeholders. Thus, the project seeks to: (i) strengthen intersectoral collaboration to better align investments in forest landscapes, (ii) reduce greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from the land sector and improve forest livelihoods through the promotion of activities to conserve and sustainably use and restore forest landscapes, (iii) maintain and enhance Mexico’s capacity to monitor and report GHG emissions. By achieving the Project objectives, the GoM will enhance its capacity to access new sources of climate finance. Mexico’s Emissions Reduction Program will adopt a programmatic approach beginning with interventions in the LULUCF sector and aiming to transitioning a potential second phase to emissions reductions from the AFOLU sector. This ESRS focuses on the first phase. A potential second phase which would be prepared separately will require a stand alone E&S assessment depending on program activities under this second phase. The Program measures/activities that will generate the Emission Reductions will be financed by CONAFOR and implemented through its public incentive programs. The project is designed in three components: Component 1: Landscape investments. Subcomponent 1.a.: Underlying activities that will generate ERs (activities financed and implemented by CONAFOR) and Subcomponent 1.b: Strengthen value chains in sustainable forest landscapes; Component 2 (the Program). Payment for Emission Reductions distributed in accordance with the agreed BSP: Subcomponent 2.a.: Payment for ERC, and Subcomponent 2.b.: Distribution of Emission Reductions payments as per an agreed BSP; and Component 3: Project and Program Operation and Coordination. Activities through this component will better target Program measures, maintain program operation, enhance GHG inventory data, improve interagency collaboration, and mainstream gender.

P181029.project_abstract: The objective of the Sustainable Financing for Rogun Hydropower Project for Tajikistan is to increase the supply of clean, affordable, and climate-resilient hydroelectricity for consumers in Tajikistan and the Central Asia region. There are four components to the project, the first component being construction activities. Activities include: (i) design and supply of replacement runners for generating units 5 and 6 and design, supply, and installation of electro-mechanical equipment for generating units 3 and 4 with a combined installed capacity of approximately 1,260 MW, including turbines, generators, frequency governor, excitation system, electrical systems, unit-related monitoring system, cooling water system, compressed air system for the governor, fire detection and suppression systems (lot 1); and (ii) installation of replacement runners for generating units 5 and 6 and other turbine rehabilitation works and control system integration (lot 1A). The design of electromechanical equipment includes design specifications that account for projected increases in precipitation due to climate change, thereby minimizing the risk of damage or dam failure. The second component is the project implementation support. This component includes project management consultants, employer costs, and implementation support. The third component is implementing a resettlement action plan (rap) and livelihood restoration plan. Finally, the fourth component is the hydro-meteorological activities. This component includes hydromet investments, TA for improved water use, and monitoring water use.

P181046.project_abstract: The project aims to support the GoA in conducting pre-feasibility studies of more than 400 existing dams. The overall goal is to reveal and address the climate risk to water management and agriculture and dilapidated water infrastructure with a focus on increasing the water storage capacity of reservoirs for adaptation to a changing climate. Specifically, the project intends to prepare grounds towards increasing the safety and water availability of dams to irrigate an area of 180,000 ha of agricultural land mostlylocated in the western–coastal part of Albania from Shkodra to Vlora, enabling 200,000 farms to increase yields and farmincome.The rehabilitation, modernization of dams, and improvements of dam safety will need to include structural and non-structural measures, hydrological assessment, structural strengthening of dams and improvement of the water storage in the reservoirs, sediment management, modernization of control and monitoring, and other measures that would improve the safety and operation of the dams. Increasing the dam’s safety would provide socio-economic and environmental benefits in Albania.

P181064.project_abstract: This proposed project will support the government of Belize to improve its policy and technical capacity of managing sustainable development of its coastal areas and blue economy, finance climate-resilient green and grey infrastructure that are critical to the livelihood of its population and the sustainability of its blue economy, and provide immediate and effective response to an eligible crisis or emergency. It will support the following four components:Component 1: Policy Development and Capacity Building. This component aims to improve Belize's policy framework and technical capacity for the sustainable development of Belize’s coastal areas and blue economy. It will support four subcomponents: (a) policy development, (b) capacity building, (c) carbon market readiness, and (d) blue economy investment program. Component 2: Water, Sanitation and Waste Management in Targeted Urban Areas. This component will support (a) the upgrade of Belize City Wastewater Treatment Plant; (b) the expansion of the water network in South San Pedro; and (c) the development of a waste management pilot in San Pedro. Component 3: Project Management. This component will support project management, including monitoring and evaluation (M&E). It will finance project implementation costs associated with the project implementation units (PIU) in key agencies involved in project execution.Component 4: Contingent Emergency Response Component. This component will allow for rapid reallocations of uncommitted project funds in the event of an eligible emergency as defined in OP 8.00 to finance eligible expenses associated with the response.

P181263.project_abstract: The development objective of First Fiscal Policy and Sustainable Growth Development Policy Financing Deferred Drawdown Option Project for Peru is to support the Government of Peru (GoP) in: (i) strengthening climate change adaptation; and (ii) promoting decarbonized development in selected sectors. The policy program supports Peru’s economic development through CC adaptation and mitigation reforms that are aligned with the goals established in Peru’s PEDN 2050, NDC 2020, and National Climate Change Adaptation Plan. The operation is aligned with the CPF for Peru (FY2023-FY2027) and complements various ongoing projects and Technical Assistance (TA) engagements. The program also supports WBG Corporate Priorities, by promoting gender equality and mobilizing private capital. The DPF program complements activities by the International Finance Corporation (IFC) and the Multilateral Investment Guarantee Agency (MIGA) to enhance the ecosystem for affordable and green housing provision.

P181344.project_abstract: The development objective of Disaster Resilience Development Policy Credit with Catastrophe Deferred Drawdown Option Project for Nepal is to strengthen the capacity of the government of Nepal to manage disaster and climate risks, including disease outbreaks. This operation is aligned with the goals of the Paris Agreement. First, the operation is fully aligned with the policy recommendations that have emerged from the Nepal’s Country Climate and Development Report (CCDR) and is consistent with the climate priorities outlined in its Second Nationally Determined Contributions (NDC), National Adaptation Plan (NAP), and Long-term Strategy (LTS). Second, on mitigation goals, the reforms under this operation do not significantly increase greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and do not introduce or reinforce persistent barriers to the country’s transition to low-GHG emissions. Third, on adaptation and resilience goals, risks from climate hazards are not likely to have an adverse effect on the prior actions’ contribution to the PDO. Prior Action 1 (PA1) aids the NAP and NDC goals to enhance disaster response, while PA2 focuses on updating building designs for climate resilience, in line with NDC and NAP. PA3 targets the NDC goal of expanding multi-hazard early warning systems. PA4 supports the NAP's capacity building initiatives for climate-informed policies and water infrastructure design. PA5 is in sync with the Disaster Risk Reduction (DRR) National Strategic Plan of Action, NAP, and NDC objectives to improve climate-sensitive disease surveillance by 2025. Therefore, all prior actions of the program are aligned with the adaptation and resilience goals of the Paris Agreement.

P181389.project_abstract: This program document proposes the first operation for US800 million dollars of a programmatic series of two development policy loans (DPLs) to support energy transition and climate resilience reforms in the Philippines. The Program Development Objective (PDO) of the operation is to support Government of the Philippines (GOP) reforms to (1) scale up the adoption of clean energy technologies, (2) increase the security, flexibility, and competition of the electricity markets; and (3) improve water management across water uses; represented in three pillars. Pillar 1 aims to strengthen the policies and regulations for scaling up renewable energy (RE), energy efficiency (EE) and adoption of electric vehicles (EVs). Pillar 2 will help establish electricity market mechanisms for accelerating grid integration of variable renewable energy (VRE) and improving competition in wholesale and retail electricity markets, keeping a downward pressure on electricity costs. Pillar 3 will support creating an enabling environment for water financing and management that promotes the incentives and governance framework in the national and local governments for climate-resilient water supply and sanitation (WSS) services. The three pillars integrate to facilitate a robust policy foundation for building a low-carbon and climate-resilient economy, continuing the development trajectory envisioned in the Philippine Development Plan 2023-2028 (PDP 2023-28).

P181501.project_abstract: The development objective of Enhancing Prosperity and Sustainability in the State of Sergipe Development Policy Financing Project for Brazil is to support the State of Sergipe to: (i) strengthen fiscal sustainability and (ii) improve the management of natural resources in the areas of water, sanitation, solar energy and solid waste. The government of Sergipe’s program outlines a multifaceted strategy to address key challenges, emphasizing poverty reduction, economic development, infrastructure, sustainability, strengthened governance and efficient state management. Highlighting a strong commitment to strong environmental stewardship, the government’s program also sets strategic goals for improving environmental sustainability and natural resource management, including through sanitation. Key aspects include the expansion and improvement of access to water and sanitation with a focus on improving service delivery. Further, the program focuses on promoting regionalized and integrated solid waste management and strengthening water resources management to enhance resilience to the impacts of climate change. Reforms supported under the program are instrumental to achieving these objectives. The first pillar of the Development Policy Finance (DPF) supports reforms that improve fiscal sustainability. The second pillar of the DPF supports selected policies to improve the management of natural resources. The two pillars of the DPF are closely aligned with the guiding themes of the government’s Multiannual Plan (Plano Plurianual – PPA). The proposed operation is aligned with the goals of the Paris Agreement. The project is compliant with the Maximizing Finance for Development (MFD) approach by implementing fiscal management and regulatory reforms that will enable new private sector investment.

P181538.project_abstract: Given the climate change vulnerability and other water security challenges faced in Mali, a more integrated approach is crucial to sustain economic development and reduce fragility and conflict. The proposed project will enhance drought and flood resilience by supporting sector development through a comprehensive water management approach, focused on improving access to drinking water supply service as a continuation of Kabala investment program in Bamako and in priority areas while protecting associated water resources at the sub-basin level. To this end, the project will support keystone climate resilient water investments for water supply and water source conservation, as well as operational and institutional measures to enhance sector performance, such as non-revenue water reduction and support to define priority reforms. The project will not only tackle immediate water access and quality concerns but also lay the groundwork for long-term resilience and contribute to water-related job growth and economic activity. In supporting these activities, the project contributes to building a more stable, sustainable future for the country, reducing the risks of water-related conflicts, and supporting economic growth in vulnerable regions.

P181603.project_abstract: The development objective of the Disaster Risk Management Development Policy with a Catastrophe Deferred Drawdown Option Project is to strengthen Rwanda’s preparedness and response to disaster and health emergencies, while strengthening the resilience of its built environment and natural resources. The Cat DDO directly supports the GOR’s DRM program through three strategic pillars. Pillar 1 enhances disaster risk information and institutional coordination; Pillar 2 strengthens climate and disaster resilience of the built environment; and Pillar 3 improves natural resources management for resilience. These pillars align with the 2023 DRRM Policy and NST2 priorities, and the associated prior actions were jointly developed with relevant ministries, departments, and agencies. The Cat DDO is fully aligned with the World Bank Group’s Country Partnership Framework (CPF) for FY21–FY26. The CPF emphasizes the need to enhance resilience to climate-related risks, particularly urban flood risks exacerbated by unmanaged urban growth. The Cat DDO contributes to Objective 5 of the CPF by supporting policy reforms that reduce vulnerability and promote sustainable urban development. The operation complements other WBG operations supporting efforts to mainstream DRM and climate considerations. The design of the Rwanda Cat DDO integrates key lessons from previous operations to ensure strong ownership, coordination, and implementation.

P181633.project_abstract: The project will contribute to protecting the human capital of the population of Yemen by supporting the delivery of essential health, nutrition, water, sanitation and hygiene services at both primary health care (PHC) level and at referral secondary care centers, as well as capacity building of the health and water and sanitation systems.
P181633.regionname: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan, and Pakistan

P500620.project_abstract: The proposed operation is aimed at providing universal access and improving the sustainability of water supply and sanitation (WSS) services in the Kyrgyz Republic through a partnership involving the Government of Kyrgyzstan, the World Bank, other development partners, and the private sector. This partnership will be operationalized through a Multiphase Programmatic Approach (MPA) using Investment Project Financing (IPF) lending instruments with some performance-based grants (PBGs). The MPA program is structured as a 10-year engagement through three IPF operations/phases to achieve the Program Development Objective. The MPA program includes: (i) a four-year Phase 1 (2024-2029) of focused investment covering unserved rural settlements while consolidating the service delivery structure into district-level Water and Sanitation Service Provider (DWSSPs) on a pilot basis and enhancing the service delivery capacity using PBGs, few and simpler sanitation interventions, complemented by extensive analysis and design of more complex sanitation solutions, including paving the way for potential private sector engagement; (ii) a three-year Phase 2 (2028-2032) to scale-up the investments, shifting to water supply infrastructure upgrade and expansion, priority sewerage investments and scale-up the service delivery models, with a focus on operational and financial efficiency, and climate adaptation; and (iii) a three-year Phase 3 (2031-2034) to scale-up sanitation investments, and consolidate the water supply improvements, with a strong emphasis on knowledge exchange and appropriation of the policies, plans, and tools developed under the previous two phases. Phase 1 project will comprise five components: Component 1 - Water Supply Investments - will finance the construction of new water supply systems in unserved rural villages as well as the upgrade of existing water supply systems in district centers and small towns in priority country areas; Component 2 - Sanitation Development - will finance the construction and/or rehabilitation/retrofitting of decentralized sanitation systems, including toilet upgrades for vulnerable households and selected public institutions (schools, kindergartens, health centers), including Technical Assistance (TA) to design more complex sewage improvements in subsequent phases; Component 3 - Performance-based Service Improvement Program - will finance PBGs to selected water sector institutions to implement their institutional and WSS services development plans and improve the sustainability of WSS services, Component 4 - MPA Program Structuring and Management - will support the establishment of a program management framework and WSS infrastructure development capacity; and Component 5- Contingency Emergency Response.The risk classification and assessment provided in this ESRS covers only Phase 1 of the MPA.

P501037.project_abstract: The development objective of the Second Inclusive and Resilient Market Economy Development Policy Operation for Uzbekistan is to support Uzbekistan’s transition to an inclusive and resilient market economy through: (i) creating markets, (ii) improving fiscal risk management and public procurement, and (iii) supporting social inclusion and climate change action. The operation supports key government priorities. The first program development objective (PDO) pillar supports measures to improve the business and investment environment, and to enhance efficiency and reduce state dominance in the key sectors of agriculture, rail, and energy. The second pillar supports measures to improve fiscal risk management, especially relating to public-private partnership (PPPs) and public procurement. The third pillar supports measures to expand in-work social protection, further measures to protect victims of domestic violence, and measures to strengthen environmental sustainability and climate change action.

P504170.project_abstract: The proposed Project builds on the ongoing water reform agenda and the progress already achieved under the GBWSP, while addressing emerging challenges. The proposed Project (hereinafter “the Project” or “SGBWSP”) will finance the completion of the water supply infrastructure, ensuring the achievement of the objective of bringing additional water supply to extended areas of GBML. In addition, the Project will support the necessary add-ons to the Wardanieh WTP to manage increased levels of pollution expected at different times of the year. The SGBWSP will also support institutional strengthening to improve the operational and commercial performance of the BMLWE in line with the MoEW vision for the recovery of the sector outlined in the NWSS 2024-2035 and the conclusions of analytics funded by the World Bank and other donors.  The project will consist of six components: Component 1: Completing remaining bulk water infrastructure.This component will finance investments to complete the remaining bulk water infrastructure started and significantly advanced under the GBWSP to ensure water is conveyed to the existing water supply distribution network serving the GBML area. It will also finance the required construction supervision services for all the works. The component will have the following subcomponents: (i) completion of the remaining tunnel and ancillary works; (ii) remaining Pipeline Works, which include the completion of the twin transmission pipelines; and (iii) construction of three regional reservoirs to increase the overall resilience and efficiency of the distribution system by allowing the BMLWE to regulate the flow of the stream depending on the fluctuations in water demand.  Component 2:  Strengthening treatment processes for improved water quality This component will finance adjustments (add-ons) to the Wardanieh WTP treatment processes to enhance the resilience of the Wardanieh WTP to variable raw water quality while maintaining operational costs within the financial capacity of the BMLWE. In addition, this component will finance activities to improve water quality management at the BMWLE, including the rehabilitation and upgrade of BMLWE’s laboratory at the Dbayeh through the expansion of existing facilities and the purchase of sufficient equipment, laboratory tools and chemicals, and the reinforcement of chlorination practices to ensure residual chlorine in BMWLE’s distribution network.Component 3: Reforms for improved utility operations This component will finance activities aimed at improving the operational, financial and customer orientation performance of the BMLWE, in alignment with the vision for the recovery of the sector in Lebanon. These activities are divided in three sub-components: (i) NRW reduction in four zones in the GBML area, namely Babdaa Suburbs, Coastal Metn, Tallet EL Khayat, and Bourj Abu Hadar (ii) modernization of BMLWE’s operational and commercial management systems, such as BMLWE’s Information and Communication Technology (ICT) infrastructure, the Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA), Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) software, customer management processes, and entity audits; and (iii) improved communications, which will finance the development and implementation of the BMLWE’s Communications Strategy and the implementation of semi-annual public awareness campaigns. Component 4: Land Acquisition and Resettlement Compensation : This component will include land acquisition and resettlement compensation to displaced people for: (i) the reservoirs Hadath 90 and Hazmieh 90 to be constructed under subcomponent 1.3;  (ii) a section of the twin transmission pipelines under subcomponent 1.2; (iii) the Joun Regulation Structure (JRS) to control the raw water flow to the WTP under subcomponent 1.1, and (iv) the land for the outflow of the Wardanieh WTP. The implementation of this component will follow the provisions of the resettlement action plan (RAP). Component 5: Project management and capacity building. This component will finance the operation of the Project Management Units (PMU). In addition, this component aims to address gaps in female representation in technical positions through focused training and capacity-building activities for women, enhancing their skills for technical and managerial positions. As such, this component will support an apprenticeship program that will target female participants to facilitate exposure to professional opportunities within BMLWE. The program will not be exclusive to women but will aim to have more than 50 percent of female participation.  The BMLWE will benefit from the work of young and well-trained apprentices, while the new graduates will be certified on utility-related technical skills (like NRW reduction, SCADA management, asset management, etc.) and gain on-the-job experience that will enhance their competitiveness on the job market. Component 6: Contingent Emergency Response Component (CERC). This component will have zero funding allocation at the onset and will only be triggered in emergency circumstances.
P504170.regionname: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan, and Pakistan

P504998.project_abstract: The project aims to (i) enhance resilience to floods in selected localities, and (ii) strengthen the Government’s capacity for managing climate- and disaster-related risks. These components are: (a) Institutional and System Strengthening: infrastructure planning, design, management and preparedness; (b) Enhancing resilience and sustainability of critical infrastructure; and (c) Project Management. Component 1 will support the development of detailed engineering designs and construction supervision for selected flood mitigation investments within the Castries and Anse La Raye areas, while also strengthening the Government of Saint Lucia’s capacity to prepare for and manage the impacts of natural hazards and climate change. Component 2 will support the implementation of prioritized activities designed under component 1, which will support an integrated river basin approach toward urban flood risk management and reduction.

P505093.project_abstract: The project (Phase I) comprises two main components: (i) Infrastructure and (ii) Governance and Project Management. The Infrastructure component focuses on the rehabilitation and expansion of water supply and sanitation (WSS) infrastructure in the Lake Titicaca and Lurin basins. This includes retrofitting designs and executing works for the drinking water and sewer networks in Puno, developing engineering designs for seven wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) in the Lake Titicaca and Lurin basin, and implementing a Managed Aquifer Recharge (MAR) pilot in the Lurin basin. The project aims to enhance climate resilience, service efficiency, and sustainability by integrating circular economy principles, reducing non-revenue water (NRW), increasing energy efficiency, and reducing greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. Project Management will finance goods and services for implementation support, including technical assistance and administrative support for M&E, procurement, FM, and E&S management. Meanwhile, the Governance component aims to support the implementation of the WSS circular economy roadmap at a national scale. This involves strengthening the capacity of key institutions under the Ministry of Housing, Construction, and Sanitation (MVCS) to incorporate circular economy principles in infrastructure design and service delivery. The component will also build the capacity of selected utilities to manage WSS infrastructure services and create an enabling environment for scaling up initiatives aimed at reducing NRW, increasing energy efficiency, and reclaiming and reusing water. The project will ultimately enhance climate adaptation and mitigate the climate impact of WSS service delivery.

P505271.project_abstract: The project will finance the following activities to realize its development objective:Component 1: Strengthening Sector Foundations  Component 2: Strengthening the Policy and Regulatory Framework   Component 3: Expanding Access to FinanceComponent 4: Project Management Component 5: Contingency Emergency Response Component

P505546.project_abstract: The project intends to restore ecosystem processes directly, it will result in expanded areas of healthier ecosystems (restored functionality) and broader range of generated ecosystem services (benefits). All this will ultimately improve landscape/habitat conditions and water quality through reanimated synergies between upstream and downstream ecosystems.Nature-based solutions (NBS) are defined as use of resources and processes already found in nature to restore ecosystems, support communities, and contribute to healthy and resilient economies. NBS for nutrient pollution management leverage, inter alia, the natural filtering and absorption capacities of ecosystems such as wetlands, forests to capture, retain, and remove nutrients like nitrogen and phosphorus from runoff, surface water, or groundwater.The project addresses pollution of freshwater environment through enhanced adaptive capacity for upstream ecosystem restoration (at landscape level), all following the EU directives and the Dniester Commission. The proposed Project’s design consists of three components: (i) Component 1: Institutional Capacity Development and Community Engagement; (ii) Component 2: Landscape Restoration and Watershed Management and (iii) Component 3: Project Management, Monitoring and Communication.

P505562.project_abstract: This Project is the first phase in the Multiphase Programmatic Approach (MPA) and focuses on improving climate and water information management, enhancing water security, irrigation services, and water supply and sanitation in two basins, Aden-Tuban and Wadi Hajir.  The Project includes four components designed around a robust systems approach that responds to the complex, FCV context in Yemen. These are informed by deep analysis and contextual awareness of the key issues in the water and agricultural sectors and include a high degree of flexibility built around a combination of top-down and bottom-up interventions. These components are 1: Climate and Water Information Management Systems (CWIMS), 2: Improved Integrated Watershed Management and Irrigation, 3: Resilient Water Supply & Sanitation Systems and WASH Services in selected areas and 4: Project Support, Management, Evaluation and Administration. The Project will be implemented through UN agencies that exhibit technical understanding and readiness for implementation in specified basins. Financial Management Framework Agreement (FMFA) and Alternative Procurement Plan (APA), allowing UN agencies to use their own Financial Regulations and procurement procedures as APA under the Procurement Framework Policy Section III.F.Under the Project, UNDP and UNOPS will serve as grant recipients and implementing entities. These agencies have robust and adaptable implementation arrangements tailored to local needs and will strengthen the capacity of local partners, whose past commitment demonstrates strong potential. The two UN agencies will provide technical assistance and training to local institutions, progressively transferring implementation technical capacities to them while retaining overall fiduciary responsibility and accountability. Local partners, high-performing private contractors, and community groups will be engaged for implementation. The Third-Party Monitoring Agents (TPMAs) will be engaged in monitoring and verification of all Project interventions, conducting quarterly supervision visits to Project sites for technical, safeguards, and fiduciary verifications.
P505562.regionname: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan, and Pakistan

P505581.project_abstract: The proposed project will provide support to strengthen communities’ engagement in the development and management of transboundary water projects and becoming more resilient to climate change, improve the collection and dissemination of information on environmental conditions and natural hazards, and improve the capacity of the NBD and its stakeholders to deliver on its mandate. The first key component on Community Engagements and Capacity Building will support (i) a program of citizen led data management on issues of water quality, water flows, land degradation, soil erosion, wetland degradation, and climate-related hazards with the aim of influencing policy, resource allocations, and behavior of citizens, (ii) community consultations with respect to select transboundary development programs, (iii) capacity building of the NDFs, and (iv) activities to strengthen communities’ resilience to climate change. The second key component on Innovative Platforms for Stakeholder Dialogue and Action will strengthen the NBD and its stakeholders through (i) Women’s empowerment and youth engagement in dialogues on Nile cooperation and transboundary water management (ii) Leveraging platforms for transboundary dialogue and knowledge exchange and (c) Scaling up the internship program through recruiting women and youth with expertise in specialized areas—water resources management, climate adaptation and resilience, data generation and management—to serve as interns.

P506027.project_abstract: The project will finance the design and piloting of an incentive scheme for select Kwenda cash transfer program beneficiaries living in climate-vulnerable areas with poor child nutrition outcomes. The first component will focus on financial incentives to promote the adoption of nutrition-sensitive services in the context of maternal, newborn, and child health. The second component will support the development of income-generating activities centered around reducing community exposure to climate risks. The project will help design and test cost-effective and scalable incentive models for these interventions, and develop operational tools to facilitate their integration into the broader Kwenda program.

P506272.project_abstract: The Program will enhance Karnataka's resilience against the impact of climate change and natural disaster, develop climate resilient infrastructure in Bengaluru to mitigate urban floods, improve carrying capacity of the waterfront and recycle, reuse floodwaters for drought mitigation, facilitate the mainstreaming of climate and disaster resilience in planning and implementation of investments. The main infrastructure to be financed under the Program includes nine sewage treatment plants, sewer line network, storm water drains, and lake rejuvenation.

P506340.project_abstract: The proposed project will strengthen multi-hazard climate and disaster risk management and institutional capacity for Maharashtra's resilient development. The planned components include the following: i) Climate-informed Flood Risk Management, ii) Multi-Hazard Resilience Building in Districts and Cities, iii) Enhanced Emergency Management Capacities, iv) Private Capital Mobilization for Risk Financing, and v) Implementation Support and Knowledge Management.

P506439.project_abstract: The regional WASH Access MPA aims to increase access to at least basic WASH services at people’s home, in schools and in health centers, following a common intervention framework across participating countries (country-level phases) and through a regional WASH acceleration  operation (regional phase). The MPA proposes an investments of around US$ 1.49 billion in IDA and US$ 0.24 billion in IBRD (Angola), aiming to leverage US$ 0.21 billion of Government financing, US$ 0.65 billion of other development partner financing, and US$ 0.26 billion of private capital financing, with the objective to reach around 30 million people with access to at least basic water and/or sanitation services. The MPA supports a "systems change"  approach tailored to the context of each participating country. The MPA designed with four pillars, each with a different menu of activities and interventions:Pillar 1:  Platforms to Accelerate Access to WASH : The national platform approach consolidates government-led efforts to achieve universal, sustainable WASH services by integrating funding and promoting cross-sectoral cooperation. National platforms, steered by political and sectoral leadership, will focus on the preparation and/or implementation of National WASH Investment Plans, as well as on policy development, and coordination and mobilization of various sources of financing, while emphasizing inclusivity and gender equality. Digital applications will be leveraged to enhance planning and transparency. Country-level implementation of WASH platforms will involve various government entities. Regional support is provided through benchmarking progress towards SDG6, facilitating knowledge exchange, and attract climate-focused financing to improve WASH access and environmental outcomes across Africa.The Regional WASH Platform (phase I), will serve as a catalyst for countries within the region to embrace more systematic approaches in scaling up access to WASH.  Phase 1 aims to facilitate countries' progress in accelerating sustainable access to WASH, through capacity building, progress monitoring, while fostering knowledge sharing among participants. Additionally, the Regional WASH platform plays a pivotal role in providing incentives and mobilizing funds, all directed towards advancing the regional WASH agenda. Through these concerted efforts, phase 1 under the MPA seeks to drive meaningful and sustainable improvements in WASH access across the region.Pillar 2: Sector Governance Reforms: This pillar focuses on strategic governance reforms, addressing non-financial barriers, and enhancing regulatory oversight. It will provide incentives and technical assistances to conduct necessary governance and institutional reforms to improve the enabling environment and the efficiency of public investments to attract increased private sector capital. Sector reforms and their implementation will be informed through analytics and the deployment of tools like Public Expenditure and Institutional Reviews, and Water Sector Assessment Programs (Water SAPs) to identify bottlenecks and establish roadmaps for improvement. The approach includes designing incentive schemes to promote efficient capital expenditure and regulatory alignment, with a focus on decentralized government levels. Under the Regional WASH Platform (phase 1), regulators and policy makers will receive technical assistance to expand their capacities and mandates through regional twinning arrangements, knowledge exchange and technical assistance on governance reforms.Pillar 3: Service Providers Performance Improvement: This pillar focuses on enhancing WASH service delivery models by improving the sustainability of operations and service provision in both urban and rural areas. This pillar aims at improving the creditworthiness of urban utilities as well as the implementation of sustainable operations and maintenance (O&M) models in rural areas. It will support the development and implementation of Performance Improvement Action Plans (PIAPs) and Rural Water and Sanitation Service Professionalization Strategies (RWSSPS) to set medium-term objectives and improve operational and financial sustainability. The implementation of such plans and strategies will aim at reducing water losses and energy consumption, improving the operating ratio of utilities and rural service providers through increasing revenues, cost-efficiency measures, leveraging digitalization. It emphasizes the importance of synchronized strategies for service expansion and operational efficiency, particularly in sanitation, and may involve City-Wide Inclusive Sanitation (CWIS) Plans and their implementation where relevant. Additionally, the pillar aims to ensure affordability and inclusion for low-income populations, promote citizen engagement, and improve service delivery through mechanisms like utility scorecards and closing customer feedback loops. Regional capacity building initiatives for utilities, rural service providers, city administrations and rural WASH agencies will be supported through the Regional WASH Platform (phase 1).Pillar 4: Leveraging Financing for Resilient Investments to expand WASH coverage: This pillar aims to leverage both public and private financing to expand resilient water and sanitation infrastructure, focusing on investments in access expansion, including trunk investments, water supply networks, WASH facilities in schools and health centers, and rural sanitation programs. By working with IFC and MIGA, this pillar will finance WASH resilient infrastructure designed to withstand climatic shocks and aims to enable and mobilize private capital, for example through PPP transactions and risk mitigation instruments to mobilize commercial financing and equity. The pillar includes a range of resilience-building activities, WRM analysis and resilient infrastructure design support. Under phase 1 it includes Technical assistance for diagnostics and analysis that can enable private capital mobilization, as well as training and competency development for private sector participation. Moreover, it  includes state-of-the art transboundary aquifer assessments and the development of transboundary groundwater / aquifer management plans for MPA countries.

P506770.project_abstract: The Program includes two results areas: Results Area 1: Strengthen institutions, planning, and financing. This RA will strengthen the institutional framework, capacities and systems at local level (CCs, Municipalities) for addressing key urban sources of water pollution. Results Area 2: Reduce urban pollution. This RA will support city corporations (CCs) and DWASA in improving the coverage and quality of sanitation services, solid waste management services and reducing water pollution in rivers and canals. An Investment Project Financing (IPF) component is proposed to support institutional capacity and systems development, investment preparation, program management, coordination and reporting. The component will support agencies to strengthen regulatory enforcement, river pollution management and planning, implement reform activities, prepare investment plans, design and implement investment projects, undertake important technical activities such as information, communication and awareness generation, E&S capacity development, policy frameworks for mitigation of any adverse impacts on non-title holders and informal occupiers, and for sustainable management of dredged material, and preparatory studies for investments to be financed in subsequent phases of the program or through other sources. Lastly, this component will finance the costs of independent verification agency, incremental operational costs including recruitment of experts (technical, environment, social, financial management, procurement), and other expenses for program management and implementation support.

P506807.project_abstract: The project is premised on the dual agenda of low-carbon and nature-positive urban development which are essential, interconnected strategies for building livable cities. Low-carbon cities focus on planning, implementing, monitoring, and accounting for low-carbon investments and climate change mitigation. Nature-positive cities prioritize urban development that enhances resilience and livability by integrating nature. A joint approach applied in strategic areas that integrates multi-sectoral interventions, offers solutions to the intertwined challenges of climate change and nature loss, and creates mutually reinforcing benefits essential for long-term economic transformation and building sustainable, livable cities. The project intervention area focuses on the Aha Lake area, strategically located in the core of Guiyang city.
