It is designated as a national wetland park in 2015, serves as a critical urban green lung with important flood control and biodiversity functions. It is recognized that a single project cannot achieve all nature-positive goals or resolve all climate-related issues in Guiyang. Instead, the project targets Aha Lake, which embodies key urban development challenges and high potential, to demonstrate an integrated approach to these goals. These efforts are further supported by city-wide interventions and innovations on low-carbon platforms, nature-positive planning, and enhanced collaboration.The project will include the following three components: Component 1 (Low-carbon Urban Mobility for Enhanced Livability): This component will finance improvements and constructions around the Aha Lake, focusing on: (i) NMT network and connections to city-wide green network; (ii) public transit connections, including road upgrades for last mile bus connections to metro stations, facilities (bus interchanges, integrated visitor and transit facilities) and services (intelligent transport systems, improved bus coverage, frequency and service); (iii) road connections to and between key city thoroughfares; (iv) integrated visitor/parking facilities and amenities including EV charging stations and bicycle stands; and (v) traffic and transport management. Component 2 (Conserve and Enhance Urban Ecosystems and Biodiversity): This component will finance interventions to improve water quality, enhance flood control, and conserve urban ecosystems and biodiversity. It will reduce GHG emissions by decreasing water pollutants and untreated wastewater, thus reducing methane and nitrous oxide , and enhance the lake's carbon sequestration. Activities under subcomponent 2.2 will restore terrestrial and aquatic habitats and conserve biodiversity. In the long term, this component will improve resilience through better flood management and cooling effects, enhance livability, and create jobs and innovation in environmental conservation, water management, and eco-tourism. Component 3 (City-Wide Institutional Strengthening for Low-Carbon and Nature-Positive Urban Development): This component will finance city-wide initiatives to support the project’s sustainability, including: (i) technical analysis and development of low-carbon and nature positive urban spatial plans; (ii) research and technical assistance for policies, regulations, and plans on carbon sequestration, carbon market mechanisms, low-carbon transport for vulnerable groups, and behavioral change towards low-carbon lifestyles; and (iii) project management, capacity building, coordination, M&E, and knowledge sharing activities. This component aims to position Guiyang as a front-runner in integrating low-carbon and nature-positive thinking into urban systems, policies and communities.

P507116.project_abstract: The development objective for the First Zambia Climate and Economic Resilience Development Policy Financing with a Catastrophe Deferred Drawdown Option Project for Zambia is to: (i) enhance fiscal management and resilience, (ii) promote private-sector investment and resilience in enabling sectors, and (iii) strengthen disaster risk management and climate resilience. The DPF supports Zambia in carrying out reforms aimed at increasing resilience to economic and climate shocks, while promoting private sector-led growth and providing financing for immediate and potential liquidity needs following disasters. It aims to enhance the government’s capability to address the effects of climate change and economic shocks and to improve fiscal resilience. The operation strengthens Zambia’s DRM systems and enhances the resilience of households and the private sector to climate shocks, addressing critical gaps in the current institutional framework. Alongside the World Bank’s ongoing efforts to support Zambia in emerging from the current catastrophic drought, this DPF series provides readily available financing in case of a future shock. It also aligns with the international commitment to help the country restore macroeconomic stability and debt sustainability under the Common Framework. The program is structured around three pillars and eight prior actions, fully aligned with the GRZ priorities. Pillar A enhances fiscal management and resilience, creating buffers against economic and climate shocks and improving the sovereign credit risk assessment framework. Pillar B promotes private-sector investment and resilience in enabling sectors, bolstering private-sector participation in the transport, energy, and water sectors and reducing the financial impacts of future natural disasters in these sectors. Pillar C strengthens disaster risk management and climate resilience, improving the coordination and effectiveness of the GRZ in managing disasters, enhancing household resilience to climate shocks, and making water resource management more climate resilient. The Cat DDO can be drawn once a “state of disaster” in case of adverse natural or public health-related events has been issued according to the process indicated in the Disaster Management Act of 2010.

P507147.regionname: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan, and Pakistan

P507207.project_abstract: The Burundi Water Supply and Sanitation Access Project aims to increase access to climate-resilient water supply and sanitation services and to strengthen the capacity of water operators. The project aims to provide safely managed drinking water services  to previously unserved populations and to improve the quality of services from basic to safely managed drinking water services in Bujumbura through expanding production capacity , distribution systems and household connections. For rural areas, the project aims to improve access to at least basic water supply and sanitation services in selected communes prioritized by the government. Moreover, the project will improve access to water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH) facilities in selected schools and health centers within the rural communes. Aligned with the regional Multi-phase Programmatic Approach (MPA) on Accelerating Access to WASH services in the Eastern and Southern Africa (AFE) region  - the AFE WASH Access MPA, the proposed components are the following:Component 1: Strengthening National WASH Platform, Governance and Reforms. Subcomponent 1.1.National WASH Programming and Platform is aligned with pillar 1 of the AFE WASH Access MPA and finances technical assistance, training and equipment to develop and implement sector-wide monitoring platforms for WASH access, medium term investment plans, joint sector reviews and future investment preparation studies, such as for Gitega. Subcomponent 1.2 Advancing Governance and Reforms is aligned with pillar 2 of the AFE WASH Access MPA and finances technical assistance, training and equipment to address governance bottlenecks and support the implementation of reform measures to enable private capital mobilization for investments in the sector in the medium term. This includes the development of regulatory instruments and policies, tariff studies, transaction advisory services, capacity development for sector ministries, the national water and sanitation regulatory entity and other sector stakeholders, as well as selected communes benefiting from investments. Component 2: Service Providers Performance Improvement: This component, mapped to pillar 3 of the AFE WASH Access MPA, finances works, equipment, technical assistance, training for the national water utility REGIDESO as well as for rural water supply providers and the rural water agency. Subcomponent 2.1 finances the implementation of REGIDESO's Performance Improvement Action Plan, including Non Revenue Water (NRW), digitalization, and water quality management improvements, household connections and technical advice for REGIDESO to develop a pro-poor household connection policy and funding mechanism. Subcomponent 2.2. finances the preparation of delegation contracts between communes and private water operators, capacity development for professionalization of domestic private water operators, household connections for rural population, as well as vocational and professional development programs for job creation in the water sector.Component 3: Increasing Climate-Resilient Water Supply and Sanitation Access . This Components aligns with Pillar 4 of the AFE WASH Access MPA. Subcomponent 3.1 finances infrastructure investments in climate -resilient urban water supply distribution and production systems for Bujumbura, including civil works for a new water intake and water treatment plant, pumping station(s), transmission mains, reservoirs and distribution network, as well as related electro-mechanical equipment and SCADA systems.  Subcomponent 3.2 finances rehabilitation and construction of solar-based climate-resilient rural water systems in selected commune(s), behavior change campaigns, material support for durable sanitation facilities, and education activities for girls on menstrual hygiene management. Component 4: Project Management and Coordination. This component finances the overall project management, monitoring and coordination costs, including operating costs and participation in international and regional knowledge and learning activities. It also finances the coordination costs for Burundi stakeholders related to their effective participation in the AFE WASH Access MPA.

P507256.project_abstract: The project is designed with four components: (i) Improving agricultural productivity and resilience to climate change, (ii) Improving storage, agro-processing and access to Markets, (iii) Promoting Access to Finance and private sector investment, and (iv) Institutional Strengthening and Project Coordination.  Component 1: Improving Agricultural Productivity and Resilience to Climate Change (US$ 96.3 million IDA). Component 1 focuses on boosting agricultural productivity and building resilience to climate change by promoting the adoption of climate-smart technologies, strengthening input supply systems, and supporting farmer organizations. Key activities include expanding access to quality seeds, fertilizers, and mechanization, enhancing extension services (with an emphasis on women farmers), and facilitating digital solutions for climate and pest monitoring. The component also supports diversified production and irrigation development, including solar-powered systems and secured land tenure, with targeted support for women and youth. Component 2: Improving Storage, Agro-processing and Access to Markets (US 23.2 million IDA). Component 2 focuses on improving market access, reducing risks, and increasing the availability of local agricultural products, with special attention to women, cooperatives, and SMEs. Planned investments include cold storage, warehouses, and climate-resilient market facilities, complemented by road and mobility projects, training processors—especially youth and women—constructing processing units, and encouraging the adoption of climate-resilient technologies. Promising SMEs will be identified for further support and scaling opportunities.Component 3: Promoting Access to Finance and Private Sector Investment (US$15 million IDA). Component 3 focuses on improving access to financial services for farmers and agri-entrepreneurs at various stages of production, processing, and marketing, while also encouraging private sector investment in key agricultural value chains. The component aims to expand financial services, especially for underserved groups—and promote green financing through activities such as matching grants for adopting climate-smart agriculture, bolstering national agricultural insurance programs, and leveraging partial credit guarantees to mobilize private capital. This includes the establishment of two windows for matching grants, targeted insurance improvements, and capacity building with an emphasis on women and youth. The component will also foster private investment by addressing barriers, providing technical assistance to business development service providers, improving market access for processed products, promoting contract farming, and disseminating information about the Agricultural Investment Code. Component 4: Institutional Strengthening, and Project Coordination (US$ 15.5 million IDA). Component 4 will address (i) sector policy and governance issues, and related training needs, and (ii) project coordination and management activities. This component will be implemented by the Project Implementation Unit (PIU) under the oversight of the Project Steering Committee (PSC).  Component 5: Contingent Emergency Response Component (CERC) (US$0).	A CERC with zero allocation may be used to contribute to an emergency response through the timely implementation of activities in response to an eligible national emergency. A CERC is included in the project in accordance with the World Bank IPF Policy (paragraphs 12 and 13, for Situations of Urgent Need of Assistance and Capacity Constraints). This will allow for rapid reallocation of uncommitted funds under the credit in the event of an eligible emergency as defined in the World Bank Operational Policy on Rapid Response to Crises and Emergencies (OP 8.00). A Contingent Emergency Response Manual will be prepared as an annex to the PIM (CERC annex). The environmental and social instruments required for the CERC are prepared, disclosed, and adopted in accordance with the CERC Manual and the Environmental and Social Commitment Plan (ESCP). For the CERC to be activated, and financing to be provided, the Government will need to: (a) submit a request letter for CERC activation, and the evidence required to determine the eligibility of the emergency, as defined in the CERC annex, an Emergency Action Plan, including the emergency expenditures to be financed; and (b) meet the environmental and social requirements as agreed in the Emergency Action Plan and ESCP.

P507433.project_abstract: Agriculture is central to Nepal's economy and integral to poverty reduction.  It accounted for over 60 percent of employment and nearly 24 percent of GDP in 2022.  But the productivity and level of income from agriculture remain below potential because of underinvestment in critical enablers, such as irrigation services, agriculture value chains, and watershed services.  Therefore, this project contributes to addressing constraints to agricultural productivity while also building the resilience of the sector to climate extremes.  Nepal is rich in water resources but only uses about 10 percent of it for productive purposes due to inadequate infrastructure.  Nepal has a total of 2.53 million ha of irrigable arable land, of which 62 percent is irrigated, and less than 40 percent has access to year-round irrigation, leaving the agricultural sector unproductive and highly exposed to rainfall variability.   The result is persistent food insecurity (affecting over 12 percent of the population) and rising food imports (150 percent increase since 2014).  The project is the last of three phases of a long-term government program that has been under implementation since 2012 to modernize the Rani Jamara Kulariya Irrigation scheme – the largest irrigation scheme in the western region of Nepal.  The Bank supported both Rani Jamara Kulariya Irrigation Project (RJKIP) Phase 1 (2012-2017) and Phase 2 (2018-2025), which modernized irrigation infrastructure on 14,300 hectares, enhanced flood and erosion control, improved rural roads and bridges for better connectivity and market access, and promoted watershed conservation, capacity building of water user associations, and agricultural support services. These efforts have benefited over 157,000 people. RJKIP 3 will build on these achievements, expand access to irrigation services, increase farm productivity, enhance climate resilience, and boost rural incomes in Kailali district, Sudurpaschim Province. It will construct the distribution network westward, along with complementary investments in agriculture and watershed management. The Project Development Objective (PDO) is to improve irrigation services and agricultural productivity in the target areas. It addresses three of the World Bank’s priority global challenges: i) Water security and access; ii) Climate change adaptation and mitigation; and (iii) Food and Nutrition Security. The project consists of four key components aimed at enhancing irrigation service, watershed management, and agricultural support services in the region. Component 1: Irrigation Improvement and Development (US$95 million) seeks to expand and improve the irrigation network across approximately 17,500 ha of existing agricultural land in Pathariya, where farmers do not have access to year-round irrigation, and the availability of water is expected to be further exacerbated by climate-induced changes in rainfall patterns and an increased likelihood of droughts. The component will distribute part of the existing intake discharge of 80 m³/s drawn at Chisapani from Karnali River. The main canal bifurcates to supply the allotted 40 m³/s to the RJK area and the remaining 40 m³/s to Lamki and Pathariya (see Figure 2). From that point, the GoN has completed the construction of the Lamki feeder canal and is in the process of developing distribution networks in its command area. However, the feeder canal and distribution networks in the Pathariya area (17,500 ha) have yet to be developed, which this project aims to complete. These investments will enable farmers in that region to access their designated canal water supply throughout the year and increase cropping intensity, thereby enhancing the resilience of beneficiaries to climate shocks and contributing to climate adaptation. Component 2: Watershed Management (US$20 million)  will finance activities to prevent soil erosion, conserve watershed areas, and mitigate the impact of floods and droughts on agricultural productivity. It includes riverbank protection, landslide and gully control, check dams, and afforestation. The component will also support the rehabilitation and augmentation of existing small-sized multipurpose water storage to capture monsoon precipitation. This is crucial for sustainable water storage and the long-term sustainability of irrigation systems. Component 3: Agriculture Support Services (US$11 million) will serve the command areas covered by the RJK irrigation system, including the area from RJKIP Phases 1 & 2, and Pathariya. The objective of this component is to enhance the productivity, capacity, and resilience of the farmers to floods and droughts through value addition, post-production support, market access, and adoption of modern technologies on a demand-driven basis. The activities will include (i) strengthening and mobilization of local cooperatives, adoption of modern technology, promoting mechanization, promotion of climate-smart agricultural (CSA) practices, nursery establishment, commercial farming targeting high-value crops; (ii) providing of grants, and (iii) providing postproduction support for storage, processing, and marketing of targeted agricultural produce; (iv) providing training and capacity building. Component 4: Institutional Support and Project Management (US$11 million) will finance the capacity building and institutional strengthening of WUAs in the project area and DoWRI, and its implementation units.The expected outcomes include (a) 17,500 ha of farmland benefiting from year-round irrigation services, (b) 52,000 people benefiting from increased agricultural production, and (c) 160,000 people benefiting from enhanced climate resilience.  The beneficiaries of the project are the farming communities in Kailali District, Sudurpaschim province, who will benefit from access to irrigation and agricultural services, thereby increasing farm productivity, enhancing climate resilience, and boosting rural incomes.

P507588.project_abstract: The Project is designed to increase access to water supply and sanitation services, improve the operational performance of water service providers, and enhance water resources management in Sierra Leone. Its components are mutually reinforcing, creating a platform for both immediate service improvements and long‐term capacity building that will drive water security. The proposed Project is structured around six components:Component 1: Institutional Strengthening and Capacity Building (US$8.55 million).MWRS will implement this component, which will include technical assistance, equipment and consulting services.  Subcomponent 1.1: Policy, Institutions, Regulation Strengthening (US$1.35 million) will: (i) review and complete existing sector policies and regulations (e.g. 2010 Water Policy and draft 2021 WASH Policy) to ensure that they contribute to delivering on WASH priorities and include provisions to address climate-related risks such as floods and landslides; (ii) support MWRS in operationalizing its sanitation mandate by developing its organizational structure, responsibility-sharing arrangements and essential management systems and tools to be able to deliver on its mandates; (iii) strengthen the role of the MWRS in coordinating WASH sector interventions across national and local governments; (iv) support logistics related to regular Donor coordination group meetings involving all development partners and NGOs involved in the sector to structure it as a platform to share information, promote coordinated approaches, ensure alignment with GoSL’s priorities, crowd in more funding for the sector and promote private sector participation; (v) review policies and regulations and advise on PPP options to leverage private sector value addition to improve the quality-of-service delivery and to extend access to WASH services in Sierra Leone; and (vi) develop sanitation and fecal sludge management guidelines and design standards for climate-smart WASH systems with specific measures to meet ECOWAS’ energy-efficiency standards; and (vii) strengthen the national WASH M&E platform.Subcomponent 1.2: Capacity Building and Knowledge Development (US$2.0 million) will: (i) assess the institutional and staff capacity building needs of MWRS, NWRMA, GVWC, SALWACO, SLEWRC, Freetown City Council (FCC) and WARDC; (ii) build on the assessment to develop a comprehensive institutional and staff capacity building plan and design adapted delivery mechanisms for each institution involved; (iii) develop training material and curriculum for entry level staff, vocational and on-the-job trainings for existing technical and managerial staff, and evidence-based decision-making course for executives and senior officials. Specific training materials, processes and incentives targeted to women will be devised to ensure that women’s perspectives are considered during curriculum development and delivery, and that training formats are adapted to encourage women's participation, including through safe learning environments and consideration of practical constraints; (iv) equip one existing training center. Parts of the capacity building activities may be delivered through the PPPs for WASH service improvement and extension.Subcomponent 1.3: Construct a WASH Administrative Center (US$5.2 million) will procure and construct a WASH Administrative Center to host the MWRS, NWRMA and SLEWRC, as the previous MWRS building burnt down in 2023. This Center will seek to showcase good water and wastewater management (e.g. rainwater harvesting for toilets and nature-based solutions to treat wastewater). It will be designed to meet or exceed ECOWAS energy efficiency standards to plant trees for shadow and install solar panels on roofs and parking shadowing screens, so it acts as a demonstration and learning site. It will also provide laboratory equipment to allow regular water quality measurements to detect industrial or domestic pollutants and will structure a revenue stream to ensure the sustainable supply of consumables.Component 1 will enhance the country’s resilience to climate change–exacerbated risks such as floods, droughts, extreme heat, and landslides by improving water governance, capacity building and strengthening relevant government institutions. Improved governance will contribute to watersheds and ecosystems preservation, and higher water quality.Component 2: Water Resources Management Improvement (US$3.0 million).This component will be managed by the NWRMA, with support and oversight by the MWRS. It will support activities to improve water resources planning and mobilization and to protect the Western Area Peninsula National Park, the only water resource for the Western Area. This will be done through technical assistance and consultants services, based on a collaborative and consultative approach. This component will also promote data-driven decision-making, stronger regulation, and community engagement—key pillars in improving WRM in Sierra Leone.Subcomponent 2.1: National Water Resources Master Plan and Sector Knowledge Enhancement (US$2.6 million). Activities under this subcomponent will develop a national Water Resources Master Plan, including updating the existing national inventory of ground- and surface water abstraction points in Sierra Leone, informing the hydrological and hydrogeological characteristics, determining the water balance in the country’s various River Basins, estimating the water resources and groundwater potential of each River basin, modelling climate-induced impacts on water resources, and mapping and documenting land and water use patterns of major River Basins. The masterplan will also examine measures to enforce the implementation of existing WRM Regulations, in particular the annual ‘licensing to operate’ for all drilling companies operating in Sierra Leone, and the need to obtain a permit and to register any new borehole into the national inventory. This activity is a prerequisite to securing water resources outside of the Western Area in subsequent MPA phases.Subcomponent 2.2: Raising awareness, catchment protection and rejuvenation need (US$0.4 million). This subcomponent will include three main activities. First, it will fund erecting billboards to demarcate the greenbelt boundary in areas requiring raising awareness of the limit beyond which human activity means encroaching and increase monitoring and surveillance. Second, it will build protective barriers to protect the catchment of selected water supply weirs either in the buffer zone outside the greenbelt or within it and which are under increasing encroachment pressure. Third, it will prepare a map of priority areas suggesting catchment restorative nature-based solutions (e.g., afforestation, soil conservation) to be implemented to restore the greenbelt forest cover in places where it has been degraded and monitor soil moisture and carbon sequestration to demonstrate that preserving forest cover increases climate resilience. To implement these activities, NWRMA will collaborate with MWRS, GVWC, Ministry of Environment and Climate Change (MoECC), the National Protected Area Authority and NGOs active in this field, such as the Catholic Relief Services. By enhancing water resources planning, monitoring, and protection, Component 2 will support long-term water security, improve resilience and reduce the potential impact of floods (as water will be less contaminated) and droughts, as enough clean water will become available when and where needed, and water service disruptions will be reduced. Component 3: Priority Investments in Urban Water Supply (US$16.25 million).This component will be implemented by GVWC, with oversight by the MWRS. It will finance works, goods and consultants’ services aiming at improving GVWC’s operational efficiency, water supply service delivery in Greater Freetown and cost recovery and creditworthiness. It will complement an ongoing project of the Africa Development Bank (AfD

P507658.project_abstract: The proposed project aims to improve the resilience of Hai Phong city through multisectoral investments in transport connectivity, sanitation improvement, urban inundation reduction, and urban resilience capacity. The project will have four components. Component 1 will support development of a new greenfield 18 km-long section of Ring Road 3. It will act as an extension of the Hanoi-Hai Phong Expressway to create better connections from the north to the Lach Huyen port, Cat Bi airport and Hai Phong’s industrial parks/economic zones, resulting in lower net GHG emissions. Component 2 would help improve environmental sanitation in the catchment area of Re River. It would include support for Re River infrastructure upgrades, Re River basin environmental sanitation improvement including construction of a Waste Water Treatment Plant (WWTP) and construction of tertiary sewer network. Component 3 will help reduce flood risk in the historical city center through an urban resilience strategy of delay, store and discharge and includes construction of two new sluice gates at the joints of existing Tam Bac River with Thuong Ly river and Cam River, thus transforming it into a regulation lake for meeting the increasing storm water regulation demand. Component 4 will help enhance capacity.

P507749.project_abstract: The Project is structured under five components. Component 1: Hazard Monitoring and Early Warning Systems, will invest in global and country-level decision support systems, instrumentation, and capacity and institutional strengthening for multi-hazard early warning systems, supporting both climate-related and geophysical hazards. Sub-components include: i) Early warning delivery systems for monitoring natural and climate  related hazards and dissemination of timely, accurate, reliable and impact-based alerts; ii) monitoring and communication equipment such as ICT hardware and software, meteorological, hydrological and volcanic monitoring equipment and technology; and iii) institutional strengthening and capacity building to ensure that the necessary skills, knowledge, and frameworks are in place to monitor hazards and disseminate early warnings effectively, improving the technical and institutional capabilities of the Government.Component 2 Emergency Preparedness and Response (EP&R), will enhance national and community-level response capacities to better prepare for, and respond to, future disasters. Sub-components include: i) Emergency management infrastructure, to enable whole of government response; ii) Emergency management capacity, to improve Samoa’s EP&R systems to maintain readiness and operational efficiency during emergencies; and iii) Dissemination, education, and outreach, to ensure that first responders and communities are well-informed and equipped to respond to emergencies.Component 3 Resilient Infrastructure - will invest in national and local activities to reduce the exposure and vulnerability of select urban areas to climate and natural-related hazards. Sub-components will include: i) Planning and institutional strengthening, including technical assistance for development of a Sālelologa township plan, SMP, and Strategic Environmental and Social Assessment (SESA), updating and development of national and subnational planning legislation and policies, and conducting technical studies, feasibility studies and vulnerability assessments; ii) Resilient public facilities, to showcase current disaster and climate resilience, sustainability, and accessibility standards including pilot waterfront upgrading in Sālelologa, combined with coastal protection and community spaces, urban streetscape upgrading, access roads to basic services, stormwater drainage, and coastal protection in urban areas of Samoa, retrofitting and construction of critical government buildings in Savai’i, and multifunctional public spaces, including parks, green spaces, and floodplain management spaces.Component 4: Project Management, will finance support project management and implementation including the recruitment of consultants to support the implementation of all Project activities;  technical assistance to support evaluation of the Project at the mid-term point and completion, as well as auditing; and (iii) capacity building and training on project management and implementation.Component 5: Contingent Emergency Response Component (CERC), will support emergency response activities under a pre-allocated CERC for quick disbursements to meet the immediate liquidity needs of the Recipient following an eligible disaster event to finance critical imports; or to finance emergency response, recovery and reconstruction works and associated services.

P507888.project_abstract: The proposed operation seeks to improve access to inclusive and resilient basic services and economic opportunities in Western Cote d’Ivoire.The project is structured around the following core components:Component 1: Resilient and inclusive local development at the community level finances (i) climate resilient, small-scale community infrastructure for health, and other basic services selected by communities and relying on community labor ; and (ii) targeted support to women and youth economic groups for income-generating activities through direct financing and technical assistance from implementing partners.Component 2: Socio-economic development at the regional level finances (i) regional-level economic infrastructures (markets, granaries etc.), based on value chains assessments to strengthen economic development, connectivity; and (ii) local investments to rehabilitate and improve existing water points (pump repair/replacement, technician refresher training, capacitating water committees).Component 3: Knowledge generation and institutional strengthening finances (i) community behavioral interventions focused on social cohesion to ensure equitable, transparent access to shared services , support local mediation at village and sous prefecture levels ; (ii) analytical work to identify potential institutionalization pathways for CLD in Cote d’Ivoire and (iii) Capacity building for local stakeholders.Component 4: Project management finances staffing and operational costs to ensure effective, transparent delivery through robust coordination, safeguards, fiduciary management, M&E, and beneficiary feedback mechanisms.

P507912.project_abstract: The Development Policy Financing with a Catastrophe Deferred Drawdown Option Program financing is an International Development Association (IDA) credit of 300 million dollars (DPF of 100 million dollars and Cat DDO of 200 million dollars). The Tanzania DPF with Cat DDO introduces a comprehensive framework to (a) strengthen Tanzania’s disaster and health-related emergency preparedness and response (EP&R) systems, (b) integrate resilience and risk prevention across critical sectors, and (c) enhance economic resilience and establish a foundation for accessing global carbon markets. Prioritized by the Government of Tanzania and backed by robust analytical work, the program will address ongoing climate and disaster risks through reforms under three pillars. Pillar 1: Strengthening institutional and financial capacity for disaster preparedness and response aims to strengthen the organized disaster response capabilities of the health and DRM sectors. This pillar will increase human capacity within government systems for effective response; promote coordination between the health and DRM sectors in areas of preparedness and response; and operationalize response teams, enhancing readiness for both natural and health-related disasters. The pillar also promotes the establishment of flood early warning systems (FEWS) and decision support systems in key flood-prone basins to safeguard at-risk populations, improving disaster preparedness. The pillar will also support the allocation of additional dedicated resources to the Contingency Fund. Pillar 2: mainstreaming disaster risk management in key sectors aims to incorporate resilience and risk prevention principles into the planning, decision-making, and management of essential infrastructure and services. This ensures that current and future infrastructure investments in key sectors are risk-informed, contributing to long-term resilience against climate-related and natural hazards. Specifically, the pillar will support efforts to reduce risk-prone urban expansion through risk-informed urban planning and bolster the resilience of Zanzibar’s water and energy sectors. It will do this by enhancing the reliability of power infrastructure and supporting sustainable management of the island’s groundwater resources, informed by aquifer monitoring and preventing overextraction and contamination, and mitigating risks of saltwater intrusion. Pillar 3: enhancing economic resilience and establishing foundations for carbon market access aims to enhance Tanzania’s economic resilience by strengthening institutional, financial, and regulatory frameworks and enabling access to global climate finance through carbon markets. The pillar promotes fiscal transparency, sustainable investment, and accountability while integrating climate risk considerations into financial institutions’ decision-making to support adaptation, mitigation, and long-term resilience to climate-related disasters.

P507944.project_abstract: The Integrated and Resilient Urban Development for Jobs (PRODUIRE) operation supports Madagascar in reducing systemic urban risk while enabling sustainable economic growth and job creation. As Phase I of a Multiphase Programmatic Approach (MPA), it addresses recurrent flooding, weak urban services, and insecure land tenure in Greater Antananarivo and selected secondary cities—key constraints to private investment and productive urban employment—through a phased, learning‑driven approach.Phase I integrates investments in flood protection and drainage, solid waste management, urban upgrading, and land administration and municipal governance to reduce climate risk, improve service reliability, and unlock productive urban land. In response to Cyclone Gezani (2026), it also includes a targeted pilot for risk‑informed housing recovery in Toamasina, designed as an urban risk‑reduction intervention rather than humanitarian relief, to test scalable approaches for future MPA phases.

P507957.project_abstract: Due to Chad’s climate-driven water variability and reliance on water-based livelihoods, a comprehensive approach (a platform) is vital for tackling water security and climate risks. The Project integrates infrastructures, reforms, and nature-based solutions to boost resilience and sustainable development. It aims to widen access to water services, invest in rainwater harvesting infrastructure, safeguard water resources at the sub-basin level and help reduce drought and flood impacts. Key reforms will improve urban utility (STE) as well as domestic private sector in rural areas’ performance and sustainability. The mutually reinforcing investments in Nature Based Solutions and operational capacity, strengthening the basis for ecological and financial sustainability. This comprehensive platform for engagement is a sound basis for building durability and provides opportunities for varied implementation. The Project will be foundational by combining infrastructure, ecosystem restoration, and social resilience. It aims to: (i) expand drinking water and sanitation access in urban and rural areas; (ii) create multi-purpose water infrastructure, and (iii) restore watersheds for sustainable resources. Activities are planned with conflict sensitivity to strengthen social cohesion and prevent violence primarily in provinces affected by refugee influx and other province. The project supports local conflict management related to natural resources and aligns with Chad's National Strategy for Social Cohesion (2024-2029), and the World Bank Group’s FCV Strategy, addressing climate change, land degradation, and supporting communities affected by the Sudan crisis.Given that all provinces in Chad face significant deficits in water services and challenges related to natural resource management, the Project will adopt a targeted and phased approach to geographic prioritization . Based on these criteria, the Project will focus on the Bahr El Gazel, Batha, Borkou, Ennedi East, Ennedi West, Ouaddai, Sila, Tibesti and Wadi Fira as well as the city of N’Djamena.

P508001.project_abstract: This project seeks to support Yemen in strengthening a Climate and Water Information Management System in selected water basins to enhance sustainable and resilient water resources management and development. It will support a comprehensive assessment of the enabling environment for the strengthening, upgrading and revitalization of the CWIMS. This will include an assessment of existing data collection infrastructure, the various databases, along with capacity in order to identify gaps at decentralized levels of the National Water Resources Authority (NWRA) and other relevant institutions.  Investment plans will be developed to address the gaps identified in the capacity assessment. The project will also support the review and development of inter-institutional rules and regulations at basin level to improve existing water resources data water management in Yemen, and the development of a cloud-based data repository which can be populated and accessed by relevant institutions. Hard and software needed for the establishment of these activities will also be financed by the project, as well as a capacity building component to strengthen the technical capacities of decentralized institutions in using these systems and the development of end-user products.
P508001.regionname: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan, and Pakistan

P508034.project_abstract: The operation will use the Multiphase Programmatic Approach (MPA). Accelerating and scaling up access to rural WASH in Pakistan requires a significant change in sector governance and hefty investments, necessitating a programmatic, long-term approach. Traditional, retail-based approaches to projects, typically focused on asset creation, have failed to deliver sustainable rural WASH solutions at scale. A system change -comprising policy and institutional reform, a professionalized and efficient service delivery model, and a significant focus on behavioral change- is essential to close the WASH access gap in rural Pakistan and to leverage scarce fiscal resources to crowd in multilateral development banks (MDBs), the private sector, and other partners. An MPA's long-term and sequential nature makes it the most suitable instrument to deliver systems change incrementally and sustainably. The MPA is comprised of four mutually reinforcing pillars: (a) sector governance, (b) access to water supply services, (c) access to sanitation, and (d) behavioral change for strengthened hygiene. Subsequent phases of the MPA will customize the theory of change (ToC) based on their needs, priorities, available resources, and capabilities, provided these are aligned with the PrDO.The MPA comprises four overlapping phases. Its total estimated envelope is US$2.55 billion, of which US$1.4 billion is financed through IDA/IBRD funds and US$900 million from counterpart funding and co-financing from other development partners. The implementation timeframe will be from FY26 to FY35, coinciding with the CPF’s timeframe. It encompasses the Sindh, KP, and Punjab provinces, with the potential to add the Balochistan Province based on client demand.STAR-WASH-1 will finance activities and investments that will contribute to reducing stunting prevalence in Sindh rural areas by addressing structural bottlenecks for service delivery, including technical, governance, and financial aspects. As such, the Project is expected to finance the extension of water supply schemes, latrines, and transport, treatment, and disposal of fecal sludge and wastewater. The investment will be supported by comprehensive behavioral change campaigns to improve hygiene practices related to water storage and treatment, handwashing, menstrual hygiene management, safe disposal of child feces, and solid and animal waste management. The inventions will occur at the household level and in public places (healthcare facilities and schools, among others). Under the STAR-WASH-1 Project, WASH investments will be complemented by support to strengthen the sector's institutional and financial architecture for a major change to the existing arrangements.STAR-WASH 1 will be co-financed by the ADB under the new co-financing framework, where the Bank will be the lead agency for preparation and implementation. ADB is expected to mobilize US$100 million.
P508034.regionname: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan, and Pakistan

P508094.project_abstract: The proposed PforR Program will support a subset of the GoPunjab’s program in selected cities focusing on two result areas: (i) Safely managed water, sanitation and drainage and basic hygiene services; (ii) Institutional strengthening and financial sustainability. Infrastructure investments through Program funds will include: (i) integrated water supply networks; (ii) sewerage networks and wastewater treatment facilities; (iii) stormwater drainage networks and storage facilities; and (iv) SWM reforms and a pilot for material recovery and sanitary disposal. The IPF Component will assist GoPunjab to further develop its sector policy, strategy, and programs; strengthen its institutions and systems; and provide technical support to key provincial departments and urban agencies to deliver on Program targets and Program-related technical, fiduciary, social and environment management aspects.
P508094.regionname: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan, and Pakistan

P508124.project_abstract: The proposed Program is aimed at providing access to improved irrigation services and safely managed rural Water Supply and Sanitation (WSS) services in selected areas of Armenia through a partnership involving the Government of Armenia (GoA), the World Bank (WB), and Agence Française de Developpement (AFD). Recognizing that the goals of this program cannot be addressed through a single operation, it will be operationalized through a Multiphase Programmatic Approach (MPA) to facilitate sustainable transformation. The phases are progressive, and each phase maintains a similar structure and builds towards the achievement of the overall program development objective. The structure of the MPA is uninterrupted and overlapping to ensure that the Program's activities continue without a gap in the implementation of the key activities. The MPA includes (i) a six-year Phase 1 (2025-2031) with focused investments in enhancing the institutional capacity of critical water sector entities, revision to the methodology and structure of irrigation and WSS tariffs, completing core water sector strategies and irrigation master plan, finalizing all infrastructure investment feasibility and design stage documents for all proposed investments in irrigation and rural WSS, civil works to modernize five irrigation schemes, and construction/rehabilitation of supply infrastructure in selected settlements for rural water supply on a pilot basis using performance-based conditions (PBCs); and (ii) a six-year Phase 2 (2029-2035) to scale-up and expand infrastructure investments in all remaining irrigation schemes and priority settlements for rural WSS with a focus on operational and financial efficiency, and climate adaptation.  The second phase will place a strong emphasis on institutionalization and widespread uptake of the policies, plans, strategies, and tools developed under the previous phase. The Phase 1 program, using the Investment Project Financing (IPF) lending instrument with PBCs, aims to enhance climate-resilient access, efficiency, and financial sustainability of irrigation and rural WSS in selected areas of Armenia. It will focus on four components: (i) Component 1 – Water Sector Reform and Institutional Strengthening with PBCs: This component will focus on strengthening national water resources policy and planning through development of a comprehensive national water strategy, a ten-year national irrigation strategy, a WSS national strategy, an irrigation master plan, and legal and regulatory reform. It will furthermore improve irrigation sector management and service delivery through a review of tariffs and subsidies, transformation of the Water Supply Agency (WSA) into an efficient and customer-oriented irrigation service provider, strengthening of the Ministry of Territorial Administration and Infrastructure (MTAI) and the Water Committee (WC), and establishing a Irrigation Water Accounting and Adaptation Center (IWAAC) for water and climate monitoring. (ii) Component 2 – Rural Water Supply and Sanitation Enhancement: This component will first focus on institutional strengthening, capacity building, and investment planning of rural WSS services through development of rural WSS sector policies, a roadmap, tariff review, and strengthening management capacities at the national and local levels. Furthermore, it will support investment planning, including the preparation of feasibility studies for priority rural WSS investments for both MPA phases. Lastly, it will support the construction of water supply infrastructure in selected unserved settlements,  which will include rehabilitation of the water network, including household connections, and any necessary water treatment infrastructure. In phase 1, these investments are expected to be in Ararat, Kotayk, Aragatsotn, Shirak, or Tavush, based on technical, economic, socio-political, and institutional considerations (iii) Component 3 – Modernizing Irrigation Infrastructure & System Management: This component will focus on financing rehabilitation and modernization of selected irrigation systems at main, secondary, and tertiary canal levels. Activities include rehabilitation and modernization of headworks, critical sections of main canals, and secondary and tertiary network levels currently managed jointly by WSA at the large main canal and reservoirs level and by Water User Associations (WUAs) at the secondary and tertiary distributary level. It will further focus on rehabilitating some critical infrastructure under the responsibility of the WSA to ensure continuous provision of irrigation water to WUAs in priority command areas. (iv) Component 4 – Project Management: This component will support project implementation, including procurement and financial management (FM) related to implementation of civil works, compliance with agreed environmental and social management measures, and project monitoring and evaluation (M&E). This component will finance staff costs; coordination of the project-financed activities with other ongoing International Financial Institution (IFI) projects in the water sector; and design, implementation, and reporting of baseline and project completion surveys.

P508202.project_abstract: The proposed Project is a is a financial intermediary project that seeks to enhance the availability of financial resources for sustainable agricultural and bioeconomy and the restoration of degraded forests and pasturelands, while simultaneously advancing the inclusion, economic stability, and climate resilience of the targeted beneficiaries.

P508221.project_abstract: The Santa Catarina Climate Resilience project will implement the first phase of an ambitious, multi-year integrated climate and disaster risk management investment program led by the Santa Catarina Civil Defense Agency. The project will support risk mitigation infrastructure works, increase institutional capacity to assess and monitor disaster risks, develop strategies to support the most vulnerable populations and increase participation of the private sector disaster recovery. Specifically, the project will carry out various river improvement, control and slope stabilization works to mitigate the impacts of floods and landslides on the residents of the rapidly urbanizing Itajai Valley, and other priority locations. The Project will also establish a foundation for future DRM investments in other parts of the state, through supporting the development of disaster risk reduction plans, which will identify specific investment priorities and estimated costings. The focus through these plans will be on the protection of vulnerable communities, public infrastructure, health and school facilities. Finally, the project will expand the climate monitoring and early warning capacities in Santa Catarina, with the aim of increasing radar coverage to the entire state, and cooperation with neighboring states, and improving the interface between climate monitoring and local civil defense agencies at the municipal level. Support to the private sector will be explored through existing emergency support programs for SMEs.

P508320.project_abstract: The proposed Central African Power Pool Regional Interconnection and Access (CAPP-RIA) Program is a Multi-Phase Programmatic Approach (MPA) operation that aims to expand regional electricity trade and increase access to electricity services in the Central African region. Supporting the establishment of a regional electricity market will enhance energy security, reduce costs of electricity supply and improve the resilience of the energy system. CAPP-RIA comprises two phases that focus on three thematic pillars: 1) augmenting regional integration, 2) expanding electricity access, and 3) strengthening institutional capacity. Over the next 10 years, CAPP-RIA will support regional integration in Central Africa through investments and technical assistance in developing the regional transmission network, reinforcing and expanding national and cross-border transmission and distribution systems, and strengthening the enabling institutional and regulatory environment for fficient power trade. Phase 1 includes preparing and implementing on-grid access projects in the Central African Republic (CAR), Chad, Gabon, Republic of Congo, and Cameroon, as well as Medium-Voltage (MV) cross-border projects. It also involves pre-investment studies for High-Voltage (HV) interconnections. The estimated budget for the first phase is US$330.5 million. For Phase 1, the CAPP Secretariat will be the implementing agency for the technical assistance aspects of the project whilst the national power utilities in the involved countries shall execute the on-grid access and MV cross-border investments.

P508364.project_abstract: The CRISP project aims to improve access to resilient irrigation services in selected areas of Ethiopia. The project will strengthen the performance, reliability, and climate resilience of irrigation systems by rehabilitating and modernizing key large- and medium-scale schemes, upgrading irrigation infrastructure down to on-farm systems, and expanding small-scale irrigation in vulnerable and food-insecure areas. It will introduce climate-smart and energy-efficient technologies to enhance water-use efficiency and sustainability, and pilot innovative approaches such as irrigation service fee systems, private service provision, water allocation and monitoring mechanisms, and salinity management practices. The project will further support agricultural production and value chain development by improving farmers’ access to inputs, mechanization, and markets, and by strengthening agri-business linkages to increase productivity and incomes. It will promote inclusive and participatory management models, empower water user associations and female farmers, and foster private sector engagement in irrigation services, financing, and agricultural commercialization. In addition, the project will establish digital tools for national irrigation information and asset management, enhance dam safety and environmental and social risk management, and build institutional and technical capacity at federal and regional levels to sustain climate-resilient irrigation development.

P508450.project_abstract: The proposed Project extends the Bank’s continued support to the provision of potable water, sanitation and transport connectivity services in the Pacific region of Colombia. It will complete and operationalize the infrastructure originally envisioned under the Water Supply and Basic Sanitation Infrastructure and Service Delivery Project  (P156239) and the Enhancing Waterway Connectivity and Water Service Provision in Colombia’s Plan PAZcifico Project (P156880). By carrying forward and finalizing these investments, the proposed Project aims to convert partial progress into sustained service delivery outcomes for water, sanitation and transport connectivity in one of Colombia’s most underserved regions, while addressing the bottlenecks that have slowed execution to date. Project implementation will build upon the experience and lessons learned from the FTSP during the execution of the original water and connectivity operations. The MIE, as implementing agency, has established a PIU for project management, fiduciary tasks, reporting, and environmental and social management. Project components include: Component 1: Water supply and sanitation improvement. This component supports expanding and upgrading potable water and sanitation services in urban areas with low coverage. Investments include water intake, treatment, distribution, metering, and sanitation infrastructure to reduce health and environmental risks. It also addresses solid waste management, strengthens the technical, administrative, and operational capacities of the entities responsible for service provision and regulation, and promotes the responsible use and sustainability of water and sanitation systems by the community.Component 2: River and river-maritime transport improvement. This component finances the design, construction, and maintenance of resilient and accessible river and maritime docks, including floating structures. It supports climate-resilient infrastructure, low-emission electric boats, and navigation aids to enhance safety and connectivity. Additionally, it includes capacity building and institutional strengthening, fostering the creation of community-based associations and providing training for transport service providers.Component 3: Project management and environmental and social management. This component provides support for project implementation, including financial management, procurement, disbursement, and the design and execution of environmental and social instruments, as well as operating costs and audits.Component 4: Contingent Emergency Response Component (CERC) establishes a mechanism for rapid access to financing in response to eligible crises or emergencies, enabling the reallocation of funds to address major adverse impacts from natural or man-made disasters.

P508541.project_abstract: Regional development remains a key priority for the government of Turkiye. A draft new National Strategy for Regional Development (NSRD) provides the framework for public investment programs. Regional public development investments depend largely on direct central government spending, administered by four Regional Development Administrations (RDAs) that cover the geographical scope of lagging regions.  These four RDAs are affiliated to the Ministry of Industry and Technology (MOIT).  Climate resilience is increasingly a key priority focus for regional development and related public investments.The government is pursuing a results-oriented program of public investment planning and budgetary allocations. This PforR (US$ 435 millon) will support the government program for Promoting Rural Initiatives and Improving Social, Economic, and Regional Development Infrastructure (PRIME), which is implemented by the RDAs and with the MOIT as the central line ministry. This PforR will reinforce ongoing and planned institutional reforms, enhancing the effectiveness of the government's regional development program (PRIME). The PforR is designed in coordination  with the Strategy and Budget Office of the Presidency (SBO) that oversees all budgetary decisions on public investments. The PforR instrument capitalizes on the Government’s transition towards a performance-based budgeting system.The IPF component of US$15 million will assist with implementation of the Program through (i) operation support, (ii) targeted technical assistance and capacity building, (iii) the integration of IT solutions, (iv) change and project management. The IPF component is not expected to finance significant IT procurement.The Program's Theory of Change focuses on overcoming identified barriers to improve the effectiveness of public investments in regional development covering three key areas: (i) enhancing agriculture climate resilience and value-addition; (ii) supporting sustainable tourism in targeted areas; and (iii) strengthening the governance and management of RDAs’ public investments to improve results-orientation and climate resilience across sectors, including in agrifood and tourism. The proposed Program is fully aligned to the new Turkiye Country Partnership Framework (CPF) FY24-28. It will particularly contribute to CPF Objective 2 on spatial development and Objective 6 on resilient infrastructure investments at the local level. It also complements other Bank investments in support of small and medium enterprises, also within the lagging regions, to stimulate economic activity (P181068); socially inclusive programs supporting women and youth entrepreneurs (P180173); and initiatives targeting economic recovery in earthquake-effected areas of  lagging regions (P502837).The World Bank can help ensure value for money and effectiveness as the government scales up its PRIME regional development program in the medium-term period ahead. The World Bank support to this PRIME government program of the RDAs will leverage the Bank’s comparative advantage to support the government on public investment management (PIM), governance and climate-smart regional convergence.

P508608.project_abstract: The development objective of First Sustainable and Inclusive Growth Development Policy Operation for Kyrgyz Republic is to support the government to ensure sustainable and equitable energy supply; enhance social service delivery and climate resilience; and strengthen fiscal sustainability and improve the business environment. The operation is informed by the government’s priorities set out in the long-term National Strategy for Development (NSD) for 2018-2040, the medium-term National Program for Development (NPD) for 2021-2026, and the Green Economy Strategy for 2024-2028. The operation supports 4 of the 6 pillars in the government’s NPD: (i) the enabling environment for development, including the investment and business climate, skills development, and access to sustainable water services; (ii) sectoral development, including green and renewable energy generation; (iii) social development including economic inclusion programs; and (iv) special development priorities, including regional and green economic development. The program also supports women’s livelihoods by providing financial assistance and financial and vocational training for investing in small-scale businesses. This operation is aligned with the goals of the Paris Agreement including the targets of the Kyrgyz Republic Nationally Determined Contributions (NDC), and the Strategy of the Kyrgyz Republic on Adaptation (2020). The DPO is aligned with the WBG’s 2024-28 Country Partnership Framework (CPF) for the Kyrgyz Republic. The operation is aligned with the WBG’s Maximizing Finance for Development (MFD) approach and private investment generated through the project is counted as Private Capital Enabling (PCE). The operation directly supports the achievement of the targets of the Kyrgyz Republic NDC and several reforms directly contribute to national mitigation and adaptation priorities.

P508689.project_abstract: Component 1 (Data Systems and Institutional Capacity) will strengthen the data systems and infrastructure on which agriculture innovation is based, while improving the capacity of regional organizations and national partners to use those data and tools to generate, manage, and disseminate innovation. Component 2 (Inclusive Innovation Scaling) will develop a menu of mechanisms through which public and private actors will deliver innovation to farmers, while improve technical and entrepreneurial skills so agriculture professionals can access job opportunities and continue driving agriculture innovation. Component 3 (Project Management) will support the day-to-day implementation, coordination, supervision and overall communication and management of project activities.

P508698.project_abstract: The proposed Tanzania Sustainable Rural Water Supply and Sanitation Program Phase II (SRWSSP2) aims to increase and sustain access to basic water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH) services across rural districts within the United Republic of Tanzania (URT). Building on Phase I, the program addresses persistent WASH service gaps, institutional weaknesses, and climate-related water stress. It will use a hybrid Program-for-Results (PforR) and Investment Project Financing (IPF) approach to enhance governance, incentivize performance, and mobilize private capital through the following three results areas: (i) Increased access and sustainability of at least basic WASH services, (ii) Policy, Institutions and Regulation for strengthened service delivery, and (iii) Maximizing finance for development.The PforR component will target only the mainland of the URT with access to WASH services while the IPF component will provide support to both mainland and Zanzibar. On Tanzania mainline, the IPF will; (i) support strengthening capacity for sustainable water resource management through aquifer mapping, resource planning, dams inventory, and development of digital systems for groundwater and dams monitoring, enhance technical; (ii) Program Implementation through: Support to the Independent Verification Agent (IVA) for results validation, Project management consultancy to strengthen procurement, financial management, M&E, safeguards, and coordination, and updating and dissemination of key safeguards instruments (GRM, Gender Action Plan, Stakeholder Engagement Plan, ESMF, RPF). Priority IPF activities in Zanzibar include: Strengthening of the Water Department through procurement of working tools and software’s for the 2 newly established Divisions (Water Resource Management and Water Supply and Sanitation Management divisions) under MoWEM; Procurement of Groundwater investigation and monitoring equipment; Technical assistance for Developing Feacal Sludge and wastewater management strategy, detailed designs, and tender documents for priority  sanitation investments; Detailed design and tender documents for water schemes development in Pemba; Drafting the National Water and sanitation Act of 2006 and its Regulations; and Rehabilitation works for Zanzibar Water Institute.The operation is aligned with and is part of the AFE Regional WASH MPA, and is expected to yield significant health, economic, and environmental benefits while addressing fiduciary, environmental, and social risks through targeted mitigation measures.

P508792.project_abstract: Azerigas is a ring-fenced business unit of the State Oil Company of the republic of Azerbaijan (SOCAR), with unbundled accounts, responsible for distribution and sales of natural gas to the domestic market in Azerbaijan. The company is experiencing significant gas leaks (fugitive methane emissions) from its legacy assets, most of which are more than fifty years old, estimated at 160 million m3/year (107 kilotonnes per annum (ktpa) CH4). Analysis suggests that around eighty percent by volume of these gas leaks can be repaired with straightforward and cost-effective interventions within a 7-year time period. Using a global warming potential (GWP) factor of 28, this would result in greenhouse gas (GHG) abatement of 2.4 million metric tonnes per annum (mmtpa) CO2e. The project will establish a recipient-executed financial Facility for gas leak detection and repair (LDAR) in the gas distribution system operated by Azerigas. The Facility will be launched with an initial grant from the GFMR trust fund. SOCAR will be the Recipient of the grant and the holder of grant and Facility accounts, and Azerigas will be the sole beneficiary of the grant and the implementing entity responsible for day-to-day implementation and project management. The money will be reallocated from SOCAR to Azerigas for agreed leak detection and repair activities to reduce fugitive emissions of methane on a rolling basis. The project will build on learnings from Azerigas’ pilot gas leak baseline program in Baku. Repair activities will be proposed and implemented by Azerigas according to preliminary agreed eligibility criteria. Proposed activities will be prioritized based on cost, complexity, impact, and other relevant criteria to maximize the amount of emissions reduced per US$ deployed.In order to guarantee a longer term impact, a key aspect of the proposed financial Facility will be that majority of financial benefits resulting from gas savings as a consequence of leak repair will be re-invested into the Facility, so as to mobilize additional capital to further rehabilitate Azerigas assets. Furthermore, the GFMR-funds initially and the Facility subsequently will be used to build capacity within Azerigas to carry out monitoring, reporting and verification (MRV) of emissions in line with the OGMP 2.0 standard.GFMR grant funding will enable activities that would otherwise not take place due to the strained and constrained financial situation of Azerigas, unlocking methane emission abatement action within the current decade.

P508930.project_abstract: The first phase of the MPA will establish a Credit Guarantee Vehicle as the first instrument of the Blended Finance Platform to mobilize private capital for investment in critical and resilient infrastructure such as the electricity, transport and logistics, and water and sanitation sector, to create jobs, reduce carbon emissions and deliver infrastructure services for citizens and businesses. Phase 1 of the MPA will consist of three parts namely: Part 1: CGV Capitalization: Provision of the Government Equity Participation to support the capitalization of the CGV and the issuance of CGV Credit Guarantees (including the establishment of the CGV and its management, staffing, governance, operational, monitoring and evaluation, financial management, procurement, environmental and social safeguards, and pipeline development arrangements, systems and procedures), Part 2: Pipeline Development: Provision of support for the development of a pipeline of potential projects for support under the CGV, including client engagement, the carrying out of risk assessments, pre-feasibility and feasibility studies, and legal, financing and operational structuring of projects and Part 3: Project Coordination and Implementation: Provision of support for the PIU and project management, including for financial management, procurement, environmental and social risk management, monitoring and evaluation, stakeholder engagement and coordination, and operational, administrative and compliance support for the establishment, operationalization and launch of the CGV.

P509041.project_abstract: Tocantins, Brazil’s youngest state, spans 277,620 km² and is home to 1.6 million people. Despite strong growth in agribusiness and transport connectivity, the state faces persistent regional disparities, rural poverty, limited productivity in family farming, and increasing vulnerability to climate change. Over half of its municipalities are classified as highly vulnerable, and infrastructure deficits contribute to economic exclusion and rural-to-urban migration.The paved state road network (5,841 km), which connects agricultural production zones to the BR-153 highway and the North-South Railway, suffers from chronic maintenance gaps, safety risks, and climate-related degradation. Tocantins is seeking to modernize road governance through long-term performance-based maintenance contracts (CREMAs and PPPs), supported by institutional reforms and targeted investments in road safety and resilience.Family agriculture represents over 70% of farms in Tocantins but contributes only 17% of agricultural output, constrained by low productivity, limited access to markets and finance, and weak farmer organizations. Public extension services are under-resourced, and climate-resilient practices are not widely adopted. Meanwhile, tourism, especially in the Jalapão region, holds untapped potential to diversify rural incomes and promote local development but remains underdeveloped and highly seasonal.The Tocantins Productive, Resilient, and Inclusive Development Project will promote (i) sustainable, safe, and resilient transport infrastructure; (ii) improved livelihoods and climate resilience for family farmers; and (iii) sustainable tourism in priority regions. The project adopts a multisectoral approach to reduce structural inequality and enhance climate adaptation, while strengthening institutional capacity and leveraging private sector participation.Component 1: Proactive maintenance, resilient and safe road infrastructure• Subcomponent 1.1: Climate-resilient road improvement, rehabilitation, and maintenance through CREMA and PPP• Subcomponent 1.2: Enhancing road safety management capacity and infrastructure• Subcomponent 1.3: Institutional strengthening for road asset management and PPPsComponent 2: Strengthening support to family agriculture• Subcomponent 2.1: Rural services for climate-smart and inclusive agriculture• Subcomponent 2.2: Direct productive support to family farmers• Subcomponent 2.3: Strengthening family farming organizationsComponent 3: Inclusive and sustainable tourism• Subcomponent 3.1: Strengthening tourism planning, governance and support infrastructure in Jalapão  • Subcomponent 3.2: Support for community-based tourism and small local tourism enterprises Component 4: Project management and institutional support

P509225.project_abstract: Rationale and Context: Good nutrition is foundational for jobs, empowering individuals with the health and nutrients needed to learn, develop skills, and perform effectively in the workplace. However, Benin continues to face serious nutrition and human capital challenges, with over 30% of children under five suffering from chronic malnutrition and high rates of anemia among children adolescent and women, which continues to impair their physical and cognitive development and hinder future productivity and economic opportunities. These issues are exacerbated by poverty, food price volatility, and limited access to diverse, nutritious foods. Poor feeding practices, low exclusive breastfeeding rates, and high prevalence of infectious diseases further worsen the situation. Gender inequality worsens nutrition challenges, as women—who provide most agricultural labor—have limited access to land and resources, restricting their ability to improve household nutrition. Their lower socio-economic status and long working hours also reduce time for childcare. Governance is a major challenge, as nutrition goals are often overlooked in sectoral plans and budgets due to poor integration and coordination across ministries. Resource allocation is consistently inadequate, with limited specialized staff and weak steering capacity, while funding streams are unpredictable and partner efforts lack effective coordination. Policies remain fragmented, focusing on isolated interventions instead of a comprehensive, multisectoral approach. Climate change further threatens food security, with frequent droughts and floods impacting agricultural yields. Despite national warning systems, agricultural actors remain vulnerable due to inadequate risk management. Other factors include poor access to health services and water, hygiene, and sanitation services as well as improving access to quality and nutritious foods.Investing in nutrition yields high returns, with every US$1 invested in undernutrition expected to generate US$23 in economic benefits, and each dollar spent on the national integrated school feeding program can generate up to 5.2 dollars in GDP returns, especially when linked to local agriculture in partnership with the private sector. Benin has also made early childhood development (ECD) a national priority, focusing on combating malnutrition through multi-sectoral, community-driven approaches and strong government commitment. Addressing these challenges requires strengthening governance, ensuring predictable and sufficient resource allocation, strategic partnerships and empowering vulnerable groups, especially women, to improve food and nutrition security in Benin. Proposed Program Scope and Boundaries:On July 26, 2023, the Government established a National Agency for Food and Nutrition (Agence Nationale de l’Alimentation et la Nutrition, ANAN)  under the Presidency to ensure the implementation of policies, strategies, standards and regulations in all areas of food and nutrition, in accordance with the national policy on food and nutrition. Almost a year later, on July 24, 2024, the Government adopted its new National Policy on Food and Nutrition for 2024-2033, laying out the need for better cross-sectoral approaches and integrated interventions to combat every form of malnutrition and improve food security. The PforR will support a subset of the government’s national nutrition program, focusing on regions with the highest burden of malnutrition based on the National Strategy on Food and Nutrition for 2026-2030. The program boundaries are defined by a set of results areas that align with the government’s strategy and the World Bank’s Country Partnership Framework (CPF) in preparation. The operation will finance a results-based portion of the government’s expenditure program, with Disbursement-Linked Indicators (DLIs) that reflect key milestones in service delivery, coverage, and outcomes.PrDO proposed: To reduce stunting among children under five years of age.PDO of ALAFIA I proposed: To improve utilization of quality integrated nutrition, health, and child development services in targeted areas. Key Results Areas being proposed: •	RA1: Increase utilization of quality integrated nutrition, health, and child development services•	RA2: Enhance community resilience and access to quality nutritious food supply through local engagement and private sector partnershipsAn IPF Component is also being proposed to improve multisectoral coordination, governance, and evidence-based nutrition program design.

P509251.project_abstract: Operation Abstract: Summary Description of Proposed OperationThe Kambarata-1 HPP, currently under development, is a transformative greenfield hydropower project, emerged among the least-cost projects for expanding clean energy resources in the Kyrgyz Republic and the Central Asia region. The project is located upstream of the Naryn river in the Kyrgyz Republic. Although initially conceived during the Soviet Union era in the 1980s, construction never commenced due to a lack of financing, which is estimated at around US$4 billion. In 2014, SNC Lavalin prepared a feasibility study for the project. Upon commissioning, the project will have an installed capacity of 1,860 MW (~50% of current installed capacity in the country), a 256-meter-high dam, 5.4 billion cubic meter reservoir, and 5,600 GWh annual generation.
