The project will serve as the main foundation of the country’s energy system, facilitating the decarbonization pathway and significantly improving water resource management in Central Asian region.The Kambarata-1 HPP project could generate the following significant economic, social, and environmental benefits for the Kyrgyz Republic if it is developed in a sustainable manner. It will: (i) support the government’s vision towards a green and affordable energy future; (ii) achieve energy security by resolving the country’s winter energy deficits; (iii) enable the energy security and political autonomy by avoiding reliance on imported energy, including from Russia; (iv) significantly gear power generation toward meeting winter peak demand, thus fundamentally solving the country’s seasonal energy deficits; (v) generate sustainable export revenues, thereby improving the sector financing performance; (vi) enable the diversification of the country’s electricity mix through integration of solar and wind energy at scale; (vii) strengthen the regional coordination efforts in energy and water resource management; and (viii) improve resilience to water related risks such as floods, droughts, and managed irrigation in the region. International Energy Agency’s net-zero assessment also confirms the hydropower as the backbone of global electricity security and the most cost-effective, dispatchable, and flexible low-carbon technology option to integrate solar and wind.Kamabarata-1 is a regional project in nature. With demand expected to grow by 40 percent by 2030, and triple by 2050 if carbon neutrality targets are to be met, substantial scale-up of generation capacity is needed for the region to cater to its growing needs while supporting a clean energy transition. The project, if fully funded, would be transformative as it would contribute to meeting the growing energy demand and lower costs of energy transition in the Central Asia region, enhance energy security through the further development of the Central Asia regional power market and help decarbonize the electricity grids in the Central Asia region, which are currently highly dependent on coal and gas (above 70 percent) for power generation. The Bank’s regional planning analyses confirm that Kambarata-1 would displace gas and coal fired generation in the region and thus support the region low carbon development. In addition, the Kambarata-1 project will enable large-scale deployment of variable renewable energy in the Kyrgyz Republic, Kazakhstan, and Uzbekistan through its flexible and storage capacity, facilitating the decarbonization pathway in Central Asia. Furthermore, the Kamabarata-1 project would allow for better management of water resources in Central Asia across seasonal variations, making it possible to better meet the irrigation and other water needs of the downstream countries (Uzbekistan, Kazakhstan).An adequate financing and development strategy will be required from the very beginning to ensure satisfactory technical, environmental, and social outcomes and address any potential macro-fiscal issues. In this regard, upon the request from the Government, the World Bank is providing a comprehensive support to the development of the Kamabarata-1 HPP project in a manner that is fiscally, financially, technically, socially, and environmentally sustainable. The Bank’s ongoing $18.6mln Technical Assistance for the Kambarata-1 HPP will finance: (i) updating previous studies to verify the technical feasibility and economic viability; (ii) strengthening the environmental and social framework; (iii) developing a fiscally sustainable financing plan and commercial framework; and (iv) project implementation support and capacity building. The Bank is in discussion with the Governments for financing of the project in phases, and have been requested to play a lead arranger role to attract other financiers for structuring a sustainable financing package for the project. The proposed Bank support under this MPA builds upon the long-standing partnership with the Government of Kyrgyz Republic, Government of Uzbekistan, and Government of Kazakhstan as the Bank has been selected as the lead partner in designing and implementing broader energy sector and tariff reforms. The MPA will also contribute to the Bank’s ongoing initiative on strengthening the energy connectivity and trade in Central Asia, including through the establishment of a regional electricity market under a pipeline project.Phase 1 of the K1-STEP Program will consist of the following components. Component 1: Early works. This component will finance the early works such as diversion tunnels, dam, powerhouse and substation pre-excavation, site installation. Component 2. Project design, management and supervision. This component will finance the PMC, project implementation support, and the operationalization of the Kambarata-1 Regional JV. (a)	Subcomponent 1. Operationalization of the Kambarata-1 Regional project company. (b)	Subcomponent 2. Project Management Consultancy (PMC). (c)	Subcomponent 3. Project implementation support. (d)	 Subcomponent 4. Owner’s cost. Component 3. Implementation of social and environmental management plans. This component will finance the implementation and monitoring of E&S instruments including ESMP, Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) and Livelihood Restoration Plan (LRP) requirements. This component will also support the implementation of a comprehensive benefit sharing plan in support of possible affected communities and national social programs.Component 4. Water management institutional capacity and regional coordination support. This component will finance a set of hydro meteorological instrumentation and consultancy services required for the Kambarata-1 HPP project, including key monitoring investments in the upper Naryn river basin to support the operation of Kambarata-1 HPP and mitigate future climate risks, and improve the water management institutional foundations within the Kambarata-1 HPP project and broader regional framework.

P509293.project_abstract: The proposed Green and Future Cities (GFC) Project is a coordinated World Bank Group (WBG) initiative that integrates International Bank for Reconstruction and Development (IBRD) and International Finance Corporation (IFC) support to ILBANK and metropolitan municipalities in Turkiye to deliver scalable, sustainable and resilient urban infrastructure and services. The initiative will help participating municipalities address urban development and municipal investment finance challenges by leveraging the international expertise and technical knowledge of IBRD and IFC to provide a broader range of tailored financing instruments. Under the IBRD operation, the Project will deliver a package of investments and technical assistance to the participating metropolitan municipalities to improve access to sustainable and resilient municipal infrastructure and services. The Project will have three components: (i) Component 1. Integrated, multi-sectoral investments in participating municipalities: The component will support municipal investments contributing to more livable, productive, and climate resilient urban infrastructure and services in the participating municipalities. The eligible sector list is expected to include urban transport, and water and sanitation. This component will cover Antalya and Konya Metropolitan Municipalities; (ii) Component 2. Strengthening capacity of ILBANK and municipalities for future investment projects: The component will help ILBANK and eligible municipalities strengthen institutional and technical capacity in municipal finance and sustainable, resilient urban management, and prepare technical documentation for viable municipal infrastructure investments. (iii) Component 3. Project Management: The component will finance provision of goods, consulting and non-consulting services, operating costs, and training to ILBANK for Project management and implementation support activities.

P509357.project_abstract: The project aims to strengthen the resilience of vulnerable rural populations in the face of climate change, including the fight against land degradation and desertification, and to support the integrated and inclusive management of natural resources and land, including increased availability of water resources to support livelihoods. The project will support the development of low-carbon, nature-positive, value chains from non-timber forest products (NTFP) for vulnerable populations, particularly women and young people in the Great Green Wall intervention zone. Value chains such as gum arabic, balanites, and forage crops, will be part of nature based solutions to improve resilience to climate variability, restore degraded lands and improve economic resilience of vulnerable populations. Investments will include planting native and drought-resistant trees and crops, forest conservation, assisted natural regeneration, agroforestry, land restoration, adequate management of surface water (stabilization of dunes, stone lines, hedges, water catchments), reforestation with local and non-invasive species. The project will invest in improving knowledge and management of water resources, essential for maintaining livelihood activities, and will include water retention weirs and underground water harvesting practices.

P509563.project_abstract: The Project aims to improve livelihoods in Aksu district—particularly for vulnerable groups—by expanding sanitation access, creating jobs, and strengthening climate resilience. With 80 percent of the population lacking adequate sanitation, it will help reduce disease and pollution while generating employment through FSM and reuse-related opportunities. Treated sludge will be promoted as fertilizer for non-food crops, cutting reliance on costly chemical inputs and supporting climate adaptation. Community-led efforts will drive health, economic, and environmental improvements, building a more resilient and empowered Aksu community. The Project includes three components:The Project includes three components: Component 1: Developing Sanitation and Fecal Sludge Treatment Infrastructure will finance  the construction of a wastewater and fecal sludge treatment plant to enable sludge reuse and by-product generation (Sub-Component 1.1: Combined Wastewater and Fecal Sludge Treatment Plant), and the construction and rehabilitation of on-site sanitation systems for vulnerable households (Sub-component 1.2: On-Site Sanitation Improvements). Component 2: Establishing FSM Services, Local Job Creation and Community Awareness will support creation of FSM units to manage, operate, and maintain sanitation infrastructure and service delivery, a certified vocational training program, and quality assurance and marketing of Sludge By-Products (Sub-component 2.1: Establishment of Sanitation and FSM operators and Vocational Training). It will also support community engagement to raise awareness on FSM and resource recovery benefits (Sub-component 2.2: Community Awareness and Behavior Change Campaign). Component 3: Project Management and Administration, Monitoring and Evaluation, and Knowledge Dissemination will finance overall project implementation, project monitoring and evaluation, and sharing of project learnings and results.

P509605.project_abstract: This Investment Project Financing (IPF) operation will be financed by a small recipient-executed trust fund (RETF) in the amount of US$4.1 million. It would complement the Mali Improving Education Quality and Results for All Project (MIQRA, P164032), and its upcoming second phase. In addition, the project will seek complementarities with other interventions, such as the Mali Community Resilience and Inclusive Services Project – CRISP (P505025), by piloting a model of school construction in locations not covered by other projects. As such, the project proposes to establish a tested model that could be implemented in any of Mali’s diverse geographic areas using other projects, notably MIQRA’s second phase. The project is structured around three key components: Component 1 - Piloting community-based construction models in selected regions; component 2 - Identifying sustainable school construction approaches; and Component 3 - Project Management and Administration, Monitoring and Evaluation, and Knowledge Dissemination. The implementation period is three years.

P509738.project_abstract: The KEWASIP will support a subset of activities contained in The National Landscape and Ecosystems Restoration Programme Strategy. Under the KEWASIP the selected activities are expected to be grouped under three Results Areas: (i) strengthened watershed planning, governance, and monitoring; (ii) watershed services and landscape restoration (private/communal lands); (iii) Public land management and restoration (public/protected lands).

P509756.project_abstract: The objective of the Brazilian (BR) State of Piauí Strengthening Fiscal Sustainability and Fostering Sustainable Economic Development Policy Loan Program is to support reforms in the State of Piauí by (i) strengthening fiscal sustainability and the quality of public expenditure, and (ii) promoting private sector participation in water and land resource management for sustainable economic development. The first pillar aims to enhance fiscal management by implementing controls on current spending, increasing procurement efficiency, and improving the management of climate-friendly public investments. The second pillar focuses on creating an enabling environment for private sector-led management of natural resources, specifically water and land, to promote sustainable development. This will be achieved by strengthening the financial autonomy of the water and sanitation state regulator to enhance regulatory certainty and promote private investment; improving the raw water charging system to encourage efficient use; and digitizing the land tenure regularization system to improve land security and support sustainable agriculture growth. This DPF is part of a broader World Bank (WB) initiative to bolster fiscal and environmental sustainability and resilience in Brazil, aligning with the Green, Resilient, and Inclusive Development (GRID) approach. The measures are expected to yield significant fiscal gains, improve service delivery in water and sanitation, and streamline the land tenure regularization process. By 2026, current savings are projected to reach the equivalent of 8 percent of revenues, supported by improved public expenditure management. At least 60 percent of new public investment projects are expected to undergo cost-benefit analysis, and public procurement is set to become more competitive. In the water sector, reforms aim to mobilize greater investment and expand access for poor women, while doubling the volume of raw water subject to usage fees. Finally, the digitalization of land tenure regularization is expected to significantly accelerate processing times, reducing the duration from 90 to 30 days.

P509890.project_abstract: The Mozambique Urban Water Security Project aims to increase access to improved water supply services and strengthen service delivery capacity in selected cities by focusing on sector reforms to increase private sector participation; investing in infrastructure to expand water treatment, transport, storage, and distribution capacity in the Maputo metropolitan region and other southern cities; and supporting improved performance of institutions involved in water supply. The project design includes Performance-based conditions to support the performance improvement of regional water utilities, with the aim of modernizing urban water services, improving efficiency, increasing revenues, building resilient infrastructure, and developing skilled water professionals. Ultimately, the project aims to improve public health, economic development, and poverty reduction with a focus on Greater Maputo and the Southern Region, addressing water insecurity and improving performance in key regions as part of the broader country investment to close the gap towards universal access to water and sanitation in Mozambique.

P510120.project_abstract: The Ruzizi 3 Regional Hydropower Plant Project (Ruzizi 3 HPP or Project), which will have an installed generation capacity of 206 MW, is located in Central Africa along the Ruzizi River which forms a natural border between Rwanda, Burundi and Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), flowing southwards from Lake Kivu into Lake Tanganyika. Ruzizi III will be located downstream of the existing Ruzizi I (29.2MW) and Ruzizi II (43.8 MW) HPPs, commissioned respectively in 1958 and 1989. Ruzizi I HPP is located 3 km downstream of the outlet of Lake Kivu and controls the discharge from Lake Kivu. A fourth hydropower scheme, Ruzizi IV, is also planned in the future. The Ruzizi 3 HPP is configured to include the construction of a dam (51 meters high), a 3.8 km long headrace tunnel, a power station (206 MW installed capacity with an expected annual production of 1,140 GWh) and a 7 km long double circuit 220 kV transmission line to a substation at Kamanyola which is adjacent to the common border between the three countries. It includes a compact reservoir (approx. 46 hectares of inundated surface area) to provide regulation capacity and support the daily peak demand of the three countries. The scope of the Project also includes the following ancillary infrastructure: (i) permanent roads and access roads (9.6 km in DRC and 19.5 km in Rwanda), (ii) quarries and Borrow pit sites, and (iii) Spoil Disposal Areas. The project will include four components funded by various sources, including the CSs, concessional and non-concessional lenders, and the private sponsors: (i) Component 1: Hydropower Facility; (ii) Component 2: Substation and transmission lines; (iii) Component 3: Local area development; and (iv) Component 4: Project management and technical assistance. The IDA financing, credits and grants totaling US$260 million, will be provided either jointly or in parallel with the other lenders . Component 1: Hydropower Facility. This component aims at developing the hydropower facility as an Independent Power Producer (IPP). It includes the Engineering, Procurement and Construction (EPC) contract for the 206 MW hydropower facility, with an estimated cost of US$538 million. It also includes the associated project development, construction supervision, insurance, contingencies and project financing costs. The single turnkey EPC contract will cover the following items: (i) the access roads to the hydropower facility; (ii) the 51 meter high embankment dam, the river diversion works, the spillway, the bottom outlet, the eco-flow bypass and associated mini-hydro unit; (iii) the power intake, the 3,800 meter long concrete-lined headrace tunnel, the surge shaft, the 360 meter long steel-lined tunnel and penstock; (iv) the surface powerhouse with three turbine-generator units; (v) the switchyard, the 30 kV site distribution lines and the 7.1 km long 220 kV transmission line to the Kamanyola substation (in DRC); and (vi) the employer’s camp. Key resilience measures will ensure that the infrastructure is better equipped to withstand the challenges posed by climate change. The team is also exploring support from PPIAF and GIF to pilot the toolkit for resilient PPPs in hydropower projects, which would be highly beneficial to adopt innovative and best practices to include resilience measures as part of the EPC contract. The power generated by the facility will be sold equally (one third each) to the electricity utility companies of the three countries, REGIDESO in Burundi, SNEL in DRC and EUCL in Rwanda, together referred as the Offtakers, under Power Purchase Agreements signed in 2019. Component 2: Substation and Transmission Lines. This component, considered as associated facilities to component 1, will also benefit from support to pilot the climate resilience toolkit. It includes the construction of the Kamanyola 220 kV substation with a dispatch and cascade coordination center , as well as the three 220 kV transmission lines to evacuate electricity from the hydropower facility to the three countries, departing from the Kamanyola substation, along the following routes: (i) Burundi (Kamanyola-Bujumbura, approximately 77 km) ; (ii) the eastern provinces of DRC, which are currently isolated from the country’s national grid (Kamanyola-Buhandahanda, approximately 68 km) ; and (iii) Rwanda (Kamanyola-Karongi, approximately 93 km) . The climate-resilient transmission lines (reinforced poles and cables against floods and heatwaves, durable material used for transformers) are designed to withstand floods and heatwaves, using durable materials for transformers, and will help create a regional grid backbone for future power trade. Component 3: Local Area Development Program. This component will fund a local area development program (LADP) in each country, forming part of risk mitigation through community-focused activities. The LADP area includes the Groupement of Kashenyi and Karhongo in Walungu territory in DRC; Nzahaha and Bugarama sectors of Rusizi District in Western Province in Rwanda; and communes of Rugombo and Buganda in Cibitoke Province in Burundi, totaling around 385,115 people across 704 km²; about 547 average people per km2 (Burundi - 367/km2, DRC - 753/km2 and Rwanda - 874/km2). The LADPs were created with community input and feature investments in socio-economic infrastructures, livelihoods support, strengthening of existing Technical and Vocational Education and Trainings (TVETs), and tailored support to credit and saving groups. A validation process will prioritize investments based on community needs and in coordination with local authorities. Aimed at improving services and livelihoods for vulnerable groups, women, and youth, the program also seeks to enhance local administrative capacities. This approach promotes sustainable social benefits and adheres to the Bank's environmental and social framework (ESF) by mitigating adverse impacts and compensating for loss of physical assets and livelihood opportunities, connected to the routing of transmission lines and other project-connected infrastructure.Component 4: Project Support. This component will support project management, technical assistance, and capacity building for the CSs, public utility companies, and EGL. It includes advisory and analytical work to strengthen institutional, legal, and regulatory frameworks, and facilitate electricity trade and dispatch between public utilities. Associated  Facilities that will help dispatching the electricity produced by Ruzizi 3 include:•	Kamanyola 220 kV Substation is situated in DRC and developed by EGL with financial support from KfW. A Feasibility Study, ESIA and RAP were prepared in 2014. These studies will be updated by appraisal to reflect recent developments in the Ruzizi III Project and align with the World Bank’s Environemental and Social Standards.•	Kamanyola-Buhandahada 220 kV Transmission Line. It’s situated in DRC and is being developed by EGL with financial support from the KfW. Pre-Feasibility studies for the 68.5 km line have been completed. However, the studies will need to be updated by appraisal to take into account changes made to the Ruzizi-III HPP, and align with the World Bank’s Environemental and Social Standards.•	Kamanyola - Bujumbura 220 kV Transmission Line is developed in Burundi by REGIDESO with financial support from AfDB and KfW. Burundi line is already under construction and completion of works is expected in 2023.•	Kamanyola – Karongi 220 kV TL is developed by Rwanda Energy Group (REG) with financial support from the EIB. Feasibility studies are underway and ESIA is due by appraisal.•	Kamanyola - Kibuye 220 kV TL is developed by Rwanda Energy Group (REG) with financial support from the EIB. A pre-feasibility study and ESIA were  prepared in 2014. However, the feasibility study and ESIA will be updated by appraisal and a RAP prepared.The Project will be an important part of the power system in the three Contracting States (CS). It will offer si

P510227.project_abstract: The Project will adopt an integrated, multisectoral approach focusing on three interrelated topics: (a) the sustainable management of water resources, which is the foundation of water security for people, the economy and biodiversity as water is a shared and finite resource; (b) the water security for agriculture production, the main driver of Parana’s economy, and, at the same time, the reduction of agriculture contribution to water security risks and (c) the water security for potable water supply to increase its resilience to climate risks and to support the universalization goal, while reducing WSS contributions to water security risks. Retroactive financing is being considered for the Project which includes 4 components:Component 1 – Strengthen climate-informed water resources management. By promoting a better integrated, participatory, resilient and data-driven water resources governance and infrastructure, the component contributes to reducing pressures on water resources quantity and quality and supports more effective preparedness and responses to increasing climate change risks. In doing so, it lays the institutional and technical foundations for improving water security across the State, with emphasis on priority areas where activities are implemented in an integrated manner with other components. Subcomponent 1.1 – Modernize water monitoring, information and management instruments.  Subcomponent 1.2 – Improve integrated water resources governance. Subcomponent 1.3 – Reduce urban and peri-urban erosion. Component 2 – Enhance agriculture water security and reduce associated risks. This component is designed to enhance water security for agricultural production amid rising water shortages and land degradation exacerbated by climate change. It also seeks to reduce the risks of water contamination and water shortages in selected urban water sources associated with land degradation, agrochemical use, animal waste and climate change. Efforts will center on strengthening institutional and technical capacities, leveraging partnerships with cooperatives and local organizations, generating innovation and promoting the adoption of sustainable conservation and production practices.Subcomponent 2.1. – Strengthen the State capacity to manage water security risks in agriculture. Subcomponent 2.2 – Increasing Water Security for Family Farming in Areas Affected by Water Deficit and Soil Degradation.Subcomponent 2.3. – Decrease agriculture contribution to water security risks in urban water sources. Component 3 – Reduce water security risk for universal WSS access. This component aims to enhance the climate resilience, quality, and availability of water and sanitation services by reducing reduce water security risks in selected watersheds of urban water sources and addressing both infrastructure and management model gaps in rural areas, in support of the universalization goals. Subcomponent 3.1 – Reduce water security risks in selected urban water sources. Subcomponent 3.2 – Increase access to sustainable rural water and sanitation services. Component 4 – Support Project Management. This component builds the Borrower’s capacity to implement the Project by supporting fiduciary, technical, environmental, social, and monitoring activities. It provides funding for technical assistance, consulting and non-consulting services, training, operating costs, and necessary goods for key government agencies involved in implementation.

P510359.project_abstract: The operation is a multi-phase program approach (MPA) whose development objective is to support participating EAP countries in improving their water security and resilience to climate risks. Phase 1 of the MPA includes the Philippines Accelerated Water and Sanitation Project in Selected Areas (AWSPSA). The project development objectives, results indicators, and activities are aligned with those of the MPA and will contribute to the expected outcomes under the MPA. The proposed Project Development Objective (PDO) is “to increase access to safely managed water supply and sanitation services and improve the performance of water service providers in selected areas of the Philippines.”Component 1. Infrastructure Investments for Safe and Climate Resilient Water Supply Services (US$213.30 million, of which US$197.55 million IBRD).  This Component aims to increase access to safely managed water supply services through infrastructure projects, including construction and/or rehabilitation of complete water supply systems, from source development to household connections.Subcomponent 1a. Water supply infrastructure investments in the Loboc Cluster in Bohol ProvinceThis subcomponent will address the water scarcity and worsening quality that is particularly felt in the Loboc Cluster. Specifically, it will support (a) the development of a bulk water supply system sourced from the Loboc River with a capacity of 60,000 cubic meters/day; (b) the rehabilitation, upgrading, and expansion of existing water distribution systems in the Loboc Cluster; and (c) implementation of watershed protection measures. Subcomponent 1b. Water supply infrastructure investments in Siargao Island in Surigao del Norte ProvinceThis subcomponent will aim to provide safe and reliable water supply for Siargao Island residents and tourists by developing and enhancing the island’s water infrastructure. The focus will be on increasing the efficiencies of current water systems through NRW reduction and developing new sources to meet current and future demand. It will support 58 barangays in eight municipalities. Subcomponent 1c. Water supply infrastructure investments in Jolo and adjacent barangays in two municipalities in Sulu ProvinceThis subcomponent will aim to rehabilitate and upgrade the entire water supply system in the project areas, which are dilapidated and service only 35 percent of the original service area. The objective is to enhance water services' reliability, safety, and resilience to meet current and future water demand. To this end, this subcomponent will support: (a) upgrading, protecting, and treating spring sources to provide 18,000 cubic meters per day of reliable and safe water supply up to 2040; (b) total replacement of the 56-kilometer water distribution network; (c) construction of four new concrete storage tanks; and (d) installation of around 12,378 household connections Component 2. Infrastructure Investments for Safe and Climate Resilient Sanitation and WASH Services in Institutions (US$12.90 million, of which US$12.32 million IBRD). This Component aims to expand access to safe sanitation and inclusive WASH services through investments in sanitation infrastructure such as for fecal sludge management (FSM) and the construction of WASH facilities in schools and public spaces. Subcomponent 2a. Sanitation and WASH in Bohol.This subcomponent will finance the construction of new WASH facilities in 53 public schools, 43 daycare centers, and 69 health centers, some of which also function as evacuation centers during typhoons and other disasters. Related to sanitation, one FSM plant in Bohol is operating but underutilized and another one is planned; their total capacities are sufficient to address the FSM needs of the three most urbanized and populated city/municipalities – Tagbilaran City, Dauis, and Panglao. A TA under Component 4  will be provided to complement this infrastructure. This TA will focus on developing and implementing a regular FSM program requiring massive community and LGU engagement. The TA will also promote proper hygiene practices. Subcomponent 2b. Sanitation and WASH in Siargao, Surigao del NorteThis subcomponent aims to meet the island's FSM needs, given the prevalence of improperly constructed septic tanks and the absence of a sewerage system. The infrastructure and equipment to be constructed will support regular septic tank emptying services throughout Siargao Island. WASH facilities will also be constructed in schools and other institutions.Subcomponent 2c. Sanitation and WASH in Jolo, SuluThis subcomponent will finance the construction of new WASH facilities in 42 public schools, 8 daycare centers, and 3 social welfare centers, some of which also function as evacuation centers during typhoons and other disasters. A TA will also be provided under Component 4 to prepare the municipality for a regular septic tank emptying program and promote proper hygiene behavior and practices.Component 3. Institutional Reforms and Capacity Building (US$13.44 million IBRD). This Component aims to enhance the efficiency and sustainability of WSS services by supporting fundamental institutional reforms and capacity-building of provinces, LGUs, and WSPs. The WSPs will benefit from onsite practical trainings on leadership and governance, technical and commercial operations, financial management, and project management. It also includes project management costs of DILG.Component 4. Project Management and Technical Assistance (US$28.58 million IBRD, of which US$27.58 million IBRD). This Component will support effective project implementation through support to project management and technical assistance, ensuring compliance with World Bank policies while advancing long-term sanitation, water resource management, and financing strategies.Subcomponent 4a Project ManagementThis subcomponent will finance project management costs for the DPWH. Eligible activities will include consulting and non-consulting services, training, and relevant operating expenses required to implement the project following the World Bank policy and guidelines. Subcomponent 4b. Technical Assistance (TA).This subcomponent will finance TA aimed at enhancing sustainable water and sanitation management.  TA under this component will include consulting work that will support, among others, the development of long-term master plans on sanitation and/or sewerage; implementation of pilot sanitation demonstration projects; implementation of an FSM program; conduct of education campaigns on sanitation and hygiene; development and implementation of watershed management programs; comprehensive water resources and energy assessments; and other related work in the project areas. Component 5. Contingent Emergency Response Component (US$0 million). This zero-cost component will allow the GoP to quickly access project funds in response to an eligible disaster, emergency, or crisis. It will enable rapid reallocation of uncommitted project funds to address urgent needs during a natural disaster or crisis (including public health-related emergencies). A declaration of a state of calamity by a qualified national or subnational authority, under applicable laws, could serve as a possible trigger to activate this component.

P510383.project_abstract: The proposed Project is a financial intermediary project that seeks to expand access to finance for activities that promote the bioeconomy, sustainable agricultural practices, restoration of degraded forests and pasture, and water security. The Project will be implemented by the Ministry of Integration and Regional Development (MIDR), through the 3 Regional Development Funds for the North (FDNO), the Northeast (FDNE) and the Center-West (FDCO) regions. Financial products will be channeled through participating public financial institutions, that will act as financial intermediaries in delivering subloans. MIDR will coordinate these efforts, through its 3 superintendencies, capitalizing on the banks’ extensive networks to reach eligible borrowers efficiently.The Development Funds finance strategic private investments aligned with regional priorities and national development objectives, in underserved regions. The Project aims to close the persistent gap between demand for long-term credit and the limited availability of concessional financing. Targeted investments will include bioeconomy value chains, sustainable forest concessions, climate-smart agriculture, and improvements in water supply, sanitation, and irrigation systems.By doing so, the Project also seeks to enhance the economic and climate resilience of targeted beneficiaries (private sector actors, cooperatives, farmers and local entrepreneurs). The proposed project consists of two components: (1) Credit lines for FDNO, FDNE and FDCO to finance blue (WSS and irrigation) and green sectors (forest, Climate-Smart Agriculture, bioeconomy) and (2) Capacity Building and Project Management .

P510631.project_abstract: The Chao Phraya Flood Management Plan 2 (also called Eastern Floodway ) is the largest element of Thailand’s 9-part comprehensive plan for addressing the serious flood challenges in the Chao Phraya basin. Plan 2 will reduce flooding in the lower Chao Phraya basin by creating 800 cubic meters per second (cms) of additional flood routing capacity. It consists of two parts: (a) Expanding the capacity of the existing Chainat-Pasak canal (total length of 132 km) from 130 cms  to 930 cms; and (b) Construction of a new diversion canal (total length of 136 km and design capacity of 600 cms) from the Pasak River to the Gulf of Thailand. The total cost of Plan 2 program is US$4.3 billion, and it will be financed jointly by the Govt of Thailand and a consortium of development partners led by the World Bank. The Plan 2 program will be implemented in two phases, and the first phase will have the following components:  Component 1: Climate-resilient flood management infrastructure (total cost US$1,050 million, IBRD financing US$578 million). This Component will finance physical works, associated control structures and systems, and other activities for increasing the flood drainage capacity in the eastern Chao Phraya basin. Specifically, it will include:i.	Capacity-expansion  in the construction-ready sections of the existing Chainat-Pasak canal, to increase its capacity by 800 m3/s; ii.	Construction of selected sections of the new Pasak-Gulf of Thailand canal, with a design capacity of 600 m3/s; and iii.	Construction and installation of associated control structures and operating systems for the parallel canal system.Component 2: Strengthened capacity for climate-smart water and flood management (US$20 million, IBRD financing US$13 million). This component will aim to strengthen the capacity of RID and associated institutions in climate-resilient water management, focusing on sustainability, robustness, and long-term effectiveness that go beyond the project’s spatial and temporal scope. Among other things, it will finance: (i) the modernization and expansion of hydromet and telemetry networks; (ii) the upgrading/development of flood forecasting models; (iii) the development of a comprehensive Decision-Support System (DSS) for network operations; and (iv) the development of an asset management system and a digital operation and maintenance (O&M) planning platform. The component will also support interventions to strengthen institutional coordination in the water and emergency management space.Component 3: Project Management (US$30 million, IBRD financing US$19 million). This component will support project management and implementation via: (i) technical, environmental, and social supervision; (ii) project audits and M&E; (iii) recruitment of a Project Management Consultancy (PMC) to support implementation, M&E, FM, environmental and social (E&S) safeguards, and procurement; and (iv) establishing a Grievance Mechanism.

P510667.project_abstract: The Pakistan Finance for Lowering Emissions in Water Sector (Pak-FLOW) is strategically aligned with Pakistan's Country Partnership Framework (CPF), addressing key objectives across multiple outcomes to foster sustainable development and climate resilience. Specifically, it fulfills the objectives of CPF Outcome 4: Increased Decarbonization, and supported by the World Bank’s Transformative Carbon Asset Facility (TCAF).The Pak-FLOW project will support decarbonization through reducing methane emissions from wastewater treatment processes by converting the biogas to energy, promote energy efficiency measures in water pumping and the supply of clean renewable energy from solar panels. The project incorporates a robust Monitoring, Reporting, and Verification (MRV) system to ensure integrity and continuous improvement of envisaged operations, ensuring its long-term sustainability.
P510667.regionname: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan, and Pakistan

P510690.project_abstract: The proposed project takes an integrated approach to disaster risk management, supporting activities across the four thematic areas of urban flood risk management, hydromet and early warning systems, disaster risk financing and insurance, and mainstreaming disaster risk management (DRM) in development and investment planning. Component 1 will finance flood protection infrastructure investments in urban areas across three provinces and technical studies for informing future flood risk management in urban areas across two additional provinces. Component 2 will finance improvements in delivery of hydrometeorological services and pilot solutions for last-mile early warning dissemination. Component 3 will strengthen financial resilience to natural disasters by financing insurance premium to facilitate continued access to sovereign risk insurance and related technical assistance activities. Component 4 will include overall project management and coordination and activities for mainstreaming DRM considerations in development and investment planning.

P510691.project_abstract: This operation will promote forest restoration in the Arc of Restoration in Brazil with a focus on the Tocantins-Araguaia basin, a key transition area between the Cerrado and the Amazon located in the states of Para, Tocantins, Maranhão and Mato Grosso. The operation is the core of the NPC Brazil Investment Plan, which central objective is to promote forest restoration in areas of the Tocantins-Araguaia Basin and consists in providing the concessional resources to the BNDES, which in turn will apply them into its credit lines related to forest restoration in the target territory.The direct beneficiaries of the project are private companies in the forest restoration sector borrowing resources to finance their respective business projects in the Brazilian restoration chain. Project financing will be mostly invested in the restoration of areas characterized mainly by low-productivity pastures and agriculture in private rural properties. The financing of private companies will contribute to mobilize large-scale capital and to strengthen the nascent forest restoration industry.Investments in agroforestry systems and forest integration models, which incorporate high-value crops, have become increasingly attractive for agricultural producers of various scales, encouraging the adoption of sustainable practices that contribute to the recovery of degraded areas and increased agricultural productivity.  In this evolving landscape, the Project holds a huge potential for engaging small-scale farmers and traditional producers from anchor companies and for strengthening local communities, making them more resilient to climate change, increasing the provision of key eco-system services, generating global public goods.Furthermore, in order to contribute to a just climate transition with gender equality and social inclusion – a core principle of the NPC-BR Investment Program – a survey of data and information on the gaps and inequalities of gender, race and social inclusion in the intervention region shall be carried out and subborrowers shall conduct meaningful consultations with local stakeholders at the time of elaboration of forest restoration subprojects.Finally, it is worth to highlight that – as part of the NPC-BR Investment Plan – project financed resources will be complemented by the supplemental Dedicated Grant Mechanism (DGM/NPC), which will solely benefit Indigenous Peoples and traditional communities within the Tocantins-Araguaia basin.

P510696.project_abstract: The project aims to support the development of strategic infrastructure and logistics services in the Province of Salta through four main components: (1) Investment in resilient and safe road infrastructure;(2) Development of logistics centers to serve lithium and other productive sector firms;(3) Investment in critical water and wastewater treatment infrastructure for the local communities; (4) Support to the mining sector by strengthening sustainability, oversight, training, and transparency in mining operations.In addition, the project includes a CERC component and a component focused on Project Management.

P510697.project_abstract: This hybrid operation—combining Program-for-Results (PforR) and Investment Project Financing (IPF)—supports the National Strategic Investment Framework for Sustainable Landscape Management (ESIF 2, 2024–2038) by advancing long-term landscape restoration. It aims to: (i) expand sustainable land management in degraded Highland Areas; (ii) sustain and expand certification, registration, and digitalization of rural land rights in Ethiopia’s highlands, including communal forests and grazing lands; and (iii) raise farm incomes and create jobs by strengthening agribusiness value-chain linkages. Primary beneficiaries are smallholder farmers in program watersheds. The program will increase smallholders’ livelihood security and broaden income earning opportunities. Efforts will be made for the equitable inclusion of all beneficiaries in Community Watershed Users’ Cooperative Societies (CWUCS) including vulnerable groups such as landless, youth, elderly, and female-headed households.The PforR component will finance watershed-level sustainable land and natural resource management, strengthen rural land administration, improve tenure security for private and communal lands (including selected forests and grazing areas), and catalyze private-sector participation to ensure sustainability. Under Result Area 1 (Participatory Watershed and Forest Management), Disbursement-Linked Indicators (DLIs) incentivize scaling up restoration and market linkages by: (1) registering Community Watershed Users’ Cooperative Societies (CWUCS) with participatory, approved Watershed Management Plans; (2) expanding the area under sustainable landscape management; (3) establishing Participatory Forest Management with legally recognized cooperatives and approved Forest Management Plans; (4) treating acid soils; (5) integrating cooperatives into existing and new value chains within program catchments; and (6) verifying non forest emission reductions. Under Result Area 2 (Rural Land Administration), DLIs focus on expanding and digitizing secure tenure by: (1) issuing Second Level Land Holding Certificates (SLLCs) for individual parcels; (2) issuing SLLCs for communal forests and grazing lands; (3) operationalizing the National Rural Land Administration Information System in program woredas with digitized SLLCs; and (4) increasing connectivity of land administration offices and systems to national digital infrastructure. The IPF component—financed by IDA, SCALE, Nordic Development Fund (NDF), and ENABLE—will provide cross-cutting technical assistance and capacity building across both result areas and the broader government program (ESIF 2). It will support: (i) agribusiness development by linking landholders to agribusinesses; (ii) design and implementation of payment for ecosystem services (PES) schemes; (iii) access to carbon markets; and (iv) Operationalize pilots to promote voluntary land consolidation in Ethiopia’s highlands. These pilots involve baseline surveys, voluntary land exchange, and design, testing, and documentation of a computerized platform in real-world conditions. The likelihood and magnitude of risk for the voluntary exchange program pilot have been assessed. The voluntary land exchange will take place in 12 designated kebeles, involving small parcels of land and following a national legal proclamation.Targeted TA will also promote inclusive alternative livelihoods for women and youth through group-based training and livelihood grants; strengthen community capacity to scale ecosystem conservation and carbon sequestration via participatory forest and watershed management; advance land tenure and property rights awareness; and establish community-based performance monitoring. In addition, the operation will enhance MoA and Ethiopia Forest Development (EFD) operational capacity for CALM 2, including: (i) environmental and social risk management; (ii) procurement and financial management; and (iii) monitoring, reporting, and verification, including advanced remote sensing. IFC will complement implementation with integrated upstream and downstream support for agribusiness linkages, value-chain development, and access to finance, strengthening market access and supply chains through its portfolio, pipeline, and early-stage leads. IFC’s approach will promote business models that directly engage smallholder farmers and local communities, with close IFC– World Bank collaboration to ensure coordinated delivery.

P511300.project_abstract: The Uganda Cities and Municipalities Infrastructure Development (UCMID) Program aims to enhance the institutional performance of urban local governments and relevant ministries for urban planning and management. It seeks to increase access to climate-smart infrastructure, services, and local economic opportunities, benefiting both urban residents and displaced populations in refugee-hosting areas. The program will address challenges such as rapid urbanization, inadequate transport infrastructure, lack of integrated urban planning, limited technical and financial resources, climate change impacts, and issues in refugee host districts. It aligns with Uganda’s 2025–2035 Country Partnership Framework, building on lessons from the Uganda Support to Municipal Infrastructure Development (USMID) to strengthen urban service delivery. UCMID is a follow-on operation to the USMID and USMID-AF programs aiming to build on the progress and successes under both initiatives. UCMID is urban and nationwide and will be implemented in two Ministries Departments and Agencies (Ministry of Lands, Housing and Urban Development – Coordinating Ministry and Ministry of Local Government), 10 secondary cities (Jinja, Mbarara, Gulu, Arua, Masaka, Hoima, Mbale, Soroti, Lira, Fort Portal), 11 municipalities (Kabale, Moroto, Tororo, Kitgum, Kasese, Kamuli, Mubende, Busia, Ntungamo, Apac, Lugazi, Entebbe) supported under the previous USMID Program, 15 new municipalities (Masindi, Rukungiri, Bushenyi-Ishaka, Mityana, Kumi, Nebbi, Koboko, Kisoro, Kapchorwa, Ibanda, Iganga, Njeru, Bugiri, Sheema, Kotido), and 11 districts hosting refugees (Arua , Isingiro, Kamwenge, Kiryandongo, Lamwo, Maddi-Okollo, Moyo, Obongi, Terego, Yumbe). The program has two main components: Component 1 (PforR) focuses on urban development performance and investments, financing resilient infrastructure and services, and promoting peaceful coexistence in refugee-hosting areas. This component seeks to achieve results in three Result Areas (RAs): (i) RA1: Enhanced local and national level government institutional capacity and performance for sustainable urban management, (ii) RA2: Increased access to climate smart urban infrastructure, services and local economic development, and (iii) RA3: Integrated climate smart infrastructure, services and local economic development for the host communities and refugees.  The IPF component is designed to directly support the achievement of the Program Development Objectives and Disbursement-Linked Indicators (DLIs) by financing upstream activities that enable effective implementation and results delivery under the PforR. It will focus on strengthening national systems for urban development, program coordination, and technical capacity at both central and local government levels. Specifically, the IPF will finance: (i) overall program management specified in the Program Operations Manual (POM), including operational coordination, monitoring and evaluation, ESHS risk management, enforcement and supervision, and the engagement of an Independent Verification Agent (IVA) to conduct the Annual Performance Assessments (APA) and verify results against the DLI matrix; (ii) the development and rollout of key policy, regulatory, and planning tools, such as the urban data platforms, GIS-based systems and digital innovations, and national frameworks for climate-smart and inclusive urban infrastructure; and (iii) technical assistance and targeted capacity building for national and local institutions in areas such as fiduciary systems, land use management, occupational health and safety (OSH), community health and safety, labor and working conditions and climate-resilient planning. The IPF will also support the conceptualization and piloting of a corridor-based approach to urban investment planning. This includes the development of national corridor design guidelines and investment packaging tools that will help structure integrated infrastructure projects—linking roads, drainage, markets, and utility networks—along strategic urban corridors. The IPF component will dedicate US20 million to support ESHS systems improvements and capacity strengthening of key MDAs (MLHUD, MGLSD, NEMA), implementing entities, and Contractors. Activities will include (I) Professionalization and  accreditation of OHS professionals through the Learning Centre on E&S Standards at Makerere University, (II) upgrading and integration of OHS and Labor Management Information Systems to enhance incident management, reporting and compensation, (III) development of socio-economic assessments, (IV) development of  land acquisition protocols and/or resettlement plans— including livelihood restoration; and (V) finance land acquisition and resettlement expenditures; (VI) develop a communication and stakeholder engagement strategy for the Program; and (VII) strengthening  grievance management  systems including worker GRMs.UCMID will also benefit from an Enhanced Implementation Support and Monitoring (EISM) firm hired directly by the World Bank to support the Project Implementation Support Team (PIST) and the World Bank following the enactment of the Anti-Homosexuality Act 2023 in Uganda. The firm will work with NGO/CSOs and country-based development partners since knowledge of the country context and culture are key considerations to be taken onto account. The EISM will: (i) establish an effective and confidential mechanism to receive, manage, refer, and monitor grievances related to discrimination of Sexual and Gender Minorities (SGM). (ii) support outreach and sensitization to Program beneficiaries and communities. (iii) provide capacity strengthening and technical support to the PIST and (iv) regularly monitor and evaluate the implementation of measures to mitigate the risk of discrimination in all WB’s financed operations in Uganda including UCMID.

P511417.project_abstract: Yemen faces severe fragility and conflict compounded by climate shocks, widespread poverty, and collapsing services, creating one of the world’s worst humanitarian crises. Since 2015, real GDP has fallen by 43 percent and nominal GDP per capita by 70 percent, with further declines projected for 2025. About 75 percent of Yemenis live in poverty, food insecurity affects over 60 percent of households, and malnutrition rates among women and children are among the highest globally. Infrastructure and governance have largely collapsed, with two rival authorities deepening economic and institutional fragmentation. Gender inequality remains extreme, and Yemen ranks near the bottom of global development indices. Recovery requires shifting from short-term humanitarian aid to a comprehensive development agenda focused on nutrition security, climate resilience, and institutional capacity to break the cycle of fragility and aid dependence.Agriculture is a cornerstone of Yemen’s economy, contributing nearly 29 percent of GDP and employing a similar share of the workforce, while supporting food security and rural livelihoods. However, the sector faces severe challenges, including water scarcity, reliance on rainfed farming, fragmented value chains, and poor infrastructure, compounded by insecurity and institutional fragility. Most jobs are concentrated in low-productivity rural areas, limiting growth potential. Despite these constraints, examples like the NANA dairy factory in Aden demonstrate agribusiness’s transformative potential to create jobs, enhance productivity, and link on-farm and off-farm activities.Yemen’s agrifood sector offers significant opportunities for recovery through targeted investments in high-potential value chains such as livestock, dairy, poultry, coffee, horticulture, and fisheries. These value chains combine strong domestic demand, export potential, and inclusive employment generation, particularly for women and youth. Strategic interventions—expanding access to finance, improving infrastructure, fostering public-private partnerships, and strengthening producer institutions—can unlock private investment and build resilience against economic and climate shocks. Women play a vital role in agriculture, yet face systemic barriers including limited land rights, lack of access to finance and technology, and restrictive norms. Gender-sensitive approaches and climate-smart technologies are essential to close productivity gaps and empower women as agents of change.Climate change poses severe risks to Yemen’s agriculture, with rising temperatures, erratic rainfall, and extreme weather threatening productivity, especially in livestock systems. Agriculture accounts for over 60 percent of national GHG emissions, with dairy alone contributing 29 percent. The proposed program aligns with Yemen’s climate and development priorities under the Paris Agreement, bundling interventions to enhance climate resilience, reduce emissions, and strengthen institutional capacity. Components focus on improving on-farm productivity, integrating value chains, and expanding access to finance, while promoting energy efficiency and food safety. Phase 1 targets the dairy sector, aiming to boost productivity through breed improvement, animal health services, and good husbandry practices, while addressing gender disparities and empowering women in livestock management.The Yemen Agribusiness Program is a joint World Bank Group initiative—led by IDA and IFC—designed to transform Yemen’s agrifood sector, strengthen food security, and promote inclusive economic recovery in a fragile context. Using a phased Multiphase Programmatic Approach (MPA), the program will begin with targeted investments in the dairy sector and later expand to poultry, horticulture, and grains. IDA will focus on public investments in infrastructure, institutional capacity, and policy reforms, while IFC will mobilize private capital, pilot commercially viable business models, and foster partnerships with anchor firms and financial institutions. The program aims to boost productivity, improve market access, and build climate resilience, while empowering women and youth and reducing import dependence.Targeted interventions will strengthen priority value chains through breed improvement, nutrition and health services for livestock, sustainable crop practices, and modern infrastructure such as cold chains and processing facilities. Producer institutions and MSMEs will be supported to adopt food safety standards, access markets, and engage in collective marketing and branding. IFC will complement these efforts by delivering advisory support to local firms, promoting inclusive supply chains, enabling access to finance, and facilitating food safety certification. Over five years, IFC plans to engage 8–10 private firms and SME banks, establish strategic partnerships with processors like HSA and Nana, and scale climate-smart, gender-inclusive practices. Together, these efforts aim to catalyze private investment, create jobs, and build resilience across Yemen’s agribusiness landscape.The project will focus on smallholder dairy producers in governorates within a 2–3 hour radius of Aden—such as Aden, Lahj, Abyan, southern Taiz, and Al-Dalea—to ensure milk quality and minimize spoilage during transport to a planned processing facility. Beneficiaries include approximately 10,000 farming households, farmer producer organizations, and MSMEs, alongside indirect actors in input supply, veterinary services, and logistics. The design adopts a farmer-centric approach to improve service delivery, strengthen market access through village-level institutions, and engage private sector agribusinesses—including IFC-supported enterprises—in aggregation, processing, and distribution systems.To address structural constraints in Yemen’s dairy sector—low productivity, fragmented markets, and weak private engagement—the project will implement a sequenced strategy: restoring farm-level capacity, improving quality and collection logistics, and enabling private investment. Public investment will catalyze technology transfer and productivity gains, complemented by IFC partnerships with local processors to expand processing capacity and shift sourcing from imported skim milk powder to fresh milk. Interventions will include breed improvement, animal health services, and adoption of Good Animal Husbandry Practices, alongside investments in cold chain systems and farmer training. Gender disparities will be addressed through inclusive approaches that empower women in production and market participation.The project comprises three components: (i) Productive and Climate-Smart Agriculture Support, focusing on breed improvement, balanced nutrition, and veterinary services to boost milk yields and reduce emissions; (ii) Strengthening Market Access and Value Chain Integration, enhancing milk collection, bulking, and food safety through climate-resilient infrastructure and partnerships; and (iii) Facilitating Access to Financial Services, enabling farmers and MSMEs to invest in productivity-enhancing technologies via tailored financial products and literacy programs. IFC will complement these efforts through value chain assessments, policy reviews, capacity building, and investment facilitation. Together, these interventions aim to increase milk production, improve quality, strengthen resilience, and create inclusive economic opportunities across Yemen’s dairy value chain.
P511417.regionname: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan, and Pakistan

P511426.project_abstract: The proposed Project will provide ancillary services to enhance the transmission system flexibility and enable higher integration of new wind and solar generation and will therefore enhance Morocco’s energy security through more reliance on domestic energy resources.The proposed Project will finance the preparation, construction, and commissioning of the Ifahsa PHS plant with an expected capacity of 300 MW. The project is located 14 km from Chefchaouen in northern Morocco, near the Laou valley and south of Tetouan, the Ifahsa PHS plant will feature two reversible pump-turbine units of 150 MW each, operating with a maximum flow of 44 m³/s in turbine mode and 37 m³/s in pumping mode. During off-peak hours, the plant will use dispatched renewable electricity from the grid to pump water from the lower to the upper reservoir. During peak hours, it will generate electricity to meet the peak demand in the Morocco’s grid system, by conveying the stored water from the upper to the lower reservoir through hydraulic turbines. the PHS plant will be connected by 400 kV transmission to the national grid.
P511426.regionname: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan, and Pakistan

P511489.project_abstract: This project is a pilot to unlock tourism development at the regional level through multi-sector interventions. The project is designed to address the multifaceted challenges and opportunities for tourism in Arequipa through coordinated multisectoral investments aiming at identified gaps in connectivity, public services and tourism amenities and sites. The project recognizes that effective tourism development requires a holistic approach that integrates infrastructure improvements, cultural heritage preservation, and effective destination management and promotion. The project focuses on developing tourism routes between the city of Arequipa and the Colca Valley (in the Arequipa region) through the following four complementary components:Component 1. Improving the quality of selected tourist attractions : 1.1) Develop and improve services in targeted sites and attractions and 1.2) Rehabilitate cultural sites Component 2. Upgrading roads and investing in water, sanitation and health facilities focusing on : 2.1) Upgrading the quality of key roads along the identified tourism routes, and 2.2) Improving the water and sewage systems in towns on the identified tourist routes 2.3) construction of a health center and forest rehabilitation.Component 3. Institutional strengthening. Component 4. Project implementation.

P511492.project_abstract: With the DJIBOUTI GROUNDWATER RESILIENCE AND WATER SUPPLY PROJECT (P511492) Djibouti will be joining as phase II the MPA Horn of Africa - Groundwater for Resilience Project (P512774), which started with Kenya, Ethiopia, Somalia, and the Intergovernmental Authority on Development (IGAD). This regional project aims to increase sustainable access and management of groundwater resources in the Horn of Africa's borderlands, addressing urgent water supply needs and fostering transboundary cooperation.The project’s development objective is to increase the sustainable access and management of groundwater in rural areas of Djibouti through investments that pair infrastructure with robust operations and maintenance (O&M) arrangements. The project comprises four components: (i) delivering inclusive groundwater services to priority areas via rehabilitation and construction of potable water systems, complemented by fit-for-purpose O&M models (such as operator contracts, service-level agreements, and community-based maintenance with clear accountability and financing mechanisms); (ii) generating groundwater information and strengthening regional and national institutions, including improved hydrogeological mapping, monitoring networks, data systems, and capacity building for policy, regulation, and asset management; (iii) project management, monitoring and evaluation (M&E), knowledge management, and learning; and (iv) a Contingent Emergency Response Component (CERC) to enable rapid response to eligible crises and emergencies.Expected outcomes include improved and reliably maintained groundwater access for communities, strengthened regional and national groundwater management institutions capable of overseeing asset management and O&M, professionalized operation and maintenance of rural water supply to reduce downtime and extend asset life, enhanced cooperation among participating countries through IGAD-facilitated platforms and knowledge exchange, and increased resilience to water scarcity driven by climate variability and population growth.
P511492.regionname: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan, and Pakistan

P511557.project_abstract: The Project will constitute the Phase 1 of the Multiphase Programmatic Approach (MPA) ‘Integrated Water Security Program for the Northeast of Brazil’.  Most of the region’s nine states face similar constraints, including limited water availability, recurrent droughts and floods, fragmented institutions, and underdeveloped infrastructure and management tools— further shaped by transboundary watersheds and aquifers, as well as shared hydraulic infrastructure. These commonalities present a valuable opportunity for horizontal learning and collaboration, enabling states to exchange knowledge and build on each other’s innovations and good practices. Several states have already developed effective solutions that are highly relevant and replicable across the region. The MPA will facilitate structured knowledge exchange and coordination across the states, helping reduce negative externalities and enhance the collective impact of water security investments. About 7.3 percent of the Northeast Region’s population lives in the State of Paraiba; within Paraiba, 77.6 are urban residents and 22.4 percent live in rural areas (IBGE, 2022). The state's water security challenges and socioeconomic landscape present a great opportunity to test and refine the Program's strategies. Phase 1 of the MPA comprises activities prioritized by the State Government aimed at improving water security across Paraiba, strengthening key sector water institutions such as the State Water Resources Management Executive Agency (Agencia Executiva de Gestão das Águas, AESA), the State Water and Sanitation Company (Companhia de Água e Esgotos da Paraiba, CAGEPA), and a newly created Executive Management Unit for Rural Sanitation (Gerencia Executiva de Saneamento Rural, GESR), and constructing essential infrastructure such as a wastewater treatment plant (WWTP), the sectorization of the water supply distribution system to reduce non-revenue water (NRW), and the construction of a water transmission main for the Gramame system, all serving the RMJP. The Project builds on lessons from the ongoing Paraiba Improving Water Resources Management and Services Provision Project (Projeto de Seguranca Hidrica or PSH by its acronym in Portuguese),  approved in 2019. PSH includes construction of water mains to transfer water from the São Francisco River’s Integration Project to 27 municipalities in the regions in Agreste and Borborema, automation and partial sectorization of RMJP's water network to reduce NRW, improvements in wastewater collection for energy efficiency, and institutional strengthening of CAGEPA. . The Project will include the following components:Component 1. Foundations for Water Security. This component aims to contribute to proactive WRM in the state by strengthening the state WRM institution (AESA) and consolidating key management instruments related to water quantity, quality, and drought resilience. It will focus on expanding surface and groundwater monitoring, fostering dialogue on the costs and financial sustainability of the water management system, improving water licenses and charges, and establishing water quality standards through a participatory process with society, along with the necessary actions to achieve those standards. The component will also focus on increasing the state’s capacity for drought management through increased monitoring and warning mechanisms for implementation of adequate measures, and the proposition of a state water reuse policy.Component 2. Resilient Water Services. This component is designed to advance the modernization and expansion of WSS, supporting the state water utility (CAGEPA), the state regulatory agency (ARPB), and a recently created unit that will lead the development of a strategy and plan to support rural water supply and sanitation in the state, the Gerencia Executiva de Saneamento Rural (GESR). It also includes the construction and equipment of water infrastructure that will enhance the quality of life for residents, improve environmental outcomes, and promote economic growth in the metropolitan region of Joao Pessoa. It includes the following subcomponents: (2.1) Expansion of the water supply system in RJMP, this subcomponent will fund the construction of a transmission main connected to the Gramame System; the water supply system includes three existing dams, two of them interconnected and classified as large dams, Gramame and Mamuaba dams; (2.2) Reduction of water losses in RJMP, this subcomponent involves constructing and optimizing selected water supply network sectors to reduce physical water losses in the CAGEPA’s distribution network in the RMJP; (2.3) Expansion and modernization of the WWTP in RMJP, this subcomponent comprises civil works and acquisition of equipment to improve and expand wastewater treatment services in the RMJP under a design-and-build contract; (2.4) Support to the Regulatory Agency of Paraiba State, this subcomponent will fund technical assistance and training to strengthen ARPB’s institutional capacity and regulatory effectiveness; and (2.5) Rural Sanitation, which will provide technical assistance to SEIRH for the improvement of management models for the operation and maintenance of WSS services in the state. Component 3. Partnership for Water Security and Project Management. This component will fund knowledge sharing and learning activities between the Borrower and other States in areas of interest for dissemination of best practices including, inter alia, institutional capacity for WRM, data use and information systems, sustainable management models for rural WSS, and NRW control solutions. The component will also provide support to strengthening the technical and administrative capacity of the PMU with respect to technical, financial management, audit, procurement, monitoring and evaluation, social and environmental, and communication and information technology activities, including through the hiring of consultants. Activities will include the provision of independent audit services.

P511605.project_abstract: Sri Lanka faces significant spatial disparities that hinder equitable and balanced growth opportunities, particularly in the Northern and Eastern provinces. These provinces struggle with low GDP contributions (only 4.1% and 5.1%, respectively) and high poverty rates (23.8% and 18.6%) compared to the national average of 14.3%. Unemployment rates in districts like Jaffna, Kilinochchi, Ampara, and Batticaloa exceed 7%, well above the national average of 4.7%. Despite the strong potential of key productive sectors such as tourism, fisheries, and agriculture, resources and assets remain largely underutilized. This is compounded by critical gaps in enabling infrastructure—including weak connectivity, inadequate water supply, poor solid waste management, and limited productive facilities—as well as the absence of comprehensive regional development strategies and insufficient support for priority economic value chains. To address these challenges, the proposed operation adopts a programmatic and phased approach, with an emphasis on spatially targeted investments in selected clusters. Project interventions comprise a set of inter-linked components and activities, aimed at: (i) improving regional economic planning, while building institutional capacity for sustained development; (ii) financing catalytic public infrastructure investments with a view to attract private investments in the selected clusters; and (iii) supporting local enterprises in the selected clusters through business development and skills enhancement to strengthen the value chains and stimulate private sector.The project comprises two main components: Component 1 will establish the strategic foundation for long-term regional development by financing technical assistance, consultancy services, goods, and equipment to strengthen regional planning capacity and guide long-term economic development in the Northern and Eastern Provinces. The component will enhance the institutional capacity of national and provincial entities to manage integrated, climate-resilient, and private-sector-responsive development. Component 2 will finance civil works, consultancy services, feasibility studies, detailed designs, management and supervision of works for infrastructure investments in five priority clusters, with the aim of activating the private sector and promoting economic growth.  Investments will improve tourism and fisheries infrastructure, enhance basic urban services, and upgrade public spaces to strengthen the competitiveness and livability of key economic areas. The five priority clusters supported under Component 2 are: (i) Jaffna Fort Revival, focusing on heritage-led urban regeneration through site restoration, public space upgrades, and complementary tourism amenities; (ii) Guru Nagar Fisheries Town & Culinary Hub, enhancing landing, market, and waste management facilities while creating new tourism and culinary livelihood opportunities; (iii) Cod Bay Harbor, upgrading onshore fisheries infrastructure within the existing harbor footprint to improve quality standards, reduce post-harvest losses, and increase value addition; (iv) Passikudah Tourism Development, improving beach access, visitor facilities, waste management, and local retail spaces to support sustainable tourism and extend visitor stays; and (v) Arugam Bay Tourism Corridor, upgrading tourism infrastructure, service delivery, and public spaces along the beachfront and road corridor to diversify activities, strengthen sustainability, and promote inclusive local economic development.

P511719.project_abstract: The project will focus on the irrigation sub-sector. It aims at supporting irrigated agriculture in public irrigation schemes, through: (i) strengthened performance of the operator of the irrigation service; (ii) modernized irrigation infrastructure, reducing losses and ensuring more flexible access to water; and (iii) improved agricultural value addition, productivity and market access.
P511719.regionname: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan, and Pakistan

P512214.project_abstract: Kazakhstan and Uzbekistan are participating in Phase 3 of the regional Multiphase Programmatic Approach (MPA) program titled “One Health for Pandemic Prevention, Food Systems Resilience, and Ecosystem Health in Central Asia (P181459)”, as a recipient of the multi-country Pandemic Fund (PF) grant. On May 27, 2025, the World Bank’s Board of Executive Directors approved $60 million in financing to support the first phase of the MPA. The financing package of the first phase includes a $30 million concessional credit for the Kyrgyz Republic and a $30 million grant for Tajikistan to help strengthen their capacity to prevent, detect, and respond to priority health risks affecting both humans and animals. Additionally, as part of the first phase Tajikistan and Kyrgyz Republic received a $1.79 million grant from the Pandemic Fund to respond to the relevant challenges. The second phase of the program includes Turkmenistan that also received $1.79 million grant from the Pandemic Fund. Overall, the three-phase program will be implemented from 2025 to 2032 by regional and national agencies across the health, agriculture, and environment sectors in the five countries. The program development objective (PrDO) is to strengthen the capacity to prevent, detect, and respond to priority zoonotic diseases, antimicrobial resistance, and food safety issues in Central Asia through a regional One Health approach.The PF grant aims to help Kazakhstan and Uzbekistan strengthen their institutional and technical foundations for adopting a regional One Health approach. The grant will support activities that address gaps in the countries’ core capacities, focusing on establishing effective governance mechanisms and procuring targeted equipment and consumables.In Kazakhstan, the World Bank will support the implementation of activities under Components 1, 2, 3, and 4.
