In Uzbekistan, the World Bank will finance activities under Components 1, 3, and 4, while Component 2 (Knowledge and Workforce Capacity Development) will be implemented through the portion of the multi-country PF grant managed by the FAO and WHO.

P512407.project_abstract: Blue Economy has become an important engine of economic growth for Viet Nam, with its coastal provinces accounting for close to 60 percent of the national GDP. Protecting marine resources is a core priority in the 2018 Strategy for Sustainable Development of Viet Nam’s Marine Economy by 2030 and the vision for 2045 (Marine Strategy). The Marine Strategy further prioritizes ensuring prosperity, safety, and resilience for coastal communities, where a large portion of the population resides. Improved fisheries governance, recovery of fish stock, and higher fish food quality are key priorities. To achieve goals of protecting resources and safeguarding livelihoods of small-scale fishers, the adoption of related fisheries legislation emphasizes co-management in nearshore fisheries. Broader sector development directions include increasing aquaculture, decreasing capture, improving value-addition through investments in smart infrastructure, and ensuring sustainability, including though the development of modern “Blue Ports”, and continued fishing fleet rationalization. While resources and habitats are under co-management and showing improvement, development disturbances and climate change are expected to further impact capture fisheries and aquaculture substantially.The proposed Sustainable Fisheries Development work (SFDP) will support the Government of Viet Nam in advancing the implementation of key aspects of the country's Fisheries Law and the Marine Strategy. More specifically, by drawing on core fisheries sustainability pillars, the SFDP will support the newly-formed Ministry of Agriculture and Environment (MAE) and selected provinces in strengthening fisheries governance systems and undertaking strategic investments, particularly in climate-resilient smart infrastructure for protecting and improving production capacities and supply chains, as well as for scaling-up adaptation in low-laying coastal areas through support for polyculture adoption. The SFDP is further informed by the strategic development directions in the fisheries sector outlined in Viet Nam’s Socio-Economic Development Strategy (SEDS) 2021-2030 and Plan (SEDP) 2021-2025, whereby marine aquaculture and capture fisheries are recognized as priority sectors in the country’s economic development, providing significant contribution to the economy.To this end, the SFDP will focus on assisting Viet Nam to meet priority needs in developing modern “Blue Ports” and storm shelters to strengthen climate resilience, improve value addition, and strengthen port state control to meet market access and traceability requirements for sustainable fisheries management. Targeted infrastructure support to key fishing ports and vessel shelters under both central and provincial management is needed, given their importance in fisheries governance particularly for port state control of capture fisheries. Aquaculture support will further focus on strengthening coastal adaptation in conjunction with introducing poli-culture practices to expand sustainable production of Viet Nam’s key export shrimp commodity. Enabling emerging high-value mariculture commodities, such as crustaceans and mollusks will also be considered.The SFDP investments directed at protecting marine fishery resources are expected to contribute, in the long run, to mitigating biodiversity loss and diminishing ecosystem services while supporting livelihoods, food, and nutrition security, all of which are inherently linked and contribute to the foundations of a livable Planet Earth. Thus, the SFDP will help Viet Nam to grow its marine economy without depleting its natural capital and while benefiting multiple fishery communities.The investments under MAE component involving the construction/upgrading of grade I and regional fishing ports and storm shelters include: (a) wharves for vessels and logistic services, with lengths varying from 122 to 250 meters; port sections embankments of hundreds of meters long; dredging of access channels and anchoring waters with areas ranging from a few to tens of hectares; and dredged material volumes from a few thousand to tens of thousands of cubic meters. Except for the one subproject on upgrading Tam Quan storm shelter, the excavated materials is about 500,000 m3; (b) technical infrastructure, such as internal roads; electricity, water supply, drainage and wastewater treatment systems; signaling and communication systems, etc.; and (c) logistic infrastructures including warehouses for fishery classification, storage, and processing; operational houses, etc. The An Giang, Can Tho City, Thanh Hoa and Ca Mau provincial subprojects encompass investments in upgrading aquaculture infrastructure and, for Thanh Hoa and Ca Mau, provincial fishery port and storm shelter rehabilitation and upgrades. Specifically, for An Giang Province, these physical investments include: 23 sluices with widths ranging from 8-10 meters to serve rice and aquaculture farming; upgrading of transport roads to nine meters wide with a total length of 7 km; construction of low voltage and medium power lines, and 30 small transformer stations to operate the sluices. Support for An Giang also introduces and scales up innovative aquaculture practices in rice-shrimp and mangrove-shrimp/crab farming for resilient livelihoods. It includes training, linking the private sector for aquaculture products, marketing, support for origin tracking, and certification to meet VietGAP, SRP, and ASC standards, as well as GHG emission reduction measurement.For Can Tho City, these physical investments include upgrading rural roads and bridges with a total length of about 73.7 km and widths of 7.5 meters; dredging canals to provide better water supply and discharge, and to backfill the project rural roads along these canals. The total length of dredged canals is about 305 km, with excavated materials amounting to 2.9 million cubic meters. It is noted that although the total volume of Soc Trang investments is large, they are small-scale investments scattered across a large rural area of four districts in Can Tho City. The dredged canals are intra-field, and analysis showed that the dredged materials are non-contaminated. Support for Can Tho city also includes support to introduce and scale up innovative aquaculture practices for resilient livelihoods, and support to ethnic minority communes for the construction of family latrines to improve sanitation and the environment in aquaculture areas.For Thanh Hoa Province, physical investments will support rehabilitation and upgrading of provincial ports and storm shelter, including: (a) wharves for vessels and logistics services, with estimated combined length of 390 meters; port sections embankments; and dredging of access channels;  (b) technical infrastructure such as internal roads; electricity, water supply, drainage and wastewater treatment systems; signaling and communication systems, etc.; and (c) logistics infrastructure including warehouses for fishery classification, storage, and processing; operational houses, etc. Physical investments will also include upgrading access roads with a total length of about 6 km, construction of culverts, strengthening embankments and dredging canals to provide better water supply and discharge, and backfilling project rural roads along these canals. The total length of dredged canals is about 14.2 km. The dredged canals are intra-field, and analysis has shown that the dredged materials are non-contaminated. These investments will be spread across nine Thanh Hoa communes.Support for Ca Mau Province involves rehabilitation and upgrading of one provincial port, including: (a) pier expansion to 150 meters; (b) dredging of access channels and anchoring waters, and (c) logistics infrastructure including warehouses for fishery classification, storage, and processing; operational houses, etc.. Physical investments will also include upgrading rural roads with a total length of about 76.7 km

P512645.project_abstract: The proposed US$300 million project aims to enhance foundational literacy and numeracy (FLN) for all children and strengthen education system performance from pre-primary to grade 5. It will improve FLN and teaching practices through a national rollout of the FLN package with teaching materials, teacher professional development, structured pedagogy, and mentoring. To reduce inequities, it will support targeted learning acceleration programs, phased expansion of quality pre-primary education in disadvantaged districts, and improved school infrastructure, especially WASH facilities. The project will also strengthen education system performance by enhancing teacher and school leadership, improving accountability and budget execution, and aligning resources with PEDP5 priorities. Technical assistance will reinforce data systems (IPEMIS), institutionalize the National Student Assessment, pilot digital innovations such as AI-assisted teaching, and generate evidence through evaluations to inform future policy and scale-up.

P512709.project_abstract: The proposed operation is the first phase under the Montenegro Water Security and Climate Adaptation Multiphase Programmatic Approach (MPA), a multi-phase engagement designed to support the long-term transformation of the water sector in Montenegro. The total estimated cost of the program is approximately USD 200 million, with a proposed IBRD financing envelope of approximately USD 100 million, complemented by additional contributions from other development partners including the OPEC Fund.The Phase 1 project, spanning four years from 2026 to 2030, will focus on strategic investments in water services infrastructure, priority actions to improve coastal water security, and measures to strengthen water sector governance. Phase 2, covering 2030 to 2033, will scale up investments in the modernization and expansion of water services infrastructure, implement integrated catchment-based restoration measures to secure coastal water security, and mobilize additional financing from other development partners to support these efforts. The third phase, from 2033 to 2036, will consolidate the scaled-up activities, ensuring the sustainability and impact of investments made in the earlier phases. During this phase, the program will continue to mobilize additional financing to reinforce long-term improvements in water services and sector resilience.The Phase 1 project aims to enhance water services and climate resilience in Montenegro by financing strategic investments in water services infrastructure, advancing integrated approaches to coastal water security, and strengthening the enabling environment for effective sector governance and management. These activities will bolster Montenegro’s economic and environmental sustainability while supporting EU accession efforts and alignment with EU water directives. The operation is organized into four components.Component 1: Strategic Investments in Water Services Infrastructure and ResilienceThis component aims to rehabilitate, modernize and expand water services infrastructure, thereby increasing access, efficiency, safety, and resilience of services for households and businesses. The activities focus on the improvement of the reliability of public water services through targeted investments in:- Rehabilitation of dated centralized water supply systems to reduce water losses, mitigate health risks, and enhance the technical and financial sustainability of water utilities;- Expansion of centralized wastewater treatment services to reduce water pollution and improve environmental health; and - Modernization and expansion of decentralized water services infrastructure to better serve rural populations in remote areas.Component 2: Integrated Approaches for Coastal Water SecurityThis component addresses pressing challenges in securing the coastal water supply managed by the Regional Water Supply Company for the Montenegrin Coast. It includes:- Enhancement of Lake Water Treatment Capacity: Increasing the treatment capacity by 250 liters per second to address water supply shortfalls during peak summer seasons, supporting local needs and tourism growth.- Hydromorphological Regime Enhancement of the Morača River: Comprehensive assessment and modeling to improve the sediment regime, connection with the adjacent groundwater bodies, mitigate flooding risks, and ensure sustainable long-term availability of water resources for coastal water supply.Component 3: Strengthening the Enabling Environment for Effective Water Sector Governance and ManagementThis component aims to enhance the capacities of national and subnational entities and water utility operators for effective management, planning, regulation, and performance improvement. Key activities include:- Development of Institutional, Legal, and Policy Frameworks: Supporting legislative amendments and the development of a national water strategy.- Improvement of Economic Regulation System: Strengthening the Regulator (REGAGEN) through IT system development and tariff methodology implementation.- Capacity Development for Water Utilities: Expanding capacity building programs on priority topics like Asset Management and Energy Efficiency.- Support for Rural Water Services Delivery: Analyzing and improving management structures and service delivery for rural systems.Component 4: Project ManagementThis component will support effective project management and coordination to ensure timely and efficient implementation of all project activities.

P512715.project_abstract: This project aims to improve flood resilience in selected areas of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa through structural and non-structural measures and enhanced institutional capacity for disaster risk management and emergency response. Component 1 - Improving Infrastructure and Planning for Resilience: The component implemented by the provincial Irrigation Department will include construction and rehabilitation of flood protection infrastructure, such as embankments, spurs, revetments, retaining walls, flood carrier channels, gabion works, and check dams, complemented by nature-based solutions such as slope stabilization using afforestation/reforestation, terracing, and riverbank restoration. An infrastructure investment plan will also be developed, containing a framework of phased short-, medium-, and long-term investments and informing related future feasibility studies and design work. Component 2 - Establishing Flood Early Warning System: The project will also establish and operationalize a province-wide integrated Flood Early Warning System through collaboration between the Provincial Disaster Management Authority (PDMA) and Irrigation Department, by linking hydrometeorological observations, forecasting and modeling, decision support systems, and standardized alert dissemination to enable timely and coordinated preparedness and response to climate-exacerbated flood events. Component 3 - Strengthening Institutional and Community Preparedness: This project will enhance the capacity of provincial institutions and local communities in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa to prepare for and respond to climate-induced multi-hazard disasters, with a particular focus on floods. PDMA and Rescue 1122 will be strengthened through upgraded facilities, improved risk analysis and planning, equipment, specialized training, communication and dispatch systems, and expanded rescue stations. Community-level resilience will be facilitated through improved early-warning dissemination and community-based disaster risk management.
P512715.regionname: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan, and Pakistan

P512975.project_abstract: RISE-RAP supports Eastern and Southern African countries in planning and delivery through informing programs and supporting regional alignment. Countries leverage RAP-curated regional knowledge systems, coordination platforms, and TA to inform irrigation policies, investments and services. Rather than develop approaches in isolation, countries can develop shared regional resources including analytics, tools, and lessons learned and adapt them to their specific contexts, fostering efficiency and consistency while preserving country ownership of decisions. In support of achieving the overall Africa RISE MPA PrDO, the Project will support countries across Africa RISE components through facilitating country-facing acceleration activities on learning and knowledge, regional alignment and cooperation, and implementation and innovation. It supports innovation in support to Farmer-Led Irrigation Development, Performance in public service delivery and water security, and addresses the constraints of fragmented, single-project interventions by enabling alignment, shared delivery models, capacity enhancement and stronger partnerships across countries.

P513113.project_abstract: The water supply and sanitation services in The Gambia are currently facing a significant crisis due to ongoing challenges in achieving the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). According to the 2023 Joint Monitoring Programme (JMP) report, only 48% of the population has access to safely managed water services, with 68% access in urban areas compared to just 12% in rural areas. Access to basic sanitation services is also a major concern, as only 28% of the population has access to safely managed sanitation, while 15% still practice open defecation, particularly in rural regions. Additionally, the country lacks adequate wastewater treatment, with about 80% of wastewater improperly managed, leading to groundwater contamination and posing significant public health risks. Three key localities in the Greater Banjul Area (GBA)—Banjul, Kanifing, and Brikama—are home to approximately 63% of the country's population. The National Water and Electricity Company (NAWEC) currently produces 139,000 cubic meters of water daily, which is insufficient to meet the demand. The GBA mainly relies on boreholes that draw water from a sub-shallow aquifer; however, this water source is vulnerable to contamination from untreated sewage and saltwater intrusion caused by rising sea levels related to climate change. The Gambia Water and Sanitation Project (WASAP) aims to support the government's efforts to address the crisis in water and sanitation services in the Greater Banjul Area (GBA) region. This operation includes a series of activities organized into four mutually reinforcing components, which are summarized below: Component 1: Improving Access to Urban Drinking Water Services (US$14 million)             Component 2: Improving Access to Sanitation Services ($18.0 million)Component 2.1: Urban Sanitation (16.5 million)   • Emergency wastewater management ($5.0 million)   • Expansion of Kotu Wastewater System (US$3.2 million)   • Construction of a Fecal Sludge Treatment Plant ($8.3 million)Component 2.2: Rural Sanitation Development ($1.5 million)   • Construction of Safely Managed Latrines in Targeted Rural Areas ($0.9 million)   • Development of a National Sanitation Master Plan (US$0.6 M)Component 3. Institutional Reform and Capacity Building for Sustainable Water Services ($6.0 million)   • Support to sector reform and NAWEC operational and financial efficacy improvement ($3.0 million)   • Supporting transboundary cooperation on the Senegal-Mauritanian Aquifer Basin ($0.5 million)   • Enhancing water resources monitoring and conservation ($2.5 million)Component 4. Project management: ($2 million)   Component 5. Contingent Emergency Response Component (CERC)   • Component 5 	CERC 	$0 millionTOTAL: 	$30 million

P513248.project_abstract: The project will support three priority investment areas: i) tuna industry domestication, ii) small- and medium-size fisheries and aquaculture value chain development, and iii) fisheries governance and institutions' strengthening. Project themes and activities will evolve around: - Development of enabling infrastructure for efficient dockside inspections, offloading, and transshipment in Luganville, Espiritu Santo.- Work force professionalization through vocational training for fishers and fish industry workers. - Technology incubation to foster hands-on training, innovation support and enterprise development in seafood products development. - Food security through value chain development of small and medium scale fisheries, in particular through investments in cold chain systems and fish markets. - Strengthening of local fisher groups in Santo, to partake in sector management and development, including development of satellite distribution systems and inter-island fish trading. -  Aquaculture development through upgrading hatcheries in Luganville and providing seed supply for development small-scale and semi-commercial commercial aquaculture in response to high protein demand.- Institutional strengthening including construction of VFD headquarter offices. - Monitoring, Control, Surveillance and Enforcement, including improved regional cooperation. The project will be implemented by the Vanuatu Fisheries Department under the Ministry of Fisheries, Oceans, and Maritime Affairs (MFOMA).

P513301.project_abstract: The Project aims to increase access to drinking water supply and sanitation (WSS) services in underserved areas in the city of Kinshasa, the capital city of the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC). The project intends to leverage financing from IDA, as well as mobilize private capital for water supply production. The aim is to provide safely managed drinking water services to previously unserved populations and to improve the quality of services from basic to safely managed drinking water services in Kinshasa through safe and reliable water access through household connections. Moreover, the project will improve access to safely-managed sanitation through the construction of fecal sludge treatment plants (FSTP), and increasing access to water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH) facilities in selected schools and health centers within the city. Aligned with the regional Multi-phase Programmatic Approach (MPA) design, the proposed components are the following: Component 1: Strengthening Governance and Reforms and Improving the Performance of Service Providers. Subcomponent 1.1. on Governance and Reforms (mapped to pillars 1 and 2 of the MPA) finances technical assistance, training and equipment to address governance bottlenecks and support the implementation of reform measures including for the mobilization of private capital for WASH. This includes the development of regulatory instruments and policies, tariff studies, transaction advisory services, capacity development for sector ministries, and the national water and sanitation regulatory entity. Subcomponent 1.2. on Service Providers Performance Improvement, which is mapped to pillar 3 of the MPA, finances works, equipment, technical assistance, training for the national water utility REGIDESO. This covers support for the implementation of REGIDESO's Performance Improvement Action Plan, including Non-Revenue Water, digitalization, and water quality management improvements and social connection subsidies. Component 2: Leveraging Financing for Resilient Investments to Expand WASH Access (mapped to pillar 4 of the MPA). Subcomponent 2.1 finances infrastructure investments in climate -resilient urban water production and distribution systems for Kinshasa, including viability gap financing for the construction of new bulk water treatment plant(s). A Build-Operate-Transfer (BOT) arrangement is proposed for these water production plant(s), blending public and private funding to ensure an affordable tariff for REGIDESO as the off-taker, and to the households as end-consumers. IDA financing would support the viability gap financing, as well as the completion of the rehabilitation and expansion of the existing Ndjili water treatment plant, and expansion of REGIDESO’s water distribution network to maximize impact on the population. MIGA de-risking instruments will be brought as appropriate. The IFC role would be to provide debt and mobilize other lenders (either through co-development with value-for-money assurances or through a competitive process). Subcomponent 2.2 finances construction of one or more fecal sludge treatment plants (FSTPs) in Kinshasa, as well as technical assistance and support to strengthen the liquid sanitation collection, conveyance, treatment and reuse chain. This component will also provide support for climate-resilient WASH facilities in schools and health centers. Component 3: Project Management and Coordination. This component finances the overall project management, monitoring and coordination costs, including operating costs. It also finances costs related to land acquisition and resettlement related to the project. The total project cost estimation is US$500 million, of which US$200 million to be financed by IDA, US$200 million by other development partners and US$100 million in private capital mobilization.

P513341.project_abstract: The REPAIR Program aims to strengthen the financial and operational preparedness of participating countries to respond quickly to climate and other shocks. It sets up a Regional Climate Risk Fund to provide countries with access to different financial instruments to respond to shocks of various frequency and severity. And it provides technical assistance to participating countries to ensure that the funds from the financial instruments flow quickly and transparently to respond to disasters through country delivery channels. It is managed by one regional implementing entity African Risk Capacity Limited (ARC Ltd). Comoros, Madagascar and Mozambique are in the Program in Phase 1. Phase 2 countries include Angola, Burundi, Malawi, Seychelles and Zambia.  The phase 3 operation will be for DRC. The African Risk Capacity (ARC) Group is comprised of ARC Agency, a Specialised Agency of the African Union founded in 2012; and ARC Insurance Company Limited (ARC Ltd), a hybrid mutual insurer and the commercial affiliate of the Group founded in 2014. ARC Agency was established to help African governments improve their capacities to better plan, prepare, and respond to natural disasters triggered by extreme weather events, as well as outbreaks and epidemics. ARC Ltd offers complementary risk pooling and risk transfer services. The RCRF will be managed by ARC Ltd, which will ensure strong governance, and build the capacity of Ministries of Finance on managing their contingent liabilities related to climate disasters.Under Component 1, the MPA will establish a Regional Climate Risk Fund (RCRF) to provide affordable, pre-arranged financing for post-disaster responses to participating countries ahead of climate and other shocks. Activities under this component will be oriented to setting up and capitalizing the RCRF in the different financial layers, with ARC Limited as its regional manager . This component will be funded by IDA/IBRD and make up the largest portion of World Bank funding for the project.Component 2 is funded by the regional IDA grant that was approved in phase 2 of the program in July 2025. Technical assistance will support the operationalization of the RCRF and the operational preparedness of country systems to facilitate swift flows of funds upon a shock. This includes start-up and operating cost of the RCRF including staffing; preparation and testing of operational process and procedures to facilitate the fund flows between MoFs, country systems and end beneficiaries; consultancies to help MoFs decide the allocation between the instruments based on their risk appetite, and improve the management of disaster-related contingent liabilities; and capacity building of country officials on DRF, of country systems on contingency planning and of women and WSMEs on financial literacy.

P513349.project_abstract: The Cubango-Okavango river basin (CORB) is a hydrologically complex, and deeply interconnected system which highlights the importance of transboundary solutions. Community livelihoods, ranging from artisanal fisheries to small-scale agriculture in all three riparian states (Angola, Namibia and Botswana) depend on the CORB’s floodplains, rivers, wetlands and other natural resources. These resources are also vital for larger industry, including tourism. Water resources in the CORB are facing competing demands that jeopardize the sustainability of livelihoods and ecosystems. In addition to the threats posed by poverty, rapid rural population growth, and climate change, the CORB’s water resources face demands from urbanization trends; resource-based livelihoods; expanding tourism; commercial agriculture; and water extraction. Addressing these challenges require innovations in transboundary water financing to achieve economies of scale and scope for transboundary basin financing needs that cannot otherwise be accomplished at a national level. The objective of the Project is to support the capitalization of the CORB Fund towards sustainable development in the Cubango-Okavango river basin. The operationalization of the CORB Fund will be instrumental to enable a regional funding mechanism for cross-border financing needs with strong governance and decision-making capabilities, and with capacity to raise and channel financing from a diverse set of stakeholders. Project support includes the preparation of viable project concepts on the CORB Fund pillars, as well as strengthening the coordination, management, and communication between all the stakeholders involved in the basin, thus enhancing strategies to raise additional funding.Component 1 focuses on the CORB Fund’s capitalization. This component will support the consolidation and expansion of the CORB Fund as an innovative financial mechanism, and advance collaboration towards its long-term sustainability.  It includes (1.1.) the establishment and implementation of the CORB Fund’s governance, operational tools, and systems, (1.2.) the implementation of stakeholder coordination and engagement for long-term sustainability of the CORB Fund, (1.3.) support to the CORB Fund's communication and knowledge management, and (1.4.) CORB endowment fund capitalization. Component 2 focuses on developing the CORB Fund’s pipeline for potential investment. This component will focus on the development of the CORB Fund’s pipeline of investment to address the priority areas identified by Angola, Botswana and Namibia in the CORB, laying the foundation for larger/more complex investments in the future. The component’s activities will focus on two complementary areas of support: (2.1) preparing the CORB Fund Financing Roadmap as well as project concepts at pre-feasibility and feasibility level for potential investment; and (2.2) enhancing capacity on economics of biodiversity to inform investment. The development of the pipeline for potential future investment will be conducted with close engagement with the Ministers of Finance of Riparian countries to ensure high level buy-in and engagement participation in the preparation of the Fund’s long-term capitalization and development into the role as a regional financial institution. Component 3 focuses on project management, monitoring and evaluation (M&E).

P513389.project_abstract: The proposed project aims to foster sustainable tourism in selected destination supporting the creation of local jobs, prioritizing environmental stewardship, and strengthening community resilience. By developing unique tourism offerings that respect natural assets and empower local communities, the initiative seeks to drive inclusive economic growth, thereby creating conditions for sustained private sector participation.

P513708.project_abstract: Ghana has a road network of about 94,203 km, of which 27 percent paved and 73% unpaved. More than 50 percent of the network, including many feeder and secondary roads, is in fair to impassable condition. Road density and coverage are unevenly distributed: the north of the country remains poorly served, while the southern half has greater road density supporting agricultural and livestock production, but often at a poor service level that increases travel distances and costs to markets and limits opportunities. In fact, the population in the north is better connected to neighbor’s countries and has some comparative productivity agricultural advantages. To address these challenges, the Government of Ghana recently launched the Big Push Initiative 2025. The Program is aimed at reducing the investment gap in key sectors and revitalizing aging, poorly maintained infrastructure. Among its priorities are feeder roads, which play a crucial role in linking rural areas to main transport corridors. Investing in feeder roads is essential to connect farms and rural communities with markets, reduce transport costs and post-harvest losses, and improve access to services. Strengthening these networks will boost agricultural productivity, competitiveness, and inclusive growth, while better connecting isolated regions of the country. The proposed operation will support (i) the rehabilitation of selected feeder roads linked to key agricultural value chains, (ii) the maintenance of the rehabilitated roads and road fund reform, and (iii) project management and OHS enhancement. The proposed operation will be structured around 3 components: Component 1: Feeder road rehabilitation Component 2: Road maintenance and support to road fund reform Component 3: Project managementComponent 4: CERC

P513815.project_abstract: Cloudbursts, flash floods and landslide incidents triggered by heavy rain during the 2025 monsoon season (June to date) have caused extensive impacts in Himachal Pradesh, resulting in about 486 deaths and damages/losses estimated at US$653 million. Damages are primarily driven by impacts to the transport infrastructure, public buildings, power infrastructure, and water, sanitation and irrigation, while the livelihood sector additionally faces significant indirect losses due to disrupted access to markets, significant reduction of tourist arrivals etc. These events follow similarly devastating monsoon seasons in 2023 and 2024. These repeated extreme weather events have severely strained the state’s already constrained capacities, and substantial recovery needs from 2023 and 2024 remain unmet emphasizing the urgency of a speedy and resilient reconstruction and recovery effort. The proposed project will support the Government of Himachal Pradesh in advancing a resilient recovery process (transport corridors, public buildings, water/sanitation/irrigation schemes, and energy supply) while strengthening institutional capacities for disaster risk management, enhancing sectoral resilience for infrastructure sectors and supporting resilient livelihoods.

P514199.project_abstract: The project will be the first operation of an MPA, conceived to allow scalability of World Bank support for local development in rural northern Mozambique. The program design envisages two simultaneous phases implemented over an eight-year period. The phases will address three key development needs of rural northern Mozambique communities that are impacted by fragility: social cohesion, access to economic opportunities, and access to resilient infrastructure. These will be achieved by revitalizing forums for dialogue between the northern provinces, within communities, and between communities and the state, supporting a cash transfer program for vulnerable households, supporting youth upskilling and SME development, providing GBV and psycho-social support services, building systems for community participation in local development, enhancing the capacity of local government for better planning and efficient service delivery, and investing in priority community and public infrastructure. Both phases will be implemented in the three northern provinces of Cabo Delgado, Nampula, and Niassa. This US$100 million IDA Phase I operation will develop and remobilize foundational regional, province, and district-level dialogue forums, while local economic development activities and community infrastructure and basic service planning activities and investments will concentrate in selected 80 localities across the provinces’ 56 districts and public infrastructure will be concentrated in 10 districts of Cabo Delgado province. A US$150 million IDA Phase II operation will deepen local development systems and capacities and make sure they are sustainable while developing community and public infrastructure and services in additional localities according to readiness criteria.

P514272.project_abstract: The proposed STARR Project aims to strengthen agricultural and livestock systems, revitalize seed security, and promote private sector–led value chain development by delivering improved seeds, fertilizers, animal feed, and veterinary services; rehabilitating rural infrastructure; and expanding access to finance and markets for SMEs and farmer cooperatives. Together, these interventions would build a stronger agricultural sector that enhances food security, creates jobs, and supports more resilient livelihoods.The project will comprise three components: Component 1: Revitalizing Agricultural and Livestock SystemsThe project will boost productivity of key crops and livestock through improved inputs, extension services, and veterinary support. It will rehabilitate community-based infrastructure, focusing on irrigation systems, rainwater harvesting structures, and livestock watering points. Solar-powered irrigation pumps will enhance water availability, especially in drought-prone areas. Additional efforts include repairing canals, restoring storage facilities to reduce post-harvest losses, and improving rural market access via feeder roads and bridges.Component 2: Strengthening Seed SystemsThis component aims to ensure long-term access to quality seeds by restoring and expanding community seed banks and supporting local cooperatives with training on seed management. It will revive research and development for improved seed production and build capacity for seed certification aligned with global standards. Investments in local seed enterprises will increase the availability of climate-resilient varieties, reducing reliance on external sources and improving recovery readiness.Component 3: Enhancing Value Chains and Private Sector EngagementThe project will strengthen agricultural and livestock value chains to address food insecurity and promote recovery. It will mobilize private investment and empower SMEs, cooperatives, and farmer associations to manage input supply, processing, and logistics. Activities will include upgrading irrigation systems, transport, storage, and post-harvest processing, financed through matching grants and supported by technical assistance. This approach will stabilize food supply, lower prices, and create jobs for vulnerable groups, especially youth and women.Digital Market Platform linking farmers, SMEs and marketsThe project will expand and operationalize the Mahsul digital platform to improve market access and value chain integration for farmers, cooperatives, and agri-SMEs. Mahsul, launched in 2024 under the ongoing Sudan Thabat Project, currently operates in three states (Northern, River Nile and Kassala) and connects farmers to markets through transparent B2B transactions. By reducing intermediaries and promoting price transparency, the platform will stabilize prices and enable vulnerable communities to access affordable food commodities through organized selling points.  Geographical TargetingTarget localities, communities, and agricultural cooperatives will be identified using a formula established under the ongoing Sudan Thabat Project, based on clearly defined criteria including security conditions, agricultural and livestock potential, access to water resources, availability of private sector services (e.g., transport, agro-processing), and complementarity with other development partner interventions. This project is designed as a national project to ensure geographical targeting based on agriculture potential and the situation on the ground. Given the improved security situation and high agricultural potential in Gezira and Sennar states, the project may prioritize these two states for initial targeting. Should conflict escalate in any of the proposed project target areas, project resources may be reallocated to alternative localities or states, in accordance with procedures and criteria defined in the Project Implementation Manual (PIM), and in consultation with the World Bank.

P514340.project_abstract: Support to the potable water sector in Tunisia.
P514340.regionname: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan, and Pakistan

P514502.project_abstract: Since 2017, the World Bank has supported Yemen’s health sector through large-scale emergency operations that have delivered essential health, nutrition, and water, sanitation and hygiene (WASH) services to millions, while gradually investing in local institutions and systems. These efforts have sustained human capital in a protracted crisis, reaching nearly 24 million beneficiaries and training over 18,000 health personnel. Despite achievements, challenges in Yemen persist—including low immunization rates, malnutrition, conflict-related access constraints, declining donor funding, and reliance on external third-party implementation. The upcoming operation will prioritize integration, strengthen local capacity, and revamp health workforce development by aligning community cadres with national strategies, improving training systems, and creating job opportunities for health workers.  The project will consist of 5 components: Component 1: Delivery of integrated health, nutrition and WASH servicesComponent 1 will prioritize delivering an integrated minimum service package (MSP) across all levels of care—primary, secondary, and tertiary. Investments will be made to improve water and sanitation infrastructure at primary health centers. A Geographic Accelerator sub-component will tailor area-based interventions to local needs, combining MSP delivery with nutrition, WASH, and social protection interventions.Component 2: Support to prevent and respond to communicable disease outbreaksComponent 2 will retain emergency response capacity through disease surveillance, rapid response teams, laboratory support. It will also deliver emergency water trucking and to hospitals and support water quality testing. Component 3: Systems building for sustainable delivery of health and nutrition servicesComponent 3 focuses on building sustainable systems for health and nutrition service delivery by strengthening sub-national institutions and the health workforce. It includes tailored training for ministries and local health offices in planning, monitoring, and financial management, alongside support for refining national health strategies. A dedicated subcomponent will address critical health workforce reforms, aiming to resolve systemic bottlenecks and establish more sustainable funding mechanisms for health worker salaries. These reforms will also contribute to job creation and retention in the health sector, helping transition from volunteer-based service delivery to a more stable and professionalized workforce.Component 4: Project managementComponent 4 will consist of supervision costs, monitoring and evaluation, third party monitoring and financing of E&S compliance requirements, fiduciary aspects and procurement. Component 5: Contingent emergency response category (CERC)A zero cost CERC component will support the operation in the case of emergencies.
P514502.regionname: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan, and Pakistan

P515014.project_abstract: The first phase of Clean Philippines Multiphase Programmatic Approach will cover Metro Manila, the National Capital Region (NCR), including all of its 17 LGUs.  The project consists of four components: Component 1. Strengthening policy and institutions for improved solid waste management, Component 2. Improving local service delivery for better solid waste management, Component 3. Improving waste infrastructure and plastic waste management for a circular economy, and Component 4. Innovation, learning, and public awareness for behavioral change.  This first phase will be led by Metro Manila Development Authority (MMDA) and will be supported by the Department of Environment and Natural Resources (DENR) and the Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG). This phase addresses one of the country’s SWM hotspots, in an area where LGU capacity is among the highest nationwide. The project will improve solid waste management and reduce plastic pollution in the National Capital Region of the Philippines.

P515255.project_abstract: The proposed MERIT project helps to operationalize the vision and strategies laid out in the 2022 Mekong Delta Regional Master Plan and address current and future climate change impacts. The project would (i) enhance the information and management tools to support adaptive delta management; (ii) build new infrastructure and repurpose and upgrade existing regional infrastructure to enhance climate resilience and promote more sustainable and climate-smart management of water and land resources; and (iii) facilitate climate-smart livelihoods transition and diversification, value chains development, dynamic agro-entrepreneurship, and an accelerated adoption of sustainable agricultural practices
